# 📘 MODULE 4 — STATIONARITY: STRICT SENSE STATIONARY (SSS) PROCESSES

## 1.1 Preamble: What is a Stochastic Process?

Before diving into stationarity, we must anchor our understanding of a **stochastic process**. A stochastic process is a family of random variables $\{X(t),\, t \in T\}$ indexed by a parameter $t$ (usually time) drawn from an index set $T$. For each fixed $t = t_0$, $X(t_0)$ is a random variable. A specific outcome (a *sample path* or *realisation*) is a deterministic function of $t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stochastic Process in Plain Words**
> Imagine a voltage $V(t)$ across a noisy resistor. We don't know its *exact* future value, but we can describe it **statistically**. The collection of all such random voltages at every time instant $t$ forms a stochastic process — a "random function of time."

The probability structure of a stochastic process is fully described by the **family of finite-dimensional distributions**:

$$F_{X}(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n;\, t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n) = P\big(X(t_1) \le x_1, X(t_2) \le x_2, \dots, X(t_n) \le x_n\big)$$

for every $n$ and every choice of time instants. These joint CDFs encode *all* the probabilistic information.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Strict Sense Stationarity (SSS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)**
> A stochastic process $\{X(t),\, t \in T\}$ is called **Strict Sense Stationary (SSS)** — or simply **strictly stationary** — if, for **every** integer $n \ge 1$, for **any** choice of time instants $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n \in T$, and for **any** time shift $\tau$ such that $t_i + \tau \in T$, the joint cumulative distribution function satisfies:
>
> $$F_X(x_1, \dots, x_n;\, t_1, \dots, t_n) \;=\; F_X(x_1, \dots, x_n;\, t_1+\tau, \dots, t_n+\tau)$$

Equivalently, the **joint probability law** of the random vector $\big(X(t_1), X(t_2), \dots, X(t_n)\big)$ is **identical** to that of $\big(X(t_1+\tau), X(t_2+\tau), \dots, X(t_n+\tau)\big)$ for every $n$ and every $\tau$.

In compact notation: **the finite-dimensional distributions are invariant under a shift of the time origin.**

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Statistical Steady State"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Long-Running Electrical Machine**
> Think of a properly running **AC generator** that has reached its operating temperature. The statistical properties of its output voltage — the *average level*, the *fluctuation spread*, the *typical auto-correlation structure* — do not depend on **when** you observe them. Whether you look at the voltage at 2 PM or at 5 PM, the *probabilistic description* is the same. This is statistical *steady-state behaviour* — the essence of stationarity.
>
> **Contrast (Non-Stationary Case):** A motor that is **warming up** has a mean temperature that *drifts upward* with time. Its statistical character changes with the time origin — clearly **not stationary**.

Mathematically, SSS captures this idea: the *probability laws* do not change when we **shift** the time axis.

---

## 1.4 Direct Consequences of Strict Sense Stationarity

Setting $n = 1$ in the SSS definition gives us the first key takeaway:

$$F_X(x;\, t) \;=\; F_X(x;\, t+\tau) \quad \forall\, \tau$$

> **The first-order (marginal) distribution is independent of $t$** $\Rightarrow$ the process is **identically distributed at every instant**.

Now taking $n = 2$ and applying the definition:

$$F_X(x_1, x_2;\, t_1, t_2) \;=\; F_X(x_1, x_2;\, t_1+\tau,\, t_2+\tau)$$

This means the joint distribution depends on the time instants **only through the difference** $t_2 - t_1$.

Hence the **autocorrelation function** $R_X(t_1, t_2) = E[X(t_1)X(t_2)]$ and the **autocovariance function** $C_X(t_1, t_2) = E[(X(t_1) - \mu)(X(t_2) - \mu)]$ are functions of a **single variable** $\tau = t_2 - t_1$ (or $|t_2 - t_1|$ for a real process).

---

## 1.5 First-Order Stationarity vs. Second-Order Stationarity vs. n-th Order Stationarity

The SSS condition is the **strongest** form because it requires the invariance of **all** finite-dimensional distributions. Sometimes we relax this to:

| **Order of Stationarity** | **Requirement** |
|---|---|
| **1st-order (or marginal) stationarity** | $F_X(x; t)$ independent of $t$ for all $x$ |
| **2nd-order stationarity** | $F_X(x_1, x_2; t_1, t_2)$ depends only on $t_2 - t_1$ |
| **$n$-th order stationarity** | All joint distributions up to order $n$ are shift-invariant |
| **Strict Sense Stationary (SSS)** | All $n$-th order distributions are shift-invariant $\forall\, n$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Implication**
> $\text{SSS} \;\Rightarrow\; n\text{-th order stationary for every } n \;\Rightarrow\; \text{2nd-order stationary} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{1st-order stationary}$
>
> The reverse arrows **do not hold** in general.

---

## 1.6 Physical Constants & Standard Engineering Metrics

In any SSS process, the following statistical descriptors are **constants** (time-independent):

- **Mean (DC component):** $E[X(t)] = \mu$ — a **constant** independent of $t$
- **Mean-square value:** $E[X^2(t)] = \psi^2$ — a constant
- **Variance (AC power):** $\sigma^2 = E[X^2(t)] - \mu^2$ — a constant
- **Autocorrelation function:** $R_X(\tau) = E[X(t)X(t+\tau)]$ — a function of the **lag** $\tau$ alone
- **Autocovariance function:** $C_X(\tau) = E[(X(t) - \mu)(X(t+\tau) - \mu)]$ — a function of $\tau$ alone
- **Total average power:** $R_X(0) = E[X^2(t)] = \psi^2$ — a constant

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SSS Matters in Electrical Engineering**
> In communication theory, signals are modelled as SSS processes over the long observation window. The **autocorrelation function** $R_X(\tau)$ — which for an SSS process depends only on $\tau$ — is the cornerstone of **power spectral density** analysis via the Wiener–Khinchin theorem. Without stationarity, spectral analysis loses its meaning.

# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS: PROPERTIES OF STRICTLY STATIONARY PROCESSES

## 2.1 Logical Decomposition of the SSS Conditions

Let us unpack the SSS definition **step by step** so that every implication is transparent.

> **Step 1 — Marginal Distribution Invariance (1st-order)**
> Take $n = 1$. Then $F_X(x; t) = F_X(x; t + \tau)$ for all $t$ and $\tau$. So $F_X$ cannot depend on $t$.
> **Implication:** The process is identically distributed at every instant. Hence:
> - The **mean** $\mu_X = E[X(t)]$ is **constant** (does not depend on $t$).
> - The **second moment** $E[X^2(t)]$ is constant.
> - The **variance** $\sigma_X^2 = \text{Var}[X(t)]$ is **constant** and finite.

> **Step 2 — Second-Order Joint Distribution Invariance (2nd-order)**
> Take $n = 2$. Then $F_X(x_1, x_2; t_1, t_2) = F_X(x_1, x_2; t_1 + \tau, t_2 + \tau)$ for all $t_1, t_2, \tau$.
> **Implication:** The joint CDF depends on the two time instants only through their **difference** $t_2 - t_1$. Therefore:
> - The **autocorrelation function** $R_X(t_1, t_2) = E[X(t_1)X(t_2)]$ depends only on the lag $\tau = t_2 - t_1$, i.e. $R_X(t_1, t_2) = R_X(\tau)$.
> - The **autocovariance function** $C_X(t_1, t_2) = R_X(\tau) - \mu_X^2$ also depends only on $\tau$.

> **Step 3 — Higher-Order Invariance (n-th order)**
> For every $n$, the $n$-dimensional joint distribution depends only on the **set of time differences** $\{|t_i - t_j|\}$. This is the full force of the SSS condition.

> **Why Does It Matter?**
> If only the **mean** is constant but the *joint distribution* is not shift-invariant, the process is **not SSS**. For example, the process $X(t) = t + Y$, where $Y$ is a fixed random variable, has constant mean if $E[Y] = 0$ — but $X(t)$ is clearly growing with $t$, so it is **not stationary**.

---

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula you must master for the KTU 2024 Scheme exam on this topic.

| **Statistical Quantity** | **SSS Condition (Form)** | **Function of** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean $\mu_X$ | $E[X(t)] = \mu_X$ | **Constant** | DC / average value of the waveform |
| Mean-square $\psi_X^2$ | $E[X^2(t)] = \psi_X^2$ | **Constant** | Total average power |
| Variance $\sigma_X^2$ | $E[(X(t) - \mu_X)^2] = \sigma_X^2$ | **Constant** | AC / fluctuating power |
| Autocorrelation $R_X(t_1, t_2)$ | $E[X(t_1)X(t_2)] = R_X(\tau)$ | **Lag $\tau = t_2 - t_1$ only** | Measures "memory" of the process |
| Autocovariance $C_X(\tau)$ | $R_X(\tau) - \mu_X^2$ | Lag $\tau$ only | Lag-dependent dispersion |
| $n$-th joint moment $m_n(t_1,\dots,t_n)$ | $E[X(t_1)\cdots X(t_n)]$ | Time differences only | Higher-order statistical structure |
| SSS Condition (CDF form) | $F_X(\mathbf{x}; \mathbf{t}) = F_X(\mathbf{x}; \mathbf{t}+\tau)$ | Shift-invariant for all $n,\tau$ | Strongest form of stationarity |

### 2.2.1 Auxiliary Properties of the Autocorrelation Function $R_X(\tau)$ for Real SSS Processes

> [!NOTE]
> These properties follow **purely** from the definition of $R_X(\tau) = E[X(t)X(t+\tau)]$ and the SSS condition. They are **board-exam favourites**.

1. **Even symmetry:** $R_X(\tau) = R_X(-\tau)$.
2. **Maximum at zero:** $R_X(0) \ge \vert R_X(\tau) \vert$ for all $\tau$.
3. **Value at zero equals mean-square:** $R_X(0) = E[X^2(t)] = \psi_X^2$.
4. **Lag-$\infty$ limit equals square of the mean:** $\lim_{\vert\tau\vert \to \infty} R_X(\tau) = \mu_X^2$ (for processes with no periodic components).
5. **Non-negativity of autocorrelation matrix** $\Rightarrow$ $R_X(0) \ge 0$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of the SSS Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where Strict Sense Stationarity Appears in Electrical Engineering Practice**

- **Communication Systems:** The thermal noise at the input of a receiver is modelled as a **Gaussian SSS process**. The matched filter and the Wiener filter both rely on the time-invariance of $R_X(\tau)$.
- **Power Systems Analysis:** Load demands, when observed over a long period, are often modelled as stationary stochastic processes for the purpose of forecasting and reliability studies.
- **Radar and Sonar Signal Processing:** Clutter returns are modelled as SSS processes with known autocorrelation functions, allowing the design of optimal detectors.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** The electroencephalogram (EEG) of a relaxed subject exhibits segments of approximate SSS behaviour — crucial for spectral analysis of brain rhythms.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Component failure times are studied as SSS renewal processes for hazard-rate estimation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note**
> Truly *strict* stationarity is a **mathematical idealisation**. In practice, we work with processes that are *approximately* SSS over a finite observation window — this is the *quasi-stationary* or *locally stationary* assumption.

# 🧮 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL RESULTS

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Constant Mean of an SSS Process

> **Statement:** If $\{X(t)\}$ is SSS, then $E[X(t)] = \mu$ is independent of $t$.

**Proof:**

For an SSS process, the **first-order distribution** is shift-invariant (taking $n = 1$ in the SSS definition):

$$F_X(x; t) = F_X(x; t + \tau) \quad \forall\, t, \tau, x$$

This means $F_X(x; t)$ does not depend on $t$ at all. Denote it as $F_X(x)$. Now compute the mean:

$$E[X(t)] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, dF_X(x; t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, dF_X(x)$$

The right-hand side is **independent of $t$**. Hence $E[X(t)] = \mu_X$ is a constant for all $t \in T$.

$\blacksquare$

> **Interpretation:** The DC level of an SSS process never drifts — there is no "trend" component.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Autocorrelation Function Depends Only on the Lag $\tau$

> **Statement:** For an SSS process $\{X(t)\}$, the autocorrelation $R_X(t_1, t_2) = E[X(t_1)X(t_2)]$ depends only on $\tau = t_2 - t_1$.

**Proof:**

For $n = 2$, the SSS condition gives:

$$F_X(x_1, x_2; t_1, t_2) = F_X(x_1, x_2; t_1 + \tau, t_2 + \tau) \quad \forall\, t_1, t_2, \tau$$

The right-hand side may be re-parametrised. Setting $\tau_0 = t_2 - t_1$, we have $t_2 + \tau = t_1 + \tau_0 + \tau$. So the equality becomes:

$$F_X(x_1, x_2; t_1, t_2) = F_X(x_1, x_2; 0,\, t_2 - t_1) = F_X(x_1, x_2; 0,\, \tau_0)$$

The joint distribution of $(X(t_1), X(t_2))$ therefore depends on $t_1, t_2$ only through the difference $t_2 - t_1 = \tau_0$. By the definition of the autocorrelation function as a function of the joint distribution, we have:

$$R_X(t_1, t_2) = \int\!\!\!\int x_1 x_2 \, dF_X(x_1, x_2; t_1, t_2) = \int\!\!\!\int x_1 x_2 \, dF_X(x_1, x_2; 0, \tau)$$

This is a function of $\tau$ alone. Hence $R_X(t_1, t_2) = R_X(\tau)$, with $\tau = t_2 - t_1$.

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: $R_X(0) \ge \vert R_X(\tau) \vert$ for Any Real SSS Process

This is a **classic KTU-favourite derivation**. The student must internalise the elegant algebraic trick.

> **Statement:** If $\{X(t)\}$ is a real SSS process with autocorrelation $R_X(\tau)$, then $R_X(0) \ge \vert R_X(\tau) \vert$ for every $\tau$.

**Proof:**

For any real number $\alpha$ and any time $t$, consider the non-negative mean square:

$$E\big[\big(X(t) + \alpha\, X(t + \tau)\big)^2\big] \;\ge\; 0$$

Expanding the square:

$$E\big[X^2(t)\big] + 2\alpha\, E\big[X(t)X(t+\tau)\big] + \alpha^2 E\big[X^2(t+\tau)\big] \;\ge\; 0$$

Apply the SSS properties (constant mean-square, lag-only autocorrelation):

$$R_X(0) + 2\alpha\, R_X(\tau) + \alpha^2 R_X(0) \;\ge\; 0$$

$$R_X(0)\big(1 + \alpha^2\big) + 2\alpha\, R_X(\tau) \;\ge\; 0$$

This is a **quadratic in $\alpha$**: $a\alpha^2 + b\alpha + c \ge 0$ where $a = R_X(0)$, $b = 2 R_X(\tau)$, $c = R_X(0)$.

For a real quadratic $a\alpha^2 + b\alpha + c$ to be non-negative for all real $\alpha$, its **discriminant** must be non-positive:

$$\Delta = b^2 - 4ac \;\le\; 0$$

Substitute:

$$\big(2 R_X(\tau)\big)^2 - 4 \cdot R_X(0) \cdot R_X(0) \;\le\; 0$$

$$4 R_X^2(\tau) - 4 R_X^2(0) \;\le\; 0$$

$$R_X^2(\tau) \;\le\; R_X^2(0)$$

Since $R_X(0) = E[X^2(t)] \ge 0$, we take the non-negative square root:

$$R_X(0) \;\ge\; \vert R_X(\tau) \vert$$

$\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Insight**
> The maximum of the autocorrelation function occurs at zero lag. As the lag $\vert\tau\vert$ increases, the autocorrelation **decays** (in absolute value), indicating that the process becomes "less correlated" with its distant past. This is the physical notion of **decorrelation**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: $R_X(\tau) = R_X(-\tau)$ (Even Symmetry)

> **Statement:** The autocorrelation of a real SSS process is an **even** function of $\tau$.

**Proof:**

By definition, $R_X(\tau) = E[X(t)X(t+\tau)]$. Perform a change of variable $u = t + \tau$, so $t = u - \tau$:

$$R_X(\tau) = E[X(u - \tau)X(u)] = R_X(-\tau)$$

The last equality follows because the SSS autocorrelation depends only on the *lag* — the lag between $u - \tau$ and $u$ is $u - (u - \tau) = \tau$, and the lag between $u$ and $u - \tau$ is $(u - \tau) - u = -\tau$, but since the autocorrelation is a function of the lag, both expressions are valid notations for the same function evaluated at the corresponding lag.

Equivalently, by the symmetry of the joint distribution under the SSS condition:

$$R_X(\tau) = E[X(t)X(t+\tau)] = E[X(t+\tau)X(t)] = R_X(-\tau)$$

The last step uses the commutativity of multiplication in the expectation.

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Verifying SSS for the Random-Phase Sinusoid

> **Problem:** Let $X(t) = A \cos(\omega t + \Theta)$, where $A$ and $\omega$ are constants and $\Theta$ is uniformly distributed on $[-\pi, \pi]$. Determine whether $\{X(t)\}$ is SSS.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Identify the random element.** The only random variable is $\Theta \sim U(-\pi, \pi)$.

**Step 2 — Verify the mean is constant in $t$.**

$$\mu_X(t) = E[X(t)] = E[A \cos(\omega t + \Theta)] = A \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \cos(\omega t + \theta) \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi}\, d\theta$$

Substitute $u = \omega t + \theta$, $du = d\theta$:

$$\mu_X(t) = \frac{A}{2\pi} \int_{\omega t - \pi}^{\omega t + \pi} \cos(u)\, du = \frac{A}{2\pi}\big[\sin(u)\big]_{\omega t - \pi}^{\omega t + \pi}$$

Using $\sin(\omega t + \pi) - \sin(\omega t - \pi) = 2\cos(\omega t)\sin(\pi) = 0$:

$$\mu_X(t) = 0 \quad \text{(constant, independent of } t\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the autocorrelation $R_X(t_1, t_2)$.**

$$R_X(t_1, t_2) = E[X(t_1)X(t_2)] = A^2 E\big[\cos(\omega t_1 + \Theta)\cos(\omega t_2 + \Theta)\big]$$

Apply the product-to-sum identity $\cos\alpha \cos\beta = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[\cos(\alpha - \beta) + \cos(\alpha + \beta)\big]$:

$$R_X(t_1, t_2) = \frac{A^2}{2} E\big[\cos(\omega(t_1 - t_2)) + \cos(\omega t_1 + \omega t_2 + 2\Theta)\big]$$

The first term has no $\Theta$, so its expectation is itself. The second term integrates to zero (by the same calculation as in Step 2):

$$R_X(t_1, t_2) = \frac{A^2}{2} \cos(\omega(t_1 - t_2)) + 0 = \frac{A^2}{2} \cos(\omega \tau), \quad \tau = t_1 - t_2$$

This depends only on $\tau$.

**Step 4 — Verify the variance is constant.**

$$\sigma_X^2 = E[X^2(t)] - \mu_X^2 = R_X(0) - 0 = \frac{A^2}{2} \cos(0) = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

This is a constant, independent of $t$.

**Step 5 — Verify all higher-order moments depend only on time differences.** By direct computation, the $n$-th joint moment

$$E[X(t_1) X(t_2) \cdots X(t_n)]$$

can be expressed as a sum of products of cosines whose arguments involve only time differences, with the random phase $\Theta$ averaging to zero in every term except those containing "DC" cosines of zero frequency (which are constants). Hence the $n$-th joint moment depends only on the set of time differences $\{t_i - t_j\}$.

> **Conclusion:** The random-phase sinusoid $X(t) = A\cos(\omega t + \Theta)$ with $\Theta \sim U(-\pi, \pi)$ is **Strict Sense Stationary (SSS)**.

$\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Remark:** A deterministic sinusoid $A \cos(\omega t + \theta_0)$ with a *fixed* phase $\theta_0$ is **not** SSS, because the value of the signal at a given $t$ is deterministic and the joint distribution is concentrated on a single point that moves with $t$. It is the **randomisation of the phase** that produces the SSS behaviour.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: White Noise is SSS

> **Problem:** Show that a white noise process $\{W(t)\}$ with $E[W(t)] = 0$ and $R_W(t_1, t_2) = N_0\, \delta(t_1 - t_2)$ is SSS.

**Solution Outline:**

- **Constant mean:** Given $E[W(t)] = 0$, which is constant in $t$. ✓
- **Autocorrelation depends only on lag:** $R_W(t_1, t_2) = N_0\, \delta(\tau)$ is a function of $\tau$ alone. ✓
- **Higher-order moments:** For Gaussian white noise, the joint distribution of $(W(t_1), \dots, W(t_n))$ is multivariate normal with mean vector $\mathbf{0}$ and covariance matrix $N_0\, \mathbf{I}$. This matrix depends on the time instants **only through the diagonal entries** $N_0\, \delta(t_i - t_i) = N_0$, which are shift-invariant. The multivariate normal distribution depends on the covariance matrix alone, which is unchanged under a uniform time shift. ✓
- **Conclusion:** Gaussian white noise is SSS. (In fact, white noise is *both* SSS and WSS.)

$\blacksquare$

# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL SCHEMATICS: HIERARCHY OF STATIONARITY

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Conceptual Hierarchy

The diagram below illustrates the logical hierarchy among the various notions of stationarity, with **SSS** at the apex as the strongest form.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STOCHASTIC PROCESS X OF T"] --> nodeB["Strict Sense Stationary SSS"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Wide Sense Stationary WSS"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Cyclostationary Process"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Non Stationary Process"]

    nodeB --> nodeF["All n th order joint distributions are shift invariant"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Mean is constant"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Variance is constant"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Autocorrelation R of tau is lag only"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["All higher order moments depend on time differences only"]

    nodeC --> nodeK["Mean is constant"]
    nodeC --> nodeL["Autocorrelation R of tau is lag only"]

    nodeB -.-> nodeC
    nodeC -.-> nodeM["SSS implies WSS but not conversely"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The dashed arrow from SSS to WSS represents the **implication** "SSS $\Rightarrow$ WSS" — every strictly stationary process is also wide-sense stationary, but the converse fails in general. The dashed arrow to the conclusion box highlights the **non-equivalence**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SSS Verification

The following diagram maps out the **operational pipeline** an engineer/statistician follows when verifying whether a given process is SSS. Use this as a *mental checklist* during exam preparation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["INPUT: Stochastic Process X of T"] --> nodeQ["STEP 1: Compute 1st order distribution F_X x t"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR{"Is F_X independent of t?"}
    nodeR -- "NO" --> nodeS["NOT SSS - Process Fails"]
    nodeR -- "YES" --> nodeT["STEP 2: Compute 2nd order distribution F_X x1 x2 t1 t2"]

    nodeT --> nodeU{"Does it depend only on t2 minus t1?"}
    nodeU -- "NO" --> nodeS
    nodeU -- "YES" --> nodeV["STEP 3: Compute n th order joint moments"]

    nodeV --> nodeW{"Do all moments depend only on time differences?"}
    nodeW -- "NO" --> nodeS
    nodeW -- "YES" --> nodeX["OUTPUT: Process IS Strict Sense Stationary"]

    nodeX --> nodeY["Extract Constant Mean mu_X"]
    nodeX --> nodeZ["Extract Autocorrelation R_X of tau"]
    nodeX --> nodeAA["Extract Constant Variance sigma squared"]
```

> **Operational Reading:** The flowchart enforces a **strict cascade** of checks — first-order, second-order, and full $n$-th order. The process earns the "SSS" label **only if every gate returns YES**.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Diagram — SSS vs. WSS at a Glance

| **Aspect** | **Strict Sense Stationary (SSS)** | **Wide Sense Stationary (WSS)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition basis** | Joint CDF shift-invariance for all $n$ | Mean constant + autocorrelation is lag-dependent |
| **Strength** | **Strongest** form | **Weaker** (only 1st & 2nd moments considered) |
| **Constant mean required?** | Yes (implied) | Yes (explicit) |
| **Constant variance required?** | Yes (implied) | Not required (only autocorrelation is checked) |
| **Autocorrelation lag-only?** | Yes (implied) | Yes (explicit) |
| **Higher-order moments** | Must all be shift-invariant | No constraint |
| **Implication** | $\text{SSS} \Rightarrow \text{WSS}$ | $\text{WSS} \not\Rightarrow \text{SSS}$ in general |
| **Example** | Gaussian white noise, random-phase sinusoid | Any SSS process; certain non-Gaussian processes with linear structure |

# ✍️ KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> A common mistake students make is to *conflate* SSS with WSS. They write: "A process with constant mean and a function-of-$\tau$-only autocorrelation is SSS." This is **false** unless the process is *Gaussian*. The full joint distribution (not just the first two moments) must be shift-invariant. Another frequent error is forgetting to **subtract the square of the mean** when computing the variance — marks deducted without mercy.

---

## 📝 PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

> **Define a strictly stationary stochastic process. State any three of its properties.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A stochastic process $\{X(t),\, t \in T\}$ is called **strictly stationary (SSS)** if, for every positive integer $n$, every set of time instants $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n \in T$, and every shift $\tau$ such that $t_i + \tau \in T$, the joint CDF satisfies:
>
> $$F_X(x_1, \dots, x_n;\, t_1, \dots, t_n) = F_X(x_1, \dots, x_n;\, t_1+\tau, \dots, t_n+\tau)$$
>
> **Three properties:**
> 1. The mean $E[X(t)] = \mu_X$ is **constant**, independent of $t$.
> 2. The variance $\sigma_X^2 = E[X^2(t)] - \mu_X^2$ is **constant**, independent of $t$.
> 3. The autocorrelation function $R_X(t_1, t_2) = E[X(t_1)X(t_2)]$ depends only on the **time lag** $\tau = t_2 - t_1$, i.e., $R_X(t_1, t_2) = R_X(\tau)$.

*(Examiner expects: clean definition + 3 named properties. 1 mark per item.)*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

> **Examine whether the random-phase process $X(t) = A\cos(\omega t + \Theta)$, where $\Theta$ is uniformly distributed on $[0, 2\pi]$, is strictly stationary.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Yes, the process is SSS.**
>
> 1. **Mean:** $E[X(t)] = \tfrac{A}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} \cos(\omega t + \theta)\, d\theta = 0$ — a constant independent of $t$.
> 2. **Autocorrelation:** $R_X(t_1, t_2) = E[A\cos(\omega t_1 + \Theta) \cdot A\cos(\omega t_2 + \Theta)] = \tfrac{A^2}{2}\cos(\omega(t_1 - t_2))$ — a function of the lag $\tau = t_1 - t_2$ alone.
> 3. **Higher-order moments:** By similar trigonometric product-to-sum arguments, every $n$-th joint moment depends only on time differences.
>
> Hence, all finite-dimensional distributions are shift-invariant $\Rightarrow$ **SSS**.

*(Examiner expects: at least mean, autocorrelation, and the lag-only property to be derived. Marks for each criterion.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Define a strictly stationary stochastic process. Show that for an SSS process $\{X(t)\}$, the autocorrelation function satisfies $R_X(0) \ge \vert R_X(\tau) \vert$ for all $\tau$.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A process is defined as $X(t) = A\cos(\omega t + \Theta)$, where $A, \omega$ are constants and $\Theta$ is uniformly distributed on $[0, 2\pi]$. Show that $\{X(t)\}$ is strictly stationary by computing the mean, autocorrelation, and demonstrating the lag-only dependence.

#### Model Solution to (a):

> **Definition (3 marks):** A stochastic process $\{X(t), t \in T\}$ is called **Strict Sense Stationary (SSS)** if for every $n \ge 1$, every choice of $t_1, \dots, t_n \in T$ and every $\tau$ such that $t_i + \tau \in T$:
>
> $$F_X(x_1, \dots, x_n; t_1, \dots, t_n) = F_X(x_1, \dots, x_n; t_1 + \tau, \dots, t_n + \tau)$$
>
> That is, the joint distribution of $(X(t_1), \dots, X(t_n))$ is identical to that of $(X(t_1+\tau), \dots, X(t_n+\tau))$.
>
> **Proof of $R_X(0) \ge \vert R_X(\tau) \vert$ (4 marks):**
>
> For any real $\alpha$ and any $t, \tau$, the non-negative mean-square:
>
> $$E\big[\big(X(t) + \alpha X(t+\tau)\big)^2\big] \ge 0$$
>
> Expanding:
>
> $$E[X^2(t)] + 2\alpha E[X(t)X(t+\tau)] + \alpha^2 E[X^2(t+\tau)] \ge 0$$
>
> Using the SSS properties (constant mean-square, lag-only autocorrelation):
>
> $$R_X(0) + 2\alpha R_X(\tau) + \alpha^2 R_X(0) \ge 0$$
>
> This is a quadratic in $\alpha$ with non-negative leading coefficient $R_X(0) \ge 0$. For the quadratic to be non-negative for **all** real $\alpha$, the discriminant must satisfy:
>
> $$\Delta = \big(2 R_X(\tau)\big)^2 - 4 R_X(0) \cdot R_X(0) \le 0$$
>
> Simplifying:
>
> $$4 R_X^2(\tau) - 4 R_X^2(0) \le 0 \;\Rightarrow\; R_X^2(\tau) \le R_X^2(0)$$
>
> Taking non-negative square roots (since $R_X(0) \ge 0$):
>
> $$R_X(0) \ge \vert R_X(\tau) \vert$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

> **Step 1 — Identify the random element (1 mark):**
> The only randomness in $X(t) = A\cos(\omega t + \Theta)$ is in the phase $\Theta \sim U(0, 2\pi)$. The amplitude $A$ and frequency $\omega$ are deterministic constants.
>
> **Step 2 — Compute the mean (2 marks):**
>
> $$E[X(t)] = \int_0^{2\pi} A\cos(\omega t + \theta) \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi}\, d\theta$$
>
> Substitute $u = \omega t + \theta$:
>
> $$E[X(t)] = \frac{A}{2\pi} \int_{\omega t}^{\omega t + 2\pi} \cos(u)\, du = \frac{A}{2\pi}\big[\sin(u)\big]_{\omega t}^{\omega t + 2\pi}$$
>
> Using $\sin(\omega t + 2\pi) = \sin(\omega t)$:
>
> $$E[X(t)] = \frac{A}{2\pi} \cdot 0 = 0$$
>
> Hence the mean is **constant** ($\mu_X = 0$) for all $t$.
>
> **Step 3 — Compute the autocorrelation (3 marks):**
>
> $$R_X(t_1, t_2) = E[A\cos(\omega t_1 + \Theta) \cdot A\cos(\omega t_2 + \Theta)] = A^2 E[\cos(\omega t_1 + \Theta)\cos(\omega t_2 + \Theta)]$$
>
> Using $\cos\alpha \cos\beta = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(\alpha - \beta) + \cos(\alpha + \beta)]$:
>
> $$R_X(t_1, t_2) = \frac{A^2}{2} E\big[\cos(\omega(t_1 - t_2)) + \cos(\omega t_1 + \omega t_2 + 2\Theta)\big]$$
>
> The first term is deterministic. The second term averages to zero (by the same calculation as Step 2):
>
> $$E[\cos(\omega t_1 + \omega t_2 + 2\Theta)] = 0$$
>
> Therefore:
>
> $$R_X(t_1, t_2) = \frac{A^2}{2}\cos(\omega(t_1 - t_2)) = \frac{A^2}{2}\cos(\omega \tau), \quad \tau = t_1 - t_2$$
>
> This depends only on the lag $\tau$, as required.
>
> **Step 4 — Variance (1 mark):**
>
> $$\sigma_X^2 = R_X(0) - 0 = \frac{A^2}{2} \quad \text{(constant)}$$
>
> **Conclusion:** The mean is constant, the autocorrelation depends only on the lag, the variance is constant, and by extension (analogous trigonometric computations), all higher-order joint moments depend only on time differences. Hence $\{X(t)\}$ is **Strict Sense Stationary**. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Define the **autocorrelation function** $R_X(t_1, t_2)$ of a stochastic process $\{X(t)\}$. For a strictly stationary process, show that:
>   (i) $R_X(\tau)$ is an even function of $\tau$.
>   (ii) The autocorrelation matrix is positive semi-definite.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a discrete-time process $X_n = Z_n + \tfrac{1}{2} Z_{n-1}$, where $\{Z_n\}$ is a sequence of i.i.d. random variables with $E[Z_n] = 0$ and $\text{Var}[Z_n] = \sigma^2$. Verify whether $\{X_n\}$ is SSS by computing the mean, variance, and autocorrelation function $R_X(k)$.

#### Model Solution to (a):

> **Definition (1 mark):** The autocorrelation function of $\{X(t)\}$ is defined as
>
> $$R_X(t_1, t_2) = E[X(t_1)X(t_2)]$$
>
> For a strictly stationary process, $R_X(t_1, t_2) = R_X(\tau)$ where $\tau = t_2 - t_1$.
>
> **(i) Even Function — $R_X(\tau) = R_X(-\tau)$ (3 marks):**
>
> $$R_X(-\tau) = E[X(t)X(t - \tau)] = E[X(t - \tau)X(t)] = R_X(\tau)$$
>
> The last equality follows because the autocorrelation depends only on the *lag*, which is the time difference between the two indices, and multiplication inside the expectation is commutative. Hence $R_X(\tau)$ is **even** in $\tau$.
>
> **(ii) Positive Semi-Definiteness of the Autocorrelation Matrix (3 marks):**
>
> For any set of real numbers $c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n$ and any time instants $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n$, consider:
>
> $$E\Big[\Big(\sum_{i=1}^n c_i X(t_i)\Big)^2\Big] \ge 0$$
>
> Expanding:
>
> $$\sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^n c_i c_j E[X(t_i)X(t_j)] = \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^n c_i c_j R_X(t_i, t_j) \ge 0$$
>
> For an SSS process, $R_X(t_i, t_j) = R_X(t_j - t_i)$. The matrix $\mathbf{R} = [R_X(t_j - t_i)]_{i,j}$ is called the **autocorrelation matrix** and is **positive semi-definite** by the above argument. In particular, for $n = 2$ with $t_1 = 0, t_2 = \tau$:
>
> $$\det\begin{pmatrix} R_X(0) & R_X(\tau) \\ R_X(\tau) & R_X(0) \end{pmatrix} = R_X^2(0) - R_X^2(\tau) \ge 0 \;\Rightarrow\; R_X(0) \ge \vert R_X(\tau) \vert$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

> **Step 1 — Mean (2 marks):**
>
> $$E[X_n] = E\big[Z_n + \tfrac{1}{2} Z_{n-1}\big] = E[Z_n] + \tfrac{1}{2} E[Z_{n-1}] = 0 + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 0 = 0$$
>
> The mean is constant (zero) for all $n$. ✓
>
> **Step 2 — Autocorrelation $R_X(k) = E[X_n X_{n+k}]$ (3 marks):**
>
> Substitute $X_n = Z_n + \tfrac{1}{2} Z_{n-1}$ and $X_{n+k} = Z_{n+k} + \tfrac{1}{2} Z_{n+k-1}$:
>
> $$R_X(k) = E\Big[\big(Z_n + \tfrac{1}{2} Z_{n-1}\big)\big(Z_{n+k} + \tfrac{1}{2} Z_{n+k-1}\big)\Big]$$
>
> Expand into four terms:
>
> $$R_X(k) = E[Z_n Z_{n+k}] + \tfrac{1}{2} E[Z_n Z_{n+k-1}] + \tfrac{1}{2} E[Z_{n-1} Z_{n+k}] + \tfrac{1}{4} E[Z_{n-1} Z_{n+k-1}]$$
>
> Since $\{Z_n\}$ are i.i.d. with $E[Z_n^2] = \sigma^2$:
>
> - $E[Z_n Z_{n+k}] = \sigma^2$ if $k = 0$, else $0$.
> - $E[Z_n Z_{n+k-1}] = \sigma^2$ if $k = 1$, else $0$.
> - $E[Z_{n-1} Z_{n+k}] = \sigma^2$ if $k = -1$, else $0$.
> - $E[Z_{n-1} Z_{n+k-1}] = \sigma^2$ if $k = 0$, else $0$.
>
> **Case $k = 0$:**
>
> $$R_X(0) = \sigma^2 + 0 + 0 + \tfrac{1}{4}\sigma^2 = \tfrac{5}{4}\sigma^2$$
>
> **Case $k = 1$:**
>
> $$R_X(1) = 0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2 + 0 + 0 = \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2$$
>
> **Case $k = -1$:** By symmetry (even function), $R_X(-1) = \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2$.
>
> **Case $\vert k \vert \ge 2$:** All four terms are zero $\Rightarrow R_X(k) = 0$.
>
> **Step 3 — Variance (1 mark):**
>
> $$\text{Var}[X_n] = R_X(0) - (E[X_n])^2 = \tfrac{5}{4}\sigma^2 - 0 = \tfrac{5}{4}\sigma^2 \quad \text{(constant)}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
>
> The mean is constant, the variance is constant, and the autocorrelation $R_X(k)$ depends only on the lag $k$ (a single variable). Since $\{Z_n\}$ is i.i.d., all higher-order joint moments of $\{X_n\}$ also depend only on the time-lag structure. Therefore, the process $\{X_n\}$ is **Strict Sense Stationary (SSS)**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- **SSS Definition (Master it word-for-word):** Joint CDF of $(X(t_1), \dots, X(t_n))$ equals that of $(X(t_1+\tau), \dots, X(t_n+\tau))$ for every $n$ and every $\tau$.
- **Hierarchy:** SSS (strongest) $\Rightarrow$ $n$-th order $\Rightarrow$ 2nd order $\Rightarrow$ 1st order (weakest). Reverse implications **do not hold** in general.
- **Mandatory consequences of SSS:**
  - Mean $E[X(t)] = \mu$ — **constant**.
  - Mean-square $E[X^2(t)]$ — **constant**.
  - Variance $\sigma^2$ — **constant**.
  - Autocorrelation $R_X(t_1, t_2) = R_X(\tau)$ — **function of the lag alone**.
  - Autocovariance $C_X(\tau) = R_X(\tau) - \mu^2$ — **function of the lag alone**.
- **Properties of $R_X(\tau)$ for real SSS processes:**
  - **Even:** $R_X(\tau) = R_X(-\tau)$.
  - **Bounded:** $R_X(0) \ge \vert R_X(\tau) \vert$ for all $\tau$.
  - **Maximum at zero lag:** $\vert R_X(\tau) \vert$ achieves its peak at $\tau = 0$.
  - **Decay at infinity:** $\lim_{\vert\tau\vert \to \infty} R_X(\tau) = \mu^2$ (for non-periodic SSS processes).
- **SSS vs. WSS (memorise the distinction):**
  - SSS $\Rightarrow$ WSS (always). WSS $\not\Rightarrow$ SSS (unless the process is Gaussian).
  - SSS controls **all** finite-dimensional distributions. WSS controls only the **first two moments**.
- **Classic SSS examples:**
  - **Gaussian white noise:** $E[W(t)] = 0$, $R_W(\tau) = N_0\, \delta(\tau)$.
  - **Random-phase sinusoid:** $X(t) = A\cos(\omega t + \Theta)$, $\Theta \sim U(0, 2\pi)$.
  - **Strictly stationary linear processes** driven by i.i.d. noise.
- **Classic NON-SSS examples:**
  - Deterministic sinusoid with fixed phase.
  - Random walk $S_n = \sum_{i=1}^n Z_i$ (variance grows with $n$).
  - Linear trend $X(t) = at + b + Y$ (mean drifts with $t$).
- **Verification recipe:** Compute mean $\to$ check constant. Compute variance $\to$ check constant. Compute autocorrelation $\to$ check lag-only. For full SSS, verify all higher-order joint moments are shift-invariant.
- **Engineering use-cases:** Matched filtering, Wiener filtering, spectral density estimation, clutter modelling in radar, EEG analysis — all assume (at least quasi-) stationarity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner Warning**
> Marks are routinely lost in three places: (1) forgetting to check the **autocorrelation matrix** for positive semi-definiteness, (2) confusing SSS with WSS when the process is **not Gaussian**, and (3) omitting the proof that **higher-order moments** depend only on time differences. Address all three for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autocorrelation Functions and Its Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GBMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE – 4 > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Autocorrelation Functions and Its Properties ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition The **autocorrelation function** of a random process $X(t)$ is a measure of the statistical dependence between the values of the process at two different time instants. It quantifies how "correlated" or "similar" a signal is to a time-shifted version of itself. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Rigorously Adopted):** > For a real-valued random process $\{X(t), t \in T\}$, the **autocorrelation function** $R_X(t_1, t_2)$ is defined as the joint second moment of the random variables $X(t_1)$ and $X(t_2)$: > $$R_X(t_1, t_2) = E[X(t_1) X(t_2)]$$ > When the process is **strict-sense stationary (SSS)** or **wide-sense stationary (WSS)**, the autocorrelation depends only on the time difference $\tau = t_2 - t_1$ and is written as: > $$R_X(\tau) = E[X(t) X(t + \tau)]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Wider Connection:** The autocorrelation function is the cornerstone of the **Wiener–Khinchin theorem**, which links it directly to the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** via the Fourier transform — a foundational result in electrical engineering for spectrum analysis, filter design, and communication systems. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Memory" of a Signal > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy: "Yesterday's Weather vs. Today's Weather"** > Imagine a weather station recording temperature $X(t)$ every hour. The autocorrelation function $R_X(\tau)$ answers the question: *"How strongly is today's temperature correlated with the temperature $\tau$ hours from now?"* > - If $\tau = 0$: We are comparing the temperature with **itself at the same instant** — perfect correlation $\Rightarrow R_X(0)$ is the largest value, equal to the **average power** (mean square value). > - If $\tau$ is small (e.g., 1 hour): The temperature is *fairly* correlated — the function value is high. > - If $\tau$ is very large (e.g., 1 year): For a non-periodic process, the future temperature is *practically uncorrelated* with today's — $R_X(\tau)$ approaches the **square of the mean** $\mu^2$. > - For a periodic process (e.g., seasonal temperature), the autocorrelation itself becomes **periodic** in $\tau$. In electrical engineering terms, the autocorrelation function is the **time-domain fingerprint of a stochastic signal**, and the PSD is its **frequency-domain fingerprint**. ### 1.3 Why It Matters in Electrical Science | Application Area | Role of Autocorrelation | |------------------|--------------------------| | **Communication Systems** | Detection of periodic signals buried in noise; synchronization | | **Radar & Sonar** | Range estimation; matched filtering | | **Speech & Audio Processing** | Pitch detection; linear predictive coding (LPC) | | **Control Systems** | Identifying plant dynamics from input-output data | | **Power Systems** | Harmonic analysis of voltage and current disturbances | Explicit Constants and Metrics (in **bold**): - The **maximum value** of $R_X(\tau)$ always occurs at $\tau = 0$ and equals the **average power** $E[X^2(t)]$. - The **DC limit** is $\lim_{\tau \to \infty} R_X(\tau) = \mu_X^2$ (for processes without periodic components), where $\mu_X$ is the **mean**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Autocorrelation for Different Classes of Processes #### 2.1.1 Strict-Sense Stationary (SSS) Process For an SSS process, the joint PDF of $\{X(t_1), X(t_2)\}$ depends only on the lag $\tau = t_2 - t_1$: $$R_X(\tau) = E[X(t) X(t + \tau)]$$ #### 2.1.2 Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS) Process A process is WSS if its mean is constant and the autocorrelation depends only on $\tau$: $$E[X(t)] = \mu_X = \text{constant}$$ $$R_X(t, t + \tau) = R_X(\tau) \quad \text{for all } t, \tau$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hierarchy of Stationarity:** > SSS $\Rightarrow$ WSS $\Rightarrow$ Existence of $R_X(\tau)$ as a function of $\tau$ alone. > However, **WSS does NOT imply SSS** in general. #### 2.1.3 Cyclostationary Processes For periodic non-stationary processes (common in communication systems with periodic carrier signals), the autocorrelation is periodic in $t$: $$R_X(t, t + \tau) = R_X(t + T, t + T + \tau)$$ where $T$ is the fundamental period of the process. ### 2.2 The Five Fundamental Properties of the Autocorrelation Function Let $\{X(t)\}$ be a real-valued WSS process. Then the autocorrelation function $R_X(\tau)$ possesses the following properties: | # | Property | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Interpretation | |---|----------|------------------------|----------------------------| | 1 | **Even Symmetry** | $R_X(\tau) = R_X(-\tau)$ | Past-future symmetry; signal and its mirror image are equally correlated | | 2 | **Maximum at Origin** | $R_X(0) \geq R_X(\tau)$ for all $\tau$ | The signal is most correlated with itself at zero lag; $R_X(0) = E[X^2(t)]$ = average power | | 3 | **Bounded by Origin Value** | $\vert R_X(\tau) \vert \leq R_X(0)$ | The autocorrelation is always bounded by the mean square value | | 4 | **Non-Negative Definiteness** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_i a_j R_X(t_i - t_j) \geq 0$ | Ensures $R_X(\tau)$ is a valid correlation function; guarantees PSD is real and non-negative | | 5 | **Asymptotic Value (No Periodic Component)** | $\lim_{\vert\tau\vert \to \infty} R_X(\tau) = \mu_X^2$ | For a process with no periodic component, the value at infinite lag equals the squared mean | | 6 | **Periodic Behavior** | If $X(t)$ has a periodic component of period $T$, then $R_X(\tau + T) = R_X(\tau)$ | Periodic signals leave a "fingerprint" in the autocorrelation | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Hint:** Property 1 (even symmetry) and Property 2 (maximum at origin) are the **two most-tested properties** in KTU examinations. Master the proof of both! ### 2.3 Autocovariance Function The **autocovariance** function $C_X(\tau)$ strips away the effect of the mean: $$C_X(\tau) = E[\{X(t) - \mu_X\}\{X(t + \tau) - \mu_X\}] = R_X(\tau) - \mu_X^2$$ **Key facts:** - $C_X(0) = \sigma_X^2$ (the variance) - $\lim_{\vert\tau\vert \to \infty} C_X(\tau) = 0$ (for processes with no periodic component) - $R_X(\tau) = C_X(\tau) + \mu_X^2$ ### 2.4 Cross-Correlation Function (Companion Concept) For two jointly WSS processes $X(t)$ and $Y(t)$, the **cross-correlation** is: $$R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(t) Y(t + \tau)]$$ **Properties of Cross-Correlation:** - $R_{XY}(-\tau) = R_{YX}(\tau)$ - $\vert R_{XY}(\tau) \vert \leq \sqrt{R_X(0) R_Y(0)}$ - If $X(t)$ and $Y(t)$ are **uncorrelated**, then $R_{XY}(\tau) = \mu_X \mu_Y$ for all $\tau$. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula | Expression | Condition | |---------|------------|-----------| | Autocorrelation (general) | $R_X(t_1, t_2) = E[X(t_1)X(t_2)]$ | Any process | | Autocorrelation (WSS) | $R_X(\tau) = E[X(t)X(t + \tau)]$ | WSS process | | Autocovariance | $C_X(\tau) = R_X(\tau) - \mu_X^2$ | WSS process | | Even Symmetry | $R_X(\tau) = R_X(-\tau)$ | Real WSS process | | Maximum Property | $R_X(0) \geq \vert R_X(\tau) \vert$ | WSS process | | Mean-Square Value | $R_X(0) = E[X^2(t)]$ | All processes | | Asymptotic Limit | $\lim_{\vert\tau\vert \to \infty} R_X(\tau) = \mu_X^2$ | Ergodic, no periodic component | | Cross-Correlation | $R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(t)Y(t+\tau)]$ | Jointly WSS | | Cross-Correlation Symmetry | $R_{XY}(-\tau) = R_{YX}(\tau)$ | Real processes | | Bounded Cross-Correlation | $\vert R_{XY}(\tau) \vert \leq \sqrt{R_X(0) R_Y(0)}$ | Jointly WSS | ### 2.6 Real-World Utility Summary - **Power Spectral Density (PSD):** The Fourier transform of $R_X(\tau)$ gives the PSD — a critical tool for frequency analysis of random signals (Wiener–Khinchin theorem). - **Signal Detection:** Matched filters use autocorrelation to detect known signals in noise. - **System Identification:** The Wiener-Hopf equation $R_{XY}(\tau) = \int h(\alpha) R_X(\tau - \alpha) d\alpha$ relates input-output correlations to the system's impulse response $h(t)$. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Proof That $R_X(\tau)$ is an Even Function > **Statement:** For a real-valued WSS process $\{X(t)\}$, $R_X(\tau) = R_X(-\tau)$. **Proof:** By the definition of the autocorrelation function for a WSS process: $$R_X(\tau) = E[X(t) X(t + \tau)]$$ The expectation of the product of two jointly distributed random variables is **symmetric** in the sense that: $$E[A B] = E[B A]$$ Now we apply this symmetry to $R_X(\tau)$. Letting $A = X(t)$ and $B = X(t + \tau)$: $$R_X(\tau) = E[X(t) X(t + \tau)] = E[X(t + \tau) X(t)]$$ We can rewrite $E[X(t + \tau) X(t)]$ by recognizing the time-shift structure. Let $t' = t + \tau$. Then $t = t' - \tau$, and: $$E[X(t + \tau) X(t)] = E[X(t') X(t' - \tau)]$$ By WSS, $R_X(\tau)$ depends only on the time-difference, not on the absolute time. The difference here is $t' - (t' - \tau) = \tau$, so: $$E[X(t') X(t' - \tau)] = R_X(\tau)$$ But we can also write this with a sign change. Note that $R_X(-\tau)$ is defined as: $$R_X(-\tau) = E[X(t) X(t - \tau)]$$ Comparing, the expression $E[X(t') X(t' - \tau)]$ is the same as $E[X(s) X(s - \tau)]$ (with $s = t'$), which by definition is $R_X(-\tau)$. Therefore: $$R_X(\tau) = E[X(t) X(t + \tau)] = E[X(t) X(t - \tau)] = R_X(-\tau)$$ $\blacksquare$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Proof That $R_X(0) \geq R_X(\tau)$ (Maximum at Origin) > **Statement:** $R_X(0) = E[X^2(t)] \geq E[X(t)X(t+\tau)] = R_X(\tau)$ for any $\tau$. **Proof:** Consider the non-negative mean-square value of the difference $X(t) - X(t + \tau)$: $$E[\{X(t) - X(t + \tau)\}^2] \geq 0$$ This is true because the square of a real random variable is non-negative, and so is its expected value. Expanding the square: $$E[X^2(t) - 2 X(t) X(t + \tau) + X^2(t + \tau)] \geq 0$$ Using linearity of expectation: $$E[X^2(t)] - 2 E[X(t) X(t + \tau)] + E[X^2(t + \tau)] \geq 0$$ By WSS, the mean-square value is constant in time, so $E[X^2(t)] = E[X^2(t + \tau)] = R_X(0)$. Also, $E[X(t) X(t + \tau)] = R_X(\tau)$. Substituting: $$R_X(0) - 2 R_X(\tau) + R_X(0) \geq 0$$ $$2 R_X(0) - 2 R_X(\tau) \geq 0$$ $$2 R_X(0) \geq 2 R_X(\tau)$$ $$\therefore R_X(0) \geq R_X(\tau)$$ **Extension:** Replacing $X(t + \tau)$ with $a X(t + \tau)$ (where $a = -1$) gives a similar inequality proving $\vert R_X(\tau) \vert \leq R_X(0)$. This is the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality for random variables in disguise. $\blacksquare$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Asymptotic Limit of $R_X(\tau)$ > **Statement:** For an ergodic WSS process with no periodic component, $\lim_{\vert\tau\vert \to \infty} R_X(\tau) = \mu_X^2$. **Proof Outline:** For large $\tau$, the random variables $X(t)$ and $X(t + \tau)$ become **statistically independent** (as their separation in time becomes much greater than the process's "memory" or correlation time). When two random variables are independent: $$E[X(t) X(t + \tau)] = E[X(t)] \cdot E[X(t + \tau)]$$ For a WSS process, the mean is constant, $E[X(t)] = E[X(t + \tau)] = \mu_X$. Therefore: $$\lim_{\vert\tau\vert \to \infty} R_X(\tau) = \mu_X \cdot \mu_X = \mu_X^2$$ **Equivalent statement using autocovariance:** $$\lim_{\vert\tau\vert \to \infty} C_X(\tau) = \lim_{\vert\tau\vert \to \infty} [R_X(\tau) - \mu_X^2] = 0$$ This confirms that the autocovariance (and hence the "linear statistical dependence") vanishes as the time lag grows, **provided the process contains no periodic component**. $\blacksquare$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Autocorrelation Examples | Process Type | $R_X(\tau)$ | Key Insight | |--------------|-------------|-------------| | **White Noise** (zero mean, variance $\sigma^2$) | $R_X(\tau) = \sigma^2 \delta(\tau)$ | Impulsive at $\tau = 0$; uncorrelated samples | | **Constant DC** $X(t) = C$ | $R_X(\tau) = C^2$ | Flat line; $R_X(\tau)$ is constant and equals $\mu^2$ | | **Random Binary Wave** $\pm A$ with probability $0.5$ | $R_X(\tau) = A^2 \left(1 - \frac{2\vert\tau\vert}{T}\right)$ for $\vert\tau\vert \leq T/2$ | Triangular shape; drops to 0 outside | | **Sinusoidal Process** $X(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \Theta)$ with $\Theta$ uniform on $[0, 2\pi)$ | $R_X(\tau) = \dfrac{A^2}{2} \cos(\omega_0 \tau)$ | Purely oscillatory; mean is 0, so $R_X(\tau)$ oscillates around 0 (NOT around $\mu^2 = 0$, but with a periodic structure) | | **Sum of DC + Sinusoid** $X(t) = A_0 + A_1 \cos(\omega_0 t + \Theta)$ | $R_X(\tau) = A_0^2 + \dfrac{A_1^2}{2} \cos(\omega_0 \tau)$ | DC part contributes $A_0^2$ (the $\mu^2$ term); sinusoidal part contributes oscillating term | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall #1:** For a process with a **periodic component**, the asymptotic limit $\mu^2$ does **NOT** hold — the autocorrelation is **periodic** in $\tau$ and does not decay. The DC part $\mu^2$ shifts the entire autocorrelation up by $\mu^2$, but the periodic part continues to oscillate forever. ### 3.5 Detailed Worked Example > **Example (Frequently Asked in KTU):** A WSS process is given by $X(t) = 2 + Y(t)$, where $Y(t)$ is a zero-mean WSS process with autocorrelation $R_Y(\tau) = e^{-3\vert\tau\vert}$. Find: > (a) The mean of $X(t)$. > (b) The autocorrelation $R_X(\tau)$ of $X(t)$. > (c) The autocovariance $C_X(\tau)$. **Solution:** **(a) Mean of $X(t)$:** $$\mu_X = E[X(t)] = E[2 + Y(t)] = 2 + E[Y(t)] = 2 + 0 = 2$$ **(b) Autocorrelation $R_X(\tau)$:** $$R_X(\tau) = E[X(t) X(t + \tau)] = E[(2 + Y(t))(2 + Y(t + \tau))]$$ $$= E[4 + 2Y(t + \tau) + 2Y(t) + Y(t) Y(t + \tau)]$$ $$= 4 + 2 E[Y(t + \tau)] + 2 E[Y(t)] + E[Y(t) Y(t + \tau)]$$ $$= 4 + 2(0) + 2(0) + R_Y(\tau)$$ $$= 4 + e^{-3\vert\tau\vert}$$ **(c) Autocovariance $C_X(\tau)$:** $$C_X(\tau) = R_X(\tau) - \mu_X^2 = (4 + e^{-3\vert\tau\vert}) - 2^2 = e^{-3\vert\tau\vert}$$ **Verification:** $C_X(0) = e^0 = 1 = \sigma_Y^2$ (variance of $Y(t)$). ✓ **Quick check on $R_X(\tau)$:** - $R_X(0) = 4 + 1 = 5$ ✓ - $R_X(-\tau) = 4 + e^{-3\vert-\tau\vert} = 4 + e^{-3\vert\tau\vert} = R_X(\tau)$ ✓ (even) - $\lim_{\tau \to \infty} R_X(\tau) = 4 = \mu_X^2$ ✓ $\blacksquare$ ### 3.6 Another Classic KTU Example — Sinusoidal Process > **Example:** $X(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \Theta)$, where $A$ and $\omega_0$ are constants and $\Theta$ is uniformly distributed on $[0, 2\pi)$. Show that $R_X(\tau) = \dfrac{A^2}{2} \cos(\omega_0 \tau)$. **Solution:** $$R_X(\tau) = E[A \cos(\omega_0 t + \Theta) \cdot A \cos(\omega_0 (t + \tau) + \Theta)]$$ $$= A^2 E[\cos(\omega_0 t + \Theta) \cos(\omega_0 t + \omega_0 \tau + \Theta)]$$ Using the product-to-sum identity $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$: $$= \frac{A^2}{2} E\left[\cos(\omega_0 \tau) + \cos(2\omega_0 t + \omega_0 \tau + 2\Theta)\right]$$ Since $\Theta$ is uniform on $[0, 2\pi)$, the term $2\omega_0 t + \omega_0 \tau + 2\Theta$ is uniform on a $4\pi$ range. The expected value of a cosine of a uniform phase is zero: $$E[\cos(2\omega_0 t + \omega_0 \tau + 2\Theta)] = 0$$ Therefore: $$R_X(\tau) = \frac{A^2}{2} \cos(\omega_0 \tau)$$ **Key Observations:** - $\mu_X = 0$ (since the process is sinusoidal with random phase). - $R_X(0) = \dfrac{A^2}{2}$ (the average power of the sinusoid). - $R_X(\tau)$ is **periodic** with period $T_0 = 2\pi / \omega_0$ — this is the signature of a periodic process in its autocorrelation. - $\lim_{\tau \to \infty} R_X(\tau) \neq \mu_X^2 = 0$ in a simple sense — the function **oscillates** and does not settle to a single value. The envelope of oscillation is $A^2/2$, while the lower bound is $-A^2/2$. $\blacksquare$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Autocorrelation Computation The following block diagram shows the canonical procedure for computing $R_X(\tau)$ from a random process $X(t)$: ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Random Process X of t"] --> node2["Time Shift by tau: X of t plus tau"] node2 --> node3["Element-wise Product: X of t times X of t plus tau"] node1 --> node3 node3 --> node4["Statistical Expectation: Ensemble Average"] node4 --> node5["Autocorrelation Function R of tau"] node5 --> node6a{"Process WSS?"} node6a -- Yes --> node6b["Single-variable function of tau only"] node6a -- No --> node6c["Two-variable function R of t1, t2"] ``` ### 4.2 Properties Summary as a Mind-Map ```mermaid mindmap root((R_X of tau Properties)) nodeA["Symmetry"] nodeA1["Even: R of tau equals R of minus tau"] nodeB["Bounds"] nodeB1["Maximum at tau equals zero"] nodeB2["Absolute value less than or equal to R of zero"] nodeC["Limiting Behavior"] nodeC1["Limit as tau goes to infinity equals mu squared"] nodeC2["Periodic if process has periodic component"] nodeD["Spectral Link"] nodeD1["Fourier Transform gives PSD via Wiener Khinchin"] nodeE["Special Identities"] nodeE1["R of zero equals mean square value"] nodeE2["R of zero minus mu squared equals variance"] ``` ### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture: Autocorrelation in a Communication Receiver ```mermaid flowchart LR sigin["Received Signal r of t equals s of t plus n of t"] --> corr["Autocorrelator Block R of tau"] corr --> plot["Plot R of tau vs tau"] plot --> detect{"Periodic Peak Detected?"} detect -- Yes --> sync["Extract Synchronization / Range Info"] detect -- No --> noisecheck["Pure Noise: No Periodic Component"] sync --> out["Output to Decoder / Matched Filter"] noisecheck --> out ``` ### 4.4 Autocorrelation vs. Cross-Correlation — Comparison Block ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph AC["Autocorrelation"] nodeAC1["Same Process X of t"] nodeAC2["Lag: tau"] nodeAC3["R of tau equals E of X of t times X of t plus tau"] nodeAC1 --> nodeAC2 --> nodeAC3 end subgraph CC["Cross-Correlation"] nodeCC1["Two Different Processes X of t and Y of t"] nodeCC2["Lag: tau"] nodeCC3["R of XY of tau equals E of X of t times Y of t plus tau"] nodeCC1 --> nodeCC2 --> nodeCC3 end ``` ### 4.5 Wiener-Khinchin Theorem — Bridge Between Time and Frequency ```mermaid flowchart LR A["R of tau
Time Domain"] -- "Fourier Transform" --> B["S of omega
Power Spectral Density"] B -- "Inverse Fourier Transform" --> A style A fill:#f9f,stroke:#333 style B fill:#9ff,stroke:#333 ``` **Relation:** $$S_X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_X(\tau) e^{-j\omega \tau} \, d\tau \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad R_X(\tau) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_X(\omega) e^{j\omega \tau} \, d\omega$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever you see a question that asks to "find the PSD" of a process, **the first step is to compute the autocorrelation**. This is a near-universal pattern in KTU problems. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A] **Q: Define the autocorrelation function of a wide-sense stationary random process. State any two of its properties.** **Model Answer:** The autocorrelation function of a WSS random process $X(t)$ is defined as: $$R_X(\tau) = E[X(t) X(t + \tau)]$$ It depends only on the time lag $\tau$. **Two key properties:** 1. **Even Symmetry:** $R_X(\tau) = R_X(-\tau)$ for all real $\tau$. 2. **Maximum at Origin:** $R_X(0) \geq \vert R_X(\tau) \vert$ for all $\tau$, where $R_X(0) = E[X^2(t)]$ is the average power. --- #### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B] **Q: Distinguish between autocorrelation and cross-correlation functions.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Autocorrelation $R_X(\tau)$ | Cross-Correlation $R_{XY}(\tau)$ | |---------|------------------------------|-----------------------------------| | **Argument** | Same process $X(t)$ at two times | Two different processes $X(t)$ and $Y(t)$ | | **Definition** | $R_X(\tau) = E[X(t) X(t + \tau)]$ | $R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(t) Y(t + \tau)]$ | | **Symmetry** | $R_X(\tau) = R_X(-\tau)$ | $R_{XY}(-\tau) = R_{YX}(\tau)$ | | **At $\tau = 0$** | $R_X(0) = E[X^2(t)]$ (average power) | $R_{XY}(0) = E[X(t) Y(t)]$ (a measure of joint variability) | | **Use** | Detects periodicity; spectral analysis | Detects delay between signals; system ID | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper 1] > **(a)** Define the autocorrelation function of a WSS process. Prove that $R_X(\tau)$ is an even function and that $R_X(0) \geq R_X(\tau)$. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A random process is defined as $X(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \Theta)$, where $A$ and $\omega_0$ are constants and $\Theta$ is uniformly distributed over $[0, 2\pi]$. Find its autocorrelation function and show that it is periodic. Comment on the asymptotic behavior. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Definition and Proofs:** **Definition:** For a WSS process $\{X(t)\}$, the autocorrelation function is: $$R_X(\tau) = E[X(t) X(t + \tau)]$$ **Proof of Even Symmetry:** $$R_X(\tau) = E[X(t) X(t + \tau)] = E[X(t + \tau) X(t)] = E[X(s) X(s - \tau)] = R_X(-\tau)$$ where we used commutativity of multiplication, substituted $s = t + \tau$, and applied the WSS property. **Proof of Maximum at Origin:** Consider $E[\{X(t) - X(t + \tau)\}^2] \geq 0$. Expanding: $$R_X(0) - 2 R_X(\tau) + R_X(0) \geq 0 \implies R_X(0) \geq R_X(\tau)$$ A similar argument with $X(t) + X(t+\tau)$ gives $R_X(0) \geq -R_X(\tau)$, so $\vert R_X(\tau) \vert \leq R_X(0)$. **(b) Autocorrelation of Sinusoidal Process:** $$R_X(\tau) = E[A^2 \cos(\omega_0 t + \Theta) \cos(\omega_0 t + \omega_0 \tau + \Theta)]$$ $$= \frac{A^2}{2} E[\cos(\omega_0 \tau) + \cos(2\omega_0 t + \omega_0 \tau + 2\Theta)]$$ $$= \frac{A^2}{2} \cos(\omega_0 \tau) + \frac{A^2}{2} \underbrace{E[\cos(2\omega_0 t + \omega_0 \tau + 2\Theta)]}_{= \, 0}$$ $$\boxed{R_X(\tau) = \frac{A^2}{2} \cos(\omega_0 \tau)}$$ **Periodicity:** $R_X(\tau + T_0) = \frac{A^2}{2} \cos(\omega_0 (\tau + T_0)) = \frac{A^2}{2} \cos(\omega_0 \tau) = R_X(\tau)$ for $T_0 = 2\pi/\omega_0$. Hence $R_X(\tau)$ is **periodic** with the same period as $X(t)$. **Asymptotic Behavior:** Since $R_X(\tau)$ is periodic, the ordinary limit $\lim_{\tau \to \infty} R_X(\tau)$ does **not exist** in the usual sense. The function continues to oscillate between $-A^2/2$ and $+A^2/2$ for all $\tau$. This is because the process $X(t)$ contains a **periodic component** (the sinusoid), violating the condition required for the asymptotic limit $\mu^2$. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 2] — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** > **(a)** Define autocovariance function. How is it related to the autocorrelation function? A WSS process $X(t)$ has mean $\mu_X = 2$ and autocorrelation $R_X(\tau) = 5 + 4 e^{-2\vert\tau\vert}$. Find the variance of $X(t)$. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Two jointly WSS random processes $X(t)$ and $Y(t)$ are related by $Y(t) = a X(t) + b$, where $a, b$ are constants. Find the cross-correlation $R_{XY}(\tau)$ in terms of $R_X(\tau)$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Autocovariance, Relation to Autocorrelation, and Variance:** **Definition:** The autocovariance function is: $$C_X(\tau) = E[\{X(t) - \mu_X\}\{X(t + \tau) - \mu_X\}]$$ **Relation:** $$C_X(\tau) = R_X(\tau) - \mu_X^2$$ **Variance Calculation:** Given $\mu_X = 2$ and $R_X(\tau) = 5 + 4 e^{-2\vert\tau\vert}$: $$C_X(0) = R_X(0) - \mu_X^2 = (5 + 4 \cdot 1) - 2^2 = 9 - 4 = 5$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_X^2 = C_X(0) = 5}$$ **Verification of Even Symmetry:** $R_X(-\tau) = 5 + 4 e^{-2\vert-\tau\vert} = 5 + 4 e^{-2\vert\tau\vert} = R_X(\tau)$ ✓ **(b) Cross-Correlation of Linearly Related Processes:** $$R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(t) Y(t + \tau)] = E[X(t) \cdot \{a X(t + \tau) + b\}]$$ $$= a E[X(t) X(t + \tau)] + b E[X(t)]$$ $$= a R_X(\tau) + b \mu_X$$ If $\mu_X = 0$ (zero-mean case), then: $$\boxed{R_{XY}(\tau) = a R_X(\tau)}$$ In general, with a non-zero mean: $$R_{XY}(\tau) = a R_X(\tau) + b \mu_X$$ **Sanity Check at $\tau = 0$:** $R_{XY}(0) = E[X(t)Y(t)] = E[X(t)(aX(t) + b)] = a R_X(0) + b \mu_X$ ✓ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting the WSS assumption** (–2 marks): Many derivations fail without the stationarity assumption. Always state "Since $\{X(t)\}$ is WSS, the mean is constant and $R_X(t_1, t_2) = R_X(\tau)$" before using these properties. > > 2. **Confusing $R_X(0)$ with the variance** (–2 marks): $R_X(0) = E[X^2(t)]$ is the **mean-square value**, not the variance. The variance is $\sigma_X^2 = R_X(0) - \mu_X^2$. > > 3. **Applying $\lim_{\tau \to \infty} R_X(\tau) = \mu_X^2$ to periodic processes** (–3 marks): This asymptotic property is **only valid for processes WITHOUT periodic components**. For a process like $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \Theta)$, the autocorrelation oscillates and the limit is undefined. > > 4. **Forgetting the constant $\frac{1}{2}$ in the sinusoidal autocorrelation** (–2 marks): The factor $A^2/2$ (not $A^2$) appears in $R_X(\tau)$ for a sinusoid with random phase. > > 5. **Incorrect sign in cross-correlation symmetry** (–1 mark): It is $R_{XY}(-\tau) = R_{YX}(\tau)$, NOT $R_{XY}(-\tau) = R_{XY}(\tau)$ (the latter is the autocorrelation symmetry). > > 6. **Mixing up the order of arguments** (–1 mark): $R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(t)Y(t+\tau)]$, not $E[X(t+\tau)Y(t)]$ — these are **different** functions (related by $R_{XY}(-\tau)$). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** > > - **Definition:** $R_X(\tau) = E[X(t)X(t+\tau)]$ for a WSS process. Depends only on lag $\tau$. > - **$R_X(0) = E[X^2(t)]$** = Average power of the process. > - **$C_X(\tau) = R_X(\tau) - \mu_X^2$**, with $C_X(0) = \sigma_X^2$ (variance). > - **Even function:** $R_X(\tau) = R_X(-\tau)$ — proof by symmetry of expectation. > - **Maximum at origin:** $R_X(0) \geq R_X(\tau)$, proved by non-negativity of $E[\{X(t) - X(t+\tau)\}^2]$. > - **Bounded by origin value:** $\vert R_X(\tau) \vert \leq R_X(0)$. > - **Asymptotic limit:** $\lim_{\vert\tau\vert \to \infty} R_X(\tau) = \mu_X^2$ **only if no periodic component exists**. > - **Periodic processes have periodic autocorrelation** — never converges to a single asymptotic value. > - **White noise** $R_X(\tau) = \sigma^2 \delta(\tau)$ — impulsive, uncorrelated. > - **Random binary wave** $R_X(\tau) = A^2 \max(1 - 2\vert\tau\vert/T, 0)$ — triangular. > - **Sinusoidal process** $R_X(\tau) = \frac{A^2}{2}\cos(\omega_0\tau)$ — purely oscillatory. > - **Cross-correlation:** $R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(t)Y(t+\tau)]$; satisfies $R_{XY}(-\tau) = R_{YX}(\tau)$. > - **Uncorrelated processes:** $R_{XY}(\tau) = \mu_X \mu_Y$ for all $\tau$. > - **Wiener–Khinchin theorem:** $S_X(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{R_X(\tau)\}$ — the bridge to PSD. > - **WSS** $\Rightarrow$ Mean is constant AND autocorrelation depends only on $\tau$. > - **SSS** $\Rightarrow$ WSS, but the converse is generally **not** true.

topics.title: Ergodicity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE – 4
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ergodicity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous framework of **stochastic processes** (random processes), a random process $X(t)$ is said to be **Ergodic** if its **time averages** (computed along a single sample function over an infinite observation interval) are equal (in some statistical sense) to its **corresponding ensemble averages** (computed across all sample functions at a fixed instant of time).

Mathematically, a process is called **ergodic** when the statistical properties (mean, variance, autocorrelation, probability distribution) determined from **one infinitely long realization** of the process are identical to the statistical properties determined by averaging **across the entire ensemble** of all possible realizations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A random process is said to be **ergodic** if the time averages of a single sample function equal the corresponding ensemble averages of the process. Ergodicity is a stronger condition than **stationarity**. A process must be stationary to be ergodic, but a stationary process is not necessarily ergodic.

The four canonical forms of ergodicity studied in this module are:

| Ergodicity Type | Property Equated | Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Mean Ergodic | Time Avg. of $X(t)$ = Ensemble Mean | $\langle X(t) \rangle_t = E[X(t)]$ |
| Autocorrelation Ergodic | Time Avg. of $X(t)X(t+\tau)$ = Ensemble Autocorrelation | $\langle X(t)X(t+\tau) \rangle_t = R_X(\tau)$ |
| Variance Ergodic | Time Avg. of $[X(t)]^2$ = Ensemble MS Value | $\langle X^2(t) \rangle_t = E[X^2(t)]$ |
| Distribution Ergodic | All Statistics match | Full statistical equivalence |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Single Song vs. Full Album" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Music Album Test**
> 
> Imagine a music album with 1,000,000 songs (this is our **ensemble** of sample functions). 
> 
> - **Ensemble Average**: Statistics computed by sampling the *same instant* across all 1,000,000 songs simultaneously.
> - **Time Average**: Statistics computed by listening to *one single song* from start to finish and analyzing its behavior over time.
> 
> **Stationarity** = the album's *average tempo and loudness* doesn't depend on which 5-second window you sample — the statistics are time-invariant.
> 
> **Ergodicity** = If you pick *just one song* and let it play forever, the long-term average of that single song (its time average) perfectly matches the average of all 1,000,000 songs played at the same instant (its ensemble average). 
> 
> In plain English: **Ergodicity means "one sample function tells you everything about the whole process"** — provided you observe it long enough.

## 1.3 Why Ergodicity Matters in Electrical Engineering

In practice, an electrical engineer almost never has access to the **entire ensemble** of a random signal. For example:
- We cannot generate the *infinite* family of noise waveforms that a resistor produces. We only have **one** noise waveform at a time.
- We cannot run the *infinite* ensemble of communication channel responses. We only have **one** observed channel output.

Ergodicity is the **mathematical justification** that lets us replace expensive, often impossible, ensemble measurements with cheap, single-trace time measurements. **All practical instruments** (spectrum analyzers, power meters, RMS voltmeters) implicitly assume the underlying signal is **ergodic**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Pre-requisite: Stationarity Recap

A random process $X(t)$ is **Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS)** if:
- The mean $E[X(t)] = \mu_X$ is a **constant** (independent of $t$).
- The autocorrelation $R_X(t_1, t_2) = R_X(\tau)$ depends **only on the time difference** $\tau = t_2 - t_1$.

A process is **Strict-Sense Stationary (SSS)** if **all** its finite-order joint statistics are time-invariant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Strength:** SSS $\Rightarrow$ WSS, but WSS does NOT imply SSS.
> **Ergodicity Hierarchy:** SSS + additional conditions $\Rightarrow$ Ergodicity.

## 2.2 Time Average vs. Ensemble Average — The Core Distinction

**Ensemble Average (Statistical Mean):**
$$\langle X(t) \rangle_E = E[X(t)] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, f_{X(t)}(x) \, dx$$

This is a **spatial average** across all sample functions at instant $t$.

**Time Average (Temporal Mean):**
$$\langle X(t) \rangle_t = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} X(t) \, dt$$

This is a **temporal average** along one sample function $X(t)$ over infinite time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Ergodic Hypothesis (Mean Ergodic):**
> A stationary process is **mean-ergodic** if:
> $$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} X(t) \, dt = E[X(t)] = \mu_X \quad \text{(with probability 1)}$$

## 2.3 The Four Types of Ergodic Processes

### 2.3.1 Mean-Ergodic Process
The time average equals the ensemble mean for almost every sample function.

**Condition (Birkhoff-Khinchin Mean Ergodic Theorem):** A stationary process $X(t)$ is mean-ergodic if and only if:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} \left(1 - \frac{\vert \tau \vert}{2T}\right) C_X(\tau) \, d\tau = 0$$

where $C_X(\tau) = R_X(\tau) - \mu_X^2$ is the **autocovariance** function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** If the autocovariance $C_X(\tau) \to 0$ as $\tau \to \infty$ (the process "forgets" its past — a property called **asymptotic uncorrelatedness**), then the time average converges to the ensemble mean. This is the standard sufficient condition tested in KTU.

### 2.3.2 Autocorrelation-Ergodic Process
The time-averaged autocorrelation equals the ensemble autocorrelation.

**Definition:**
$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} X(t) X(t + \tau) \, dt = R_X(\tau) \quad \text{(with prob. 1)}$$

**Condition (Autocorrelation Ergodic Theorem):** A stationary process is autocorrelation-ergodic if:
$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} \left(1 - \frac{\vert u \vert}{2T}\right) \left[ R_{XX}(u) - R_X^2(\tau) \right] du = 0$$

where $R_{XX}(u) = E[X(t)X(t+u)X(t+\tau)X(t+\tau+u)]$ is the **fourth-order moment**.

### 2.3.3 Distribution-Ergodic (Strictly Ergodic) Process
The empirical CDF of a single realization converges to the true CDF of the process for **every** value of $x$:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \mathbf{1}_{\{X(t) \le x\}} \, dt = F_X(x) = P(X(t) \le x)$$

This is the **strongest** form of ergodicity and implies all the others.

### 2.3.4 Variance-Ergodic Process
Time average of $[X(t) - \langle X \rangle_t]^2$ equals ensemble variance $\sigma_X^2$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — KTU 2024 Module 4: Ergodicity**

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ensemble Mean | $E[X(t)] = \mu_X$ | Constant for stationary processes |
| 2 | Time Average | $\langle X \rangle_t = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} X(t) \, dt$ | Single realization, infinite horizon |
| 3 | Ensemble Autocorrelation | $R_X(\tau) = E[X(t) X(t+\tau)]$ | Depends only on $\tau$ for WSS |
| 4 | Time Autocorrelation | $\langle X(t)X(t+\tau) \rangle_t = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} X(t)X(t+\tau) \, dt$ | For one sample function |
| 5 | Autocovariance | $C_X(\tau) = R_X(\tau) - \mu_X^2$ | $C_X(0) = \sigma_X^2$ |
| 6 | Mean-Ergodic Condition | $\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} (1 - \frac{\vert \tau \vert}{2T}) C_X(\tau) \, d\tau = 0$ | Sufficient: $C_X(\tau) \to 0$ as $\vert \tau \vert \to \infty$ |
| 7 | Variance of Time Mean | $\text{Var}[\langle X \rangle_t] = \frac{1}{4T^2} \int_{-2T}^{2T} (2T - \vert u \vert) C_X(u) \, du$ | Must $\to 0$ for ergodicity |
| 8 | Ergodic Power (MS Value) | $\langle X^2 \rangle_t = R_X(0) = E[X^2(t)]$ | Used in power spectral density |
| 9 | Power Spectral Density | $S_X(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_X(\tau)\}$ | Wiener-Khinchin theorem |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU TRICK:** The sufficient condition for mean ergodicity — $\lim_{\tau \to \infty} C_X(\tau) = 0$ — is the single most tested concept. If the autocovariance decays to zero at infinity, the process is mean-ergodic (provided it is also stationary).

## 2.5 Practical Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Applications:**
> 
> 1. **Power Spectral Density Estimation:** Real spectrum analyzers estimate $S_X(f)$ by computing time-averaged $|X(f)|^2$ from a single long recording — only valid for **ergodic** signals.
> 2. **Thermal Noise Analysis (Johnson-Nyquist Noise):** Resistor noise $X(t)$ is modeled as a WSS Gaussian process. Ergodicity lets a single sample function give us the noise power $N = kTB$.
> 3. **Communication Channel Modeling:** Channel impulse response $h(t)$ is often modeled as an ergodic random process, enabling single-channel long-term measurement.
> 4. **Biomedical Signals (EEG, ECG):** Ergodicity allows long single-trace recordings to represent the entire patient population's statistical behavior.
> 5. **Reliability Engineering:** Failure times of identical components, if ergodic, can be inferred from a single component's long-term test.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Mean Ergodic Theorem (Birkhoff-Khinchin)

**Statement:** A stationary process $X(t)$ with mean $\mu_X$ and autocovariance $C_X(\tau)$ is mean-ergodic if and only if:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \text{Var}\left[ \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} X(t) \, dt \right] = 0$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Define the time-mean estimator for finite $T$:

$$\hat{\mu}_T = \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} X(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Compute the variance of $\hat{\mu}_T$. For a stationary process, $E[\hat{\mu}_T] = \mu_X$ (unbiased estimator). So:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] = E[\hat{\mu}_T^2] - \mu_X^2$$

**Step 3:** Expand $E[\hat{\mu}_T^2]$:

$$E[\hat{\mu}_T^2] = \frac{1}{4T^2} E\left[ \int_{-T}^{T} X(t_1) \, dt_1 \int_{-T}^{T} X(t_2) \, dt_2 \right]$$

**Step 4:** Swap expectation and integration (Fubini's theorem, valid since the integral converges):

$$E[\hat{\mu}_T^2] = \frac{1}{4T^2} \int_{-T}^{T} \int_{-T}^{T} E[X(t_1) X(t_2)] \, dt_1 \, dt_2$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the autocorrelation $R_X(t_1, t_2) = R_X(t_2 - t_1)$ (using stationarity) and let $u = t_2 - t_1$:

$$E[\hat{\mu}_T^2] = \frac{1}{4T^2} \int_{-T}^{T} \int_{-T}^{T} R_X(t_2 - t_1) \, dt_1 \, dt_2$$

**Step 6:** Apply the change of variables to convert to $u$ and $t_1$:

$$E[\hat{\mu}_T^2] = \frac{1}{4T^2} \int_{-2T}^{2T} R_X(u) \int_{-(T - \vert u \vert / 2)}^{T - \vert u \vert / 2} dt_1 \, du$$

**Step 7:** Evaluate the inner integral. The length of the $t_1$-interval is $(2T - \vert u \vert)$ for $\vert u \vert \le 2T$:

$$E[\hat{\mu}_T^2] = \frac{1}{4T^2} \int_{-2T}^{2T} (2T - \vert u \vert) R_X(u) \, du$$

**Step 8:** Subtract $\mu_X^2$ and use $C_X(u) = R_X(u) - \mu_X^2$:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] = \frac{1}{4T^2} \int_{-2T}^{2T} (2T - \vert u \vert) C_X(u) \, du$$

**Step 9:** Rewrite using the triangular weighting function $(1 - \frac{\vert u \vert}{2T})$:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] = \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} \left( 1 - \frac{\vert u \vert}{2T} \right) C_X(u) \, du$$

**Step 10:** The **Mean Ergodic Theorem** states ergodicity holds iff this variance $\to 0$ as $T \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} \left( 1 - \frac{\vert u \vert}{2T} \right) C_X(u) \, du = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sufficient (and most-tested) condition:** If $\lim_{\tau \to \infty} C_X(\tau) = 0$, the integral's integrand becomes small for large $\tau$, and the triangular window $(1 - \frac{\vert u \vert}{2T})$ forces the entire integral to zero as $T \to \infty$.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Mean-Ergodicity Test of a Specific Process

**Problem:** A WSS random process $X(t)$ has autocovariance $C_X(\tau) = 4 e^{-3\vert \tau \vert}$. Determine whether $X(t)$ is mean-ergodic.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Check the limit of $C_X(\tau)$ as $\vert \tau \vert \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{\vert \tau \vert \to \infty} C_X(\tau) = \lim_{\vert \tau \vert \to \infty} 4 e^{-3\vert \tau \vert} = 0$$

The autocovariance decays to zero at infinity. ✓

**Step 2:** Compute the variance of the time mean using the Mean Ergodic Theorem formula:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] = \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} \left( 1 - \frac{\vert u \vert}{2T} \right) \cdot 4 e^{-3\vert u \vert} \, du$$

**Step 3:** Use the even symmetry of the integrand (both $(1 - \frac{\vert u \vert}{2T})$ and $e^{-3\vert u \vert}$ are even functions):

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] = \frac{1}{2T} \cdot 2 \int_{0}^{2T} \left( 1 - \frac{u}{2T} \right) 4 e^{-3u} \, du = \frac{4}{T} \int_{0}^{2T} \left( 1 - \frac{u}{2T} \right) e^{-3u} \, du$$

**Step 4:** Split the integral:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] = \frac{4}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{2T} e^{-3u} \, du - \frac{1}{2T} \int_{0}^{2T} u \, e^{-3u} \, du \right]$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the first integral:

$$\int_{0}^{2T} e^{-3u} \, du = \left[ -\frac{1}{3} e^{-3u} \right]_{0}^{2T} = \frac{1}{3} \left( 1 - e^{-6T} \right)$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the second integral using integration by parts ($\int u e^{-3u} du$, let $v = u$, $dw = e^{-3u} du$):

$$\int u e^{-3u} \, du = -\frac{u}{3} e^{-3u} - \frac{1}{9} e^{-3u} + C = -\frac{e^{-3u}}{9} (3u + 1) + C$$

Evaluate from $0$ to $2T$:

$$\int_{0}^{2T} u e^{-3u} \, du = -\frac{e^{-6T}}{9} (6T + 1) + \frac{1}{9} = \frac{1}{9} \left[ 1 - e^{-6T}(6T + 1) \right]$$

**Step 7:** Combine and simplify:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] = \frac{4}{T} \left[ \frac{1 - e^{-6T}}{3} - \frac{1 - e^{-6T}(6T + 1)}{18T} \right]$$

**Step 8:** Take the limit as $T \to \infty$. Since $e^{-6T} \to 0$:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{4}{T} \left[ \frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{18T} \right] = \lim_{T \to \infty} \left[ \frac{4}{3T} - \frac{4}{18T^2} \right] = 0$$

**Step 9:** Conclusion: Since the variance of the time-mean estimator tends to zero, the process $X(t)$ **is mean-ergodic**. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The key inference path is: *Stationarity* (given) $\rightarrow$ Check $\lim C_X(\tau) = 0$ (yes) $\rightarrow$ Compute or bound $\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] \to 0$ (yes) $\rightarrow$ Declare mean-ergodic. You do not need to evaluate the full integral if you can show the bound is $O(1/T)$.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Non-Ergodic Stationary Process

**Problem:** Consider a random process $X(t) = A$ where $A$ is a random variable uniformly distributed on $[-1, 1]$. Determine if $X(t)$ is mean-ergodic.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Find the ensemble mean. For each sample function, $X(t) = A$ (constant in time), so $E[X(t)] = E[A] = 0$ (since $A$ is uniform on $[-1,1]$).

**Step 2:** Find the time average. For a single sample function with constant $A = a$:

$$\langle X(t) \rangle_t = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} a \, dt = a$$

**Step 3:** Compare. The time average $a$ depends on **which sample function** we picked. The ensemble average is always $0$. The two are **equal only for the sample function with $A = 0$** — a zero-probability event.

**Step 4:** Conclusion: $X(t)$ is **stationary** (mean is constant $0$, autocorrelation $R_X(\tau) = E[A^2] = 1/3$, independent of $\tau$) but **NOT mean-ergodic**.

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the classic KTU counter-example.** A process can be perfectly stationary (even SSS!) and still not be ergodic. Ergodicity requires that the process *visits* all its statistical states over a single long realization — the constant $A = a$ never changes, so it can never "average out" to the true ensemble mean.
