# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q:** Define **mean-ergodic process**. State the necessary and sufficient condition for a wide-sense stationary process to be mean-ergodic.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** A wide-sense stationary (WSS) random process $X(t)$ is said to be **mean-ergodic** if the time average of any sample function $X_i(t)$ equals the ensemble mean of the process, with probability 1. That is:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} X_i(t) \, dt = E[X(t)] = \mu_X \quad \text{(with prob. 1)}$$

**Condition (1.5 Marks):** A WSS process is mean-ergodic if and only if:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} \left( 1 - \frac{\vert \tau \vert}{2T} \right) C_X(\tau) \, d\tau = 0$$

where $C_X(\tau) = R_X(\tau) - \mu_X^2$ is the autocovariance. A sufficient condition (commonly tested) is:

$$\lim_{\vert \tau \vert \to \infty} C_X(\tau) = 0$$

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q:** Distinguish between **stationarity** and **ergodicity** of a random process. Give one example of a stationary but non-ergodic process.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Distinction (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | Stationarity | Ergodicity |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Statistics are time-invariant | Time avg. = Ensemble avg. (for one sample) |
| **Reference Set** | Ensemble of all sample functions | One sample function observed for long time |
| **Strength** | Weaker condition | Stronger condition |
| **Implication** | Independent of time origin | Time avg. independent of chosen sample |

Stationarity says statistics don't depend on the time origin. Ergodicity says a single sample function, observed long enough, gives the same statistics as the entire ensemble.

**Example (1 Mark):** $X(t) = A$, where $A$ is a random variable with mean $\mu_A$ and variance $\sigma_A^2$.
- **Stationary:** Yes, since $E[X(t)] = \mu_A$ (constant) and $R_X(t_1, t_2) = E[A^2]$ (depends only on $\tau = 0$).
- **Not ergodic:** The time average of any sample function is the constant $A = a$, which equals $\mu_A$ only by chance.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Solutions must show every derivation step.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define an **autocorrelation-ergodic process**. A WSS process $X(t)$ has the autocorrelation function $R_X(\tau) = 9 + 4 e^{-2 \vert \tau \vert}$. Determine whether $X(t)$ is **mean-ergodic** and **autocorrelation-ergodic**. Justify your answer by computing the relevant limits.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a stationary process with mean $\mu_X = 3$ and autocovariance $C_X(\tau) = 5 e^{-4 \vert \tau \vert}$, derive the variance of the time-mean estimator $\hat{\mu}_T$ and prove that the process is mean-ergodic by showing $\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] \to 0$ as $T \to \infty$.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

##### Part (a) — Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 Mark): Definition of Autocorrelation-Ergodic Process**

A WSS process $X(t)$ is **autocorrelation-ergodic** if for almost every sample function $X_i(t)$:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} X_i(t) X_i(t + \tau) \, dt = R_X(\tau) \quad \text{(with prob. 1)}$$

That is, the time-averaged autocorrelation of a single sample function equals the ensemble autocorrelation, for all values of $\tau$.

**Step 2 (1 Mark): Extract Mean and Autocovariance from Given $R_X(\tau)$**

Given $R_X(\tau) = 9 + 4 e^{-2 \vert \tau \vert}$.

The mean squared is $\mu_X^2 = R_X(0) = 9 + 4 = 13$, so $\mu_X = \pm \sqrt{13}$.

The autocovariance is $C_X(\tau) = R_X(\tau) - \mu_X^2 = 9 + 4 e^{-2 \vert \tau \vert} - 13 = 4 e^{-2 \vert \tau \vert} - 4$.

**Step 3 (2 Marks): Mean-Ergodicity Test**

Apply the sufficient condition $\lim_{\vert \tau \vert \to \infty} C_X(\tau) = 0$:

$$\lim_{\vert \tau \vert \to \infty} C_X(\tau) = \lim_{\vert \tau \vert \to \infty} \left( 4 e^{-2 \vert \tau \vert} - 4 \right) = 0 - 4 = -4 \neq 0$$

The autocovariance does **not** decay to zero! Therefore, $X(t)$ is **NOT mean-ergodic**.

**Step 4 (2 Marks): Autocorrelation-Ergodicity Test**

For autocorrelation ergodicity, we need $\lim_{\tau \to \infty} R_X(\tau) = \mu_X^2 = 9$:

$$\lim_{\vert \tau \vert \to \infty} R_X(\tau) = \lim_{\vert \tau \vert \to \infty} \left( 9 + 4 e^{-2 \vert \tau \vert} \right) = 9 + 0 = 9 = \mu_X^2 \quad \checkmark$$

Since $R_X(\tau) \to \mu_X^2$ as $\vert \tau \vert \to \infty$, the process **IS autocorrelation-ergodic** (under the loose sufficient condition).

**Step 5 (1 Mark): Conclusion**

$X(t)$ is **not mean-ergodic** (because $C_X(\tau) \not\to 0$) but **autocorrelation-ergodic** (because $R_X(\tau) \to \mu_X^2$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse the two limits. Mean ergodicity needs $C_X(\tau) \to 0$; autocorrelation ergodicity needs $R_X(\tau) \to \mu_X^2$. They are equivalent when $\mu_X^2 = 0$ (zero-mean processes) but can differ when $\mu_X \neq 0$. Read the question carefully — "mean ergodic" and "autocorrelation ergodic" are different tests.

---

##### Part (b) — Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 Mark): Set Up the Time-Mean Estimator**

For a finite observation window of length $2T$:

$$\hat{\mu}_T = \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} X(t) \, dt$$

Given: $\mu_X = 3$, $C_X(\tau) = 5 e^{-4 \vert \tau \vert}$.

**Step 2 (1 Mark): State the Variance Formula**

For a stationary process, the variance of the time-mean estimator is:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] = E[\hat{\mu}_T^2] - \mu_X^2 = \frac{1}{4T^2} \int_{-2T}^{2T} (2T - \vert u \vert) R_X(u) \, du - \mu_X^2$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks): Substitute $R_X(u) = C_X(u) + \mu_X^2$**

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] = \frac{1}{4T^2} \int_{-2T}^{2T} (2T - \vert u \vert) [C_X(u) + \mu_X^2] \, du - \mu_X^2$$

$$= \frac{1}{4T^2} \int_{-2T}^{2T} (2T - \vert u \vert) C_X(u) \, du + \frac{\mu_X^2}{4T^2} \int_{-2T}^{2T} (2T - \vert u \vert) \, du - \mu_X^2$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark): Show the $\mu_X^2$ Term Cancels**

Compute $\int_{-2T}^{2T} (2T - \vert u \vert) du$ using even symmetry:

$$\int_{-2T}^{2T} (2T - \vert u \vert) du = 2 \int_{0}^{2T} (2T - u) du = 2 \left[ 2Tu - \frac{u^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{2T} = 2 \cdot 2T^2 = 4T^2$$

Therefore, the $\mu_X^2$ term becomes $\frac{\mu_X^2}{4T^2} \cdot 4T^2 = \mu_X^2$, which exactly cancels the $-\mu_X^2$:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] = \frac{1}{4T^2} \int_{-2T}^{2T} (2T - \vert u \vert) C_X(u) \, du$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark): Substitute the Given $C_X(u)$**

Since $C_X(u) = 5 e^{-4 \vert u \vert}$ is even:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] = \frac{1}{4T^2} \cdot 2 \int_{0}^{2T} (2T - u) \cdot 5 e^{-4u} \, du = \frac{5}{2T^2} \int_{0}^{2T} (2T - u) e^{-4u} \, du$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark): Evaluate the Integral**

$$I = \int_{0}^{2T} (2T - u) e^{-4u} \, du = 2T \int_{0}^{2T} e^{-4u} \, du - \int_{0}^{2T} u e^{-4u} \, du$$

First piece: $\int_{0}^{2T} e^{-4u} du = \frac{1}{4}(1 - e^{-8T})$.

Second piece: using integration by parts ($\int u e^{-4u} du = -\frac{e^{-4u}}{16}(4u + 1)$):

$$\int_{0}^{2T} u e^{-4u} du = \frac{1 - e^{-8T}(8T + 1)}{16}$$

Therefore:

$$I = 2T \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-8T}}{4} - \frac{1 - e^{-8T}(8T + 1)}{16} = \frac{2T(1 - e^{-8T})}{4} - \frac{1 - e^{-8T}(8T + 1)}{16}$$

As $T \to \infty$, $e^{-8T} \to 0$, so:

$$I \to \frac{2T}{4} - \frac{1}{16} = \frac{T}{2} - \frac{1}{16}$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark): Take the Limit as $T \to \infty$**

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] = \frac{5}{2T^2} \cdot \left( \frac{T}{2} - \frac{1}{16} \right) = \frac{5}{4T} - \frac{5}{32T^2}$$

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] = \lim_{T \to \infty} \left( \frac{5}{4T} - \frac{5}{32T^2} \right) = 0 - 0 = 0$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] \to 0$ as $T \to \infty$, the time-mean estimator converges in mean-square to $\mu_X$, and the process is **mean-ergodic**. ∎

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **strict-sense ergodic** (distribution-ergodic) process. Show, with a counter-example, that a strictly stationary process need not be ergodic. Compute the time average and ensemble average for the counter-example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A stationary Gaussian process $X(t)$ has zero mean and autocovariance $C_X(\tau) = \sigma^2 e^{-\alpha \vert \tau \vert}$, where $\sigma = 2$ and $\alpha = 5$. Verify that this process is **mean-ergodic** and **autocorrelation-ergodic**. State the practical engineering significance of your conclusion.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

##### Part (a) — Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (2 Marks): Definition of Distribution-Ergodic Process**

A WSS random process $X(t)$ is said to be **distribution-ergodic** (or **strictly ergodic**) if, for **every** value of $x \in \mathbb{R}$, the proportion of time that $X_i(t) \le x$ along a single sample function $X_i(t)$ converges (as $T \to \infty$) to the true CDF $F_X(x)$ of the process. Formally:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \mathbf{1}_{\{X_i(t) \le x\}} \, dt = P(X(t) \le x) = F_X(x) \quad \text{(with prob. 1)}$$

where $\mathbf{1}_{\{\cdot\}}$ is the indicator function.

**Step 2 (2 Marks): Counter-Example Setup**

Consider the random process:

$$X(t) = A \cdot \cos(\omega_0 t + \Theta)$$

where $A$ is a random variable uniformly distributed on $[0, 1]$ (independent of $\Theta$), $\Theta \sim U(0, 2\pi)$, and $\omega_0$ is a constant.

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks): Compute Ensemble Average**

$$E[X(t)] = E[A] \cdot E[\cos(\omega_0 t + \Theta)] = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0 = 0$$

(using $E[A] = 1/2$ and $E[\cos(\omega_0 t + \Theta)] = 0$ for uniform $\Theta$).

**Step 4 (1.5 Marks): Compute Time Average**

For a single realization with fixed $A = a$ and $\Theta = \theta$:

$$\langle X(t) \rangle_t = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} a \cos(\omega_0 t + \theta) \, dt = 0$$

(by the integral property of cosine over a full period). So time average = $0$ = ensemble mean here.

**Step 5 (Bonus Insight — why is it still NOT distribution-ergodic):** Although the mean matches, the **distribution** does not match. For each fixed $A = a$, $X(t) = a \cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)$ takes values in $[-a, a]$, while the ensemble distribution of $X(t)$ at any instant $t$ is supported on $[-1, 1]$ (since $A$ can be up to $1$). So the time-spent statistic $\neq F_X(x)$ for $x > a$.

**Conclusion:** A stationary process can be **mean-ergodic** (averages match) but **NOT distribution-ergodic** (the full distribution differs). Therefore stationarity alone is insufficient for ergodicity. ∎

##### Part (b) — Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 Mark): Identify the Given Parameters**

$X(t)$ is stationary Gaussian, $E[X(t)] = 0$, $C_X(\tau) = \sigma^2 e^{-\alpha \vert \tau \vert}$ with $\sigma = 2$, $\alpha = 5$.

So $C_X(\tau) = 4 e^{-5 \vert \tau \vert}$.

**Step 2 (1.5 Marks): Mean-Ergodicity Test**

Check the sufficient condition:

$$\lim_{\vert \tau \vert \to \infty} C_X(\tau) = \lim_{\vert \tau \vert \to \infty} 4 e^{-5 \vert \tau \vert} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

The autocovariance decays exponentially to zero. The process is **mean-ergodic**.

**Step 3 (2 Marks): Compute the Variance of Time-Mean (Verification)**

$$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] = \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} \left(1 - \frac{\vert u \vert}{2T}\right) 4 e^{-5 \vert u \vert} du = \frac{4}{T} \int_0^{2T} \left(1 - \frac{u}{2T}\right) e^{-5u} du$$

Bounding: $\int_0^{2T} e^{-5u} du \le \int_0^{\infty} e^{-5u} du = \frac{1}{5}$ and $\int_0^{2T} u e^{-5u} du \le \frac{1}{25}$. So:

$$0 \le \text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] \le \frac{4}{T} \left[ \frac{1}{5} + \frac{1}{50T} \right] = \frac{4}{5T} + \frac{4}{50T^2} \to 0$$

as $T \to \infty$. Confirmed: **mean-ergodic**. ✓

**Step 4 (1.5 Marks): Autocorrelation-Ergodicity Test**

For a **Gaussian** process, all higher-order moments are determined by the second-order moments. Hence the autocorrelation ergodic condition reduces to checking the fourth-order moment, which for a Gaussian process:

$$E[X(t) X(t+u) X(t+\tau) X(t+\tau+u)] = R_X^2(u) + R_X(\tau) R_X(\tau) = R_X^2(u) + R_X^2(\tau)$$

Since $R_X(\tau) = C_X(\tau) + \mu_X^2 = 4 e^{-5 \vert \tau \vert} \to 0$ as $\vert \tau \vert \to \infty$, the autocorrelation ergodic condition is satisfied. The process is **autocorrelation-ergodic**. ✓

**Step 5 (1 Mark): Engineering Significance**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Engineering Significance:**
> 1. A single recording of length $\ge 10/\alpha = 2$ seconds of this signal is sufficient to estimate $\mu_X$ and $R_X(\tau)$ to within $1\%$ accuracy.
> 2. **Power spectral density** $S_X(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_X(\tau)\}$ can be accurately measured from a single observation using a spectrum analyzer.
> 3. The signal can be used in **noise modeling** for communication systems, where ergodicity guarantees the noise statistics inferred from one channel apply to all channels.
> 4. **Monte-Carlo simulations** of communication receivers are valid — we can replace a 1-million-receiver ensemble with a 1-receiver long simulation, saving 10$^6 \times$ computational cost.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU 2024 EXAMINER'S PITFALL CALLOUT — WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $R_X(\tau) \to 0$ with $C_X(\tau) \to 0$** (lose 1–2 marks): For zero-mean processes they are the same; for non-zero-mean processes, $C_X(\tau) = R_X(\tau) - \mu_X^2 \neq R_X(\tau)$. Mean ergodicity needs the **autocovariance** to decay to zero.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting stationarity prerequisite** (lose 1 mark): Ergodicity is **defined only for stationary processes**. If a problem doesn't explicitly state stationarity, first verify it.
> 
> 3. **Confusing the limits** (lose 2 marks): 
>    - Mean ergodic: $\lim_{\tau \to \infty} C_X(\tau) = 0$
>    - Autocorrelation ergodic: $\lim_{\tau \to \infty} [R_{XX}(u) - R_X^2(\tau)] = 0$
> 
> 4. **Sign errors in variance calculation** (lose 1 mark): $\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T]$ is **always** $\ge 0$; check sign at the end.
> 
> 5. **Not computing the integral explicitly** (lose 2 marks): KTU examiners require the **explicit computation** of $\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] \to 0$ in 14-mark problems — a verbal claim "since $C_X \to 0$, it's ergodic" is insufficient for full marks. Show the integration, even if bounded.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up time average and ensemble average symbols** (lose 1 mark): Use $\langle \cdot \rangle_t$ for time average, $E[\cdot]$ for ensemble average. Never write "$E[\hat{\mu}_T]$" as a "time average".
> 
> 7. **Wrong counter-example** (lose 1 mark): The counter-example for "stationary but not ergodic" must be **stationary** — verify $E[X(t)]$ constant and $R_X(\tau)$ time-invariant first, then show time average $\ne$ ensemble average.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 KTU 2024 — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST (Module 4: Ergodicity)**
> 
> **Core Concepts (Must Know):**
> - ☐ Ergodic process: time average = ensemble average (for almost every sample function).
> - ☐ Ergodicity **implies** stationarity, but stationarity does **NOT** imply ergodicity.
> - ☐ Four types: **mean**, **autocorrelation**, **variance**, **distribution** (strict).
> - ☐ Distribution ergodicity is the **strongest**; mean ergodicity is the **weakest**.
> 
> **Key Formulas (Must Memorize):**
> - ☐ Time mean: $\langle X \rangle_t = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} X(t) dt$
> - ☐ Ensemble mean: $E[X(t)] = \int x \, f_X(x) \, dx$
> - ☐ Mean-Ergodic condition: $\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} (1 - \frac{\vert \tau \vert}{2T}) C_X(\tau) d\tau = 0$
> - ☐ Sufficient condition: $\lim_{\vert \tau \vert \to \infty} C_X(\tau) = 0$
> - ☐ Variance of time mean: $\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_T] = \frac{1}{4T^2} \int_{-2T}^{2T} (2T - \vert u \vert) C_X(u) du$
> 
> **Critical Theorems:**
> - ☐ **Birkhoff-Khinchin Mean Ergodic Theorem** — provides necessary & sufficient condition.
> - ☐ **Wiener-Khinchin Theorem** — links $S_X(f) \leftrightarrow R_X(\tau)$.
> - ☐ **Glivenko-Cantelli Theorem** — guarantees distribution ergodicity for i.i.d. samples.
> 
> **Counter-Examples (Must Know):**
> - ☐ $X(t) = A$ (constant random variable) — stationary, not ergodic.
> - ☐ $X(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \Theta)$ with random $A$ — mean-ergodic, NOT distribution-ergodic.
> - ☐ $X(t) = Y$ (DC random) vs. $X(t) = Y$ for half the time, $-Y$ for the other half — second IS ergodic.
> 
> **Engineering Applications (Must State):**
> - ☐ Spectrum analyzer measurements assume ergodicity.
> - ☐ Single-trace noise power estimation ($N = kTB$).
> - ☐ Communication channel modeling (ergodic fading channels).
> - ☐ Biomedical signal processing (long single-trace recordings).
> 
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:**
> - ☐ Do not declare ergodicity just because a process is "stationary". Always check $C_X(\tau) \to 0$ (or compute the variance of the time mean).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mean-Ergodic Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE – 4
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mean-Ergodic Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A stochastic process $\{X(t), t \in T\}$ is called a **Mean-Ergodic Process** if its **time average** equals its **ensemble average (statistical mean)** in the mean-square sense. In other words, the long-run temporal average of a single realization of the process converges to the constant ensemble mean $\mu_X$ of the entire process.

The process $\{X(t)\}$ is said to be **mean-ergodic** in the mean-square sense if:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} E\left[\left(\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} X(t)\, dt - \mu_X\right)^{\!2}\right] = 0$$

where $\mu_X = E[X(t)]$ is the (assumed constant) ensemble mean of the process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> A stochastic process is mean-ergodic if the time average of the mean converges to the ensemble mean. The Mean-Ergodic Theorem states that a WSS process with constant mean and continuous autocovariance satisfying $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert\, d\tau < \infty$ is mean-ergodic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Voting Booth" Intuition

Imagine a country with millions of voters, each with a slightly different opinion score about a policy. The **ensemble average** is the *national average opinion* you would get by polling *all voters at one single instant* (a horizontal snapshot of the population).

Now imagine following **one single voter** and recording their opinion *every day for 50 years*. The **time average** is the long-run average of that one voter's opinions (a vertical snapshot through time).

> [!NOTE]
> **Ergodicity (in mean)** means these two averages agree. The one voter you followed behaves, *on average*, like the entire population. This is the central assumption that allows engineers to infer statistical properties of signals by observing *one* long recording, instead of needing infinitely many recordings.

This is precisely why ergodicity is the bedrock of practical signal processing: a single long recording of a random voltage, current, or noise signal can be used to estimate its statistical mean.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Metrics

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Ensemble Mean | $\mu_X$ | $E[X(t)]$ — average across all realizations at fixed $t$ |
| Time Average | $\langle X \rangle_T$ | $\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} X(t)\, dt$ — average across time of one realization |
| Autocovariance | $C_{XX}(\tau)$ | $E[(X(t+\tau)-\mu_X)(X(t)-\mu_X)]$ |
| Mean-Square Error | $\text{MSE}$ | $E[(\langle X\rangle_T - \mu_X)^2]$ |
| Wide-Sense Stationary | WSS | Process with constant mean and time-invariant autocovariance |

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mean-Ergodic Theorem — Underlying Logic

For a Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS) process $\{X(t)\}$ with constant mean $\mu_X$ and autocovariance $C_{XX}(\tau)$, the **Mean-Square Error** between the time average and the ensemble mean for a finite window of length $2T$ is given by:

$$\text{MSE}(T) = E\left[\left(\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} X(t)\, dt - \mu_X\right)^{\!2}\right] = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-2T}^{2T}\left(1 - \frac{\vert \tau \vert}{2T}\right) C_{XX}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

This is the **fundamental identity** of mean-ergodic theory. The process is mean-ergodic **if and only if** this quantity tends to zero as $T \to \infty$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Define the time average estimator:** $\hat{\mu}_T = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} X(t)\, dt$ for a single realization.
- **Step 2 — Form the bias-squared + variance decomposition:** $E[(\hat{\mu}_T - \mu_X)^2] = [\text{Bias}]^2 + \text{Variance}$. For a WSS process, bias = 0.
- **Step 3 — Reduce the double integral:** Since $X$ is WSS, the double integral collapses to a single integral involving $C_{XX}(\tau)$ and the triangular window $(1 - \vert \tau\vert/2T)$.
- **Step 4 — Apply Riemann-Lebesgue style argument:** The triangular window $(1 - \vert \tau\vert/2T) \to 1$ for any fixed $\tau$ as $T \to \infty$, but the integration range $\vert \tau \vert \le 2T$ grows. Under absolute integrability of $C_{XX}$, the integral vanishes.
- **Step 5 — Conclude mean-ergodicity:** $\lim_{T \to \infty} \text{MSE}(T) = 0 \implies$ the process is mean-ergodic.

## 2.3 Sufficient Conditions for Mean-Ergodicity

A WSS stochastic process $\{X(t)\}$ is mean-ergodic in the mean-square sense if **all** the following hold:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Condition 1 — Constant Mean:** $E[X(t)] = \mu_X$ for all $t$ (i.e., the process must be at least first-order stationary).
>
> **Condition 2 — Continuous Autocovariance:** $C_{XX}(\tau)$ is continuous at $\tau = 0$ (which is equivalent to mean-square continuity).
>
> **Condition 3 — Absolute Integrability:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert\, d\tau < \infty$.

A commonly used *simpler* sufficient condition (often used in KTU problems) is:

$$C_{XX}(\tau) \to 0 \quad \text{as} \quad \vert \tau \vert \to \infty \quad \text{(autocovariance vanishes at infinity)}$$

combined with Conditions 1 and 2.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula bank**. Memorize every row — KTU questions are direct applications of these identities.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\mu_X = E[X(t)]$ | Ensemble mean (assumed constant) |
| 2 | $\hat{\mu}_T = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} X(t)\, dt$ | Time-average estimator |
| 3 | $C_{XX}(\tau) = E[(X(t+\tau)-\mu_X)(X(t)-\mu_X)]$ | Autocovariance function |
| 4 | $R_{XX}(\tau) = E[X(t+\tau)X(t)]$ | Autocorrelation function |
| 5 | $C_{XX}(\tau) = R_{XX}(\tau) - \mu_X^2$ | Relationship between AC and ACV |
| 6 | $\text{MSE}(T) = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-2T}^{2T}\left(1-\frac{\vert \tau \vert}{2T}\right)C_{XX}(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Mean-square error of time average |
| 7 | $\lim_{T\to\infty} \text{MSE}(T) = 0$ | **Definition of mean-ergodicity** |
| 8 | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert\, d\tau < \infty$ | Sufficient condition for ergodicity |
| 9 | $\lim_{\vert \tau \vert \to \infty} C_{XX}(\tau) = 0$ | Practical sufficient condition (decorrelation) |
| 10 | $\hat{\mu}_T \xrightarrow{\text{m.s.}} \mu_X$ | Mean-square convergence shorthand |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Electrical Engineers Care:**
> - **Communication systems:** Thermal noise voltages are modeled as WSS ergodic processes; the DC offset (mean) of a noise signal is extracted by time-averaging one long recording.
> - **Power systems:** RMS voltage and current are computed from time averages assuming voltage/current waveforms are mean-ergodic.
> - **Signal processing:** Spectrum estimation via the Wiener–Khinchin theorem assumes the process is ergodic; otherwise ensemble averaging across many realizations is required.
> - **Reliability engineering:** Component failure times are modeled as ergodic renewal processes, allowing one long dataset to characterize an entire population.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Mean-Square Error of the Time Average

We will now derive, in full algebraic detail, the **fundamental identity** for the mean-square error of the time average of a WSS process. This is a **14-mark KTU favourite**.

### Statement

For a WSS process $\{X(t)\}$ with constant mean $\mu_X$ and autocovariance $C_{XX}(\tau)$, prove that

$$E\left[\left(\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} X(t)\, dt - \mu_X\right)^{\!2}\right] = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-2T}^{2T}\left(1 - \frac{\vert \tau \vert}{2T}\right) C_{XX}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

### Proof

**Step 1 — Set up the mean-square error expression.**

Let $Y_T = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} X(t)\, dt - \mu_X$. We want to compute $E[Y_T^2]$.

By linearity, $E[Y_T] = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} E[X(t)]\, dt - \mu_X = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} \mu_X\, dt - \mu_X = \mu_X - \mu_X = 0$. So $Y_T$ has zero mean, and $E[Y_T^2] = \text{Var}(Y_T)$.

**Step 2 — Expand the squared integral.**

$$E[Y_T^2] = E\left[\left(\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} [X(t) - \mu_X]\, dt\right)^{\!2}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Square the integral (product of two integrals).**

$$E[Y_T^2] = \frac{1}{(2T)^2}\, E\left[\int_{-T}^{T}\int_{-T}^{T} [X(t_1) - \mu_X][X(t_2) - \mu_X]\, dt_1\, dt_2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Interchange expectation and integration (Fubini's theorem).**

$$E[Y_T^2] = \frac{1}{(2T)^2} \int_{-T}^{T}\int_{-T}^{T} E\big[[X(t_1)-\mu_X][X(t_2)-\mu_X]\big]\, dt_1\, dt_2$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the inner expectation as the autocovariance.**

The inner expectation is exactly $C_{XX}(t_1 - t_2)$, by definition. So

$$E[Y_T^2] = \frac{1}{(2T)^2} \int_{-T}^{T}\int_{-T}^{T} C_{XX}(t_1 - t_2)\, dt_1\, dt_2$$

**Step 6 — Change of variables.** Let $\tau = t_1 - t_2$, $u = t_2$. The Jacobian is $1$, and the square region $[-T,T] \times [-T,T]$ maps to the strip $\vert \tau \vert \le 2T$ with $u$ ranging over a length $2T - \vert \tau \vert$.

$$\int_{-T}^{T}\int_{-T}^{T} C_{XX}(t_1 - t_2)\, dt_1\, dt_2 = \int_{-2T}^{2T} C_{XX}(\tau)\left(\int_{-T+\tau/2}^{T+\tau/2} du \right) d\tau$$

Wait — carefully, since $u \in [-T, T]$ intersected with $u \in [t_2 \text{ valid}]$, the inner integral's length is $2T - \vert \tau \vert$ (for $\vert \tau \vert \le 2T$, else 0). So:

$$\int_{-2T}^{2T} C_{XX}(\tau) \cdot (2T - \vert \tau \vert)\, d\tau$$

**Step 7 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$E[Y_T^2] = \frac{1}{(2T)^2} \int_{-2T}^{2T} (2T - \vert \tau \vert)\, C_{XX}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

Dividing numerator and denominator:

$$E[Y_T^2] = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-2T}^{2T} \left(1 - \frac{\vert \tau \vert}{2T}\right) C_{XX}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

This is the desired result. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection to Ergodic Theorem:** A process is mean-ergodic if and only if this integral tends to **0** as $T \to \infty$. The integrand contains the triangular window $(1 - \vert\tau\vert/2T)$ which acts as a natural "low-pass filter" on the autocovariance.

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU 14-Mark Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A WSS process $\{X(t)\}$ has mean $\mu_X = 2$ and autocovariance $C_{XX}(\tau) = 4 e^{-2\vert \tau\vert}$. Show that the process is mean-ergodic in the mean-square sense. Compute the asymptotic variance of the time average.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Verify constant mean and continuity.**

$\mu_X = 2$ is constant. $C_{XX}(\tau) = 4 e^{-2\vert \tau\vert}$ is continuous at $\tau = 0$, where $C_{XX}(0) = 4$. ✓

**Step 2 — Check absolute integrability of autocovariance.**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert\, d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} 4 e^{-2\vert \tau\vert}\, d\tau = 4 \cdot 2 \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2\tau}\, d\tau = 8 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 4 < \infty$$

✓ The autocovariance is absolutely integrable.

**Step 3 — Conclude mean-ergodicity by the Mean-Ergodic Theorem.**

Since $\{X(t)\}$ is WSS with constant mean, continuous autocovariance, and $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert\, d\tau < \infty$, the process is **mean-ergodic** in the mean-square sense.

**Step 4 — Compute the asymptotic variance.**

The asymptotic variance (limit of $\text{MSE}(T)$ as $T \to \infty$) is $0$ for a mean-ergodic process by definition. But to demonstrate, evaluate the mean-square error explicitly:

$$\text{MSE}(T) = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-2T}^{2T}\left(1 - \frac{\vert \tau \vert}{2T}\right) 4 e^{-2\vert \tau\vert}\, d\tau = \frac{4}{2T} \cdot 2\int_{0}^{2T}\left(1 - \frac{\tau}{2T}\right) e^{-2\tau}\, d\tau$$

By dominated convergence (or direct evaluation), $\lim_{T \to \infty} \text{MSE}(T) = 0$. ✓

**Conclusion:** The process is mean-ergodic, and the time average converges in mean-square to the ensemble mean $\mu_X = 2$.

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: Decision Logic for Mean-Ergodicity

The following block diagram summarizes the **algorithmic decision procedure** a KTU student should follow when examining a process for mean-ergodicity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Examine stochastic process X of t"] --> B{"Is the mean E of X of t constant in t"}
    B -- "No" --> C["Process is NOT mean-ergodic"]
    B -- "Yes" --> D{"Is the autocovariance C_XX of tau continuous at tau equals 0"}
    D -- "No" --> C
    D -- "Yes" --> E{"Does integral of C_XX from minus infinity to infinity converge absolutely"}
    E -- "No" --> F{"Does C_XX of tau tend to 0 as tau tends to infinity"}
    F -- "No" --> C
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Process IS mean-ergodic m.s."]
    E -- "Yes" --> G
    G --> H["Time average equals ensemble average"]
    H --> I["End: Conclude ergodicity in mean"]
    C --> J["End: Process is not ergodic in mean"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Mean-Ergodic Theorem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["WSS Process X of t"] --> B["Constant Mean mu_X"]
    A --> C["Autocovariance C_XX of tau"]
    B --> D["Mean Average Estimator: Time Average over 2T"]
    C --> E["Triangular Window: 1 minus tau over 2T"]
    E --> F["Integrate C_XX times window over minus 2T to 2T"]
    F --> G["Divide by 2T"]
    G --> H["MSE of T equals the integral expression"]
    H --> I{"Limit as T to infinity equals 0"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Mean-Ergodic Process Concluded"]
    I -- "No" --> K["NOT Mean-Ergodic"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Time Average vs. Ensemble Average

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph EnsembleView["ENSEMBLE AVERAGE Horizontal view at fixed t0"]
        E1["X1 of t0"]
        E2["X2 of t0"]
        E3["X3 of t0"]
        E4["X4 of t0"]
        E5["Average across realizations at t0"]
    end
    subgraph TimeView["TIME AVERAGE Vertical view along one sample path"]
        T1["X1 of t1"]
        T2["X1 of t2"]
        T3["X1 of t3"]
        T4["X1 of t4"]
        T5["Average across time for one realization"]
    end
    EnsembleView --> Result["Mean-Ergodic: Ensemble Avg = Time Avg"]
    TimeView --> Result
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define a mean-ergodic process. State the mean-ergodic theorem.**

**Model Answer:**

A stochastic process $\{X(t), t \in T\}$ is called a **mean-ergodic process** if the time average of any sample function equals the ensemble (statistical) mean of the process, in the mean-square sense. Mathematically, $\{X(t)\}$ is mean-ergodic if

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} E\left[\left(\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} X(t)\, dt - \mu_X\right)^{\!2}\right] = 0$$

**Mean-Ergodic Theorem:** A WSS process $\{X(t)\}$ with constant mean $\mu_X$, continuous autocovariance $C_{XX}(\tau)$ satisfying $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert\, d\tau < \infty$ is mean-ergodic in the mean-square sense.

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Mark Scoring Pattern:** Definition (1.5 marks) + Theorem statement (1.5 marks). Students typically lose 0.5 marks for omitting the absolute value in the integral condition.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Distinguish between ensemble average and time average. Why is the distinction important in electrical signal analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Ensemble Average | Time Average |
|--------|------------------|--------------|
| Domain | Across realizations (samples) | Across time (one sample) |
| Formula | $E[X(t)] = \mu_X$ | $\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} X(t)\, dt$ |
| Requires | Many realizations | One long recording |
| Nature | Statistical / horizontal | Deterministic / vertical |

**Importance in Electrical Signal Analysis:** In practice, we usually have access to *one* long recording of a signal (e.g., one channel of thermal noise, one waveform of speech). We cannot record infinitely many copies of the signal. Ergodicity guarantees that the time average of *one* recording equals the ensemble mean that we would obtain by averaging many copies. This is the theoretical foundation of every spectrum analyzer, RMS voltmeter, and signal averager used in electrical engineering.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive, step by step, the expression for the mean-square error of the time average of a WSS process $\{X(t)\}$ with constant mean $\mu_X$ and autocovariance $C_{XX}(\tau)$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A WSS process has mean $\mu_X = 3$ and autocovariance $C_{XX}(\tau) = 5 e^{-3\vert \tau\vert}$. Determine whether the process is mean-ergodic in the mean-square sense. Justify each step. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of MSE Expression

**Step 1:** Let $\hat{\mu}_T = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} X(t)\, dt$ be the time-average estimator. Define the error

$$Y_T = \hat{\mu}_T - \mu_X = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} [X(t) - \mu_X]\, dt$$

**Step 2:** Compute the mean of $Y_T$:

$$E[Y_T] = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} [E[X(t)] - \mu_X]\, dt = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} 0\, dt = 0$$

Hence $E[Y_T^2] = \text{Var}(Y_T)$.

**Step 3:** Square the integral (it becomes a double integral):

$$E[Y_T^2] = E\left[\frac{1}{(2T)^2}\int_{-T}^{T}\int_{-T}^{T} [X(t_1)-\mu_X][X(t_2)-\mu_X]\, dt_1\, dt_2\right]$$

**Step 4:** Interchange expectation and integration:

$$E[Y_T^2] = \frac{1}{(2T)^2}\int_{-T}^{T}\int_{-T}^{T} E[[X(t_1)-\mu_X][X(t_2)-\mu_X]]\, dt_1\, dt_2$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the inner expectation as the autocovariance:

$$E[Y_T^2] = \frac{1}{(2T)^2}\int_{-T}^{T}\int_{-T}^{T} C_{XX}(t_1 - t_2)\, dt_1\, dt_2$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $\tau = t_1 - t_2$ and use the change-of-variables formula for a square region. For $\vert \tau \vert \le 2T$, the inner integral over $t_2$ has length $2T - \vert \tau \vert$:

$$\int_{-T}^{T}\int_{-T}^{T} C_{XX}(t_1 - t_2)\, dt_1\, dt_2 = \int_{-2T}^{2T} (2T - \vert \tau \vert)\, C_{XX}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 7:** Substitute back:

$$\boxed{\;E[Y_T^2] = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-2T}^{2T} \left(1 - \frac{\vert \tau \vert}{2T}\right) C_{XX}(\tau)\, d\tau\;}$$

The process is mean-ergodic iff this tends to $0$ as $T \to \infty$.

---

#### Part (b) — Verifying Mean-Ergodicity

**Given:** $\mu_X = 3$ (constant), $C_{XX}(\tau) = 5 e^{-3\vert \tau\vert}$.

**Step 1 — Constant mean:** $\mu_X = 3$ is independent of $t$. ✓

**Step 2 — Continuity of $C_{XX}$ at $\tau = 0$:** $C_{XX}(0) = 5 e^{0} = 5$, and the function is continuous everywhere. ✓

**Step 3 — Absolute integrability:**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert\, d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} 5 e^{-3\vert \tau\vert}\, d\tau = 5 \cdot 2 \int_0^{\infty} e^{-3\tau}\, d\tau = 10 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{10}{3} < \infty$$

✓ The autocovariance is absolutely integrable.

**Step 4 — Apply Mean-Ergodic Theorem:** All three conditions (constant mean, continuous autocovariance, absolute integrability) are satisfied. Therefore, the process $\{X(t)\}$ is **mean-ergodic** in the mean-square sense. $\blacksquare$

**Bonus — Asymptotic behavior:** Since $\lim_{T \to \infty} \text{MSE}(T) = 0$, the time average of any single realization converges in mean-square to $\mu_X = 3$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a)** State and prove the Mean-Ergodic Theorem for a WSS process. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A WSS process has autocovariance $C_{XX}(\tau) = 6 e^{-\vert \tau\vert}\cos(2\tau)$. Test the process for mean-ergodicity. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Mean-Ergodic Theorem: Statement and Proof

**Statement:** Let $\{X(t), t \in \mathbb{R}\}$ be a WSS process with constant mean $\mu_X$, continuous autocovariance $C_{XX}(\tau)$ satisfying $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert\, d\tau < \infty$. Then $\{X(t)\}$ is mean-ergodic in the mean-square sense, i.e.,

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} E\left[\left(\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} X(t)\, dt - \mu_X\right)^{\!2}\right] = 0$$

**Proof Sketch:** From Part (a) of Question A, the MSE is

$$E[Y_T^2] = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-2T}^{2T} \left(1 - \frac{\vert \tau \vert}{2T}\right) C_{XX}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

We bound this as follows. Split the integral at $\vert \tau \vert = T^{\alpha}$ for some $0 < \alpha < 1$. On the inner region, the window is bounded by 1. On the outer region, the window is bounded by $(1 - \vert\tau\vert/2T) \le 1$. Apply dominated convergence: as $T \to \infty$, the integration range covers $\mathbb{R}$, and the integrand converges pointwise to $C_{XX}(\tau)$. Since $\vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert$ is absolutely integrable, the integral converges to $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} C_{XX}(\tau)\, d\tau$. But wait — we need the integral to be **zero**, not just bounded.

**Refined argument:** For any $\epsilon > 0$, choose $M$ such that $\int_{\vert \tau \vert > M} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert\, d\tau < \epsilon$. For $T > M$, split:

$$E[Y_T^2] = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{\vert \tau \vert \le M}(\cdot)\, d\tau + \frac{1}{2T}\int_{M < \vert \tau \vert \le 2T}(\cdot)\, d\tau$$

The first piece is bounded by $\frac{M}{T} \cdot C_{XX}(0) \to 0$. The second is bounded by $\frac{1}{2T}\int_{M < \vert \tau \vert \le 2T} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert\, d\tau \le \frac{1}{2T} \cdot \epsilon \cdot 2T = \epsilon$.

Hence $E[Y_T^2] \to 0$. $\blacksquare$

#### Part (b) — Testing for Mean-Ergodicity

**Given:** $C_{XX}(\tau) = 6 e^{-\vert \tau\vert}\cos(2\tau)$ (assumed constant mean $\mu_X$).

**Step 1 — Continuity:** $C_{XX}(\tau)$ is continuous everywhere. ✓

**Step 2 — Absolute integrability:**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert\, d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} 6 e^{-\vert \tau\vert} \vert \cos(2\tau) \vert\, d\tau$$

Since $\vert \cos(2\tau) \vert \le 1$:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert\, d\tau \le \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} 6 e^{-\vert \tau\vert}\, d\tau = 6 \cdot 2 \cdot 1 = 12 < \infty$$

✓ The autocovariance is absolutely integrable.

**Step 3 — Decorrelation:** $\lim_{\vert \tau \vert \to \infty} C_{XX}(\tau) = \lim_{\vert \tau \vert \to \infty} 6 e^{-\vert \tau\vert}\cos(2\tau) = 0$. ✓

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** All conditions of the Mean-Ergodic Theorem are satisfied. The process is **mean-ergodic** in the mean-square sense. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Mean-Ergodicity Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value** in $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert\, d\tau < \infty$. Examiners specifically test whether you wrote absolute integrability or mere integrability. **Cost: 1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Assuming WSS without stating it.** The theorem *requires* the process to be WSS. If a question says "stochastic process" without specifying WSS, you must **first state the assumption** and then apply the theorem. **Cost: 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Mixing up autocovariance and autocorrelation.** A frequent error: writing $R_{XX}(\tau)$ where $C_{XX}(\tau)$ is needed, or vice versa. Remember: $C_{XX}(\tau) = R_{XX}(\tau) - \mu_X^2$. If the mean is non-zero, substituting $R_{XX}$ for $C_{XX}$ in the MSE formula gives the wrong answer. **Cost: 2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Skipping the change-of-variables step** in the MSE derivation. The transition from $\int_{-T}^{T}\int_{-T}^{T} C_{XX}(t_1 - t_2)\, dt_1\, dt_2$ to $\int_{-2T}^{2T} (2T - \vert\tau\vert) C_{XX}(\tau)\, d\tau$ is the **heart of the proof** — skipping it loses 3-4 marks.
>
> 5. **Confusing "ergodic in mean" with "ergodic in autocorrelation."** These are two different (and independent) properties. Mean-ergodicity says nothing about autocorrelation-ergodicity. Examiners love to test this distinction.
>
> 6. **Writing $\lim_{T \to \infty}$ instead of $\lim_{T \to \infty}$** with the variable clearly indicated. Use a finite $T$ expression and take the limit explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Mean-Ergodic Process**
>
> ✅ **Definition (m.s. sense):** $E\big[(\text{time avg} - \mu_X)^2\big] \to 0$ as $T \to \infty$.
>
> ✅ **Three sufficient conditions:** (i) constant mean, (ii) continuous $C_{XX}$ at 0, (iii) $\int \vert C_{XX}\vert < \infty$.
>
> ✅ **Master identity (memorize!):** $\text{MSE}(T) = \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-2T}^{2T}\big(1 - \frac{\vert\tau\vert}{2T}\big) C_{XX}(\tau)\, d\tau$.
>
> ✅ **Ergodic in mean $\ne$ Ergodic in autocorrelation** — they are independent properties.
>
> ✅ **$C_{XX}(\tau) = R_{XX}(\tau) - \mu_X^2$** — use autocovariance (not autocorrelation) in the MSE formula.
>
> ✅ **Practical check:** If $C_{XX}(\tau) \to 0$ as $\vert\tau\vert \to \infty$ and mean is constant, the process is mean-ergodic.
>
> ✅ **Engineering meaning:** One long recording is statistically equivalent to averaging many copies.
>
> ✅ **Famous examples:** White noise (ideal, but not strictly realizable), random telegraph process (mean-ergodic if independent increments), sinusoidal process with random phase.
>
> ✅ **Non-example:** $X(t) = A$ where $A$ is a random constant with non-zero variance — *not* mean-ergodic because $C_{XX}(\tau) = \sigma_A^2 \not\to 0$.
>
> ✅ **Verification procedure for KTU problems:** (1) Check mean constancy, (2) compute $C_{XX}(\tau)$, (3) verify continuity, (4) evaluate $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert\, d\tau$, (5) conclude.
>
> ✅ **Derivation in 7 steps:** Definition → Mean zero → Square integral → Fubini → Autocovariance → Change of variables → Triangular window form.
>
> ✅ **Units reminder:** $C_{XX}(\tau)$ has units of $[\text{signal}]^2$ (e.g., $V^2$ for voltage noise). The MSE has the same units.
>
> ✅ **Common KTU trick:** "Show that the process is mean-ergodic" — never say "yes" without invoking the theorem explicitly with all three conditions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mean-Ergodic Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE – 4
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mean-Ergodic Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mean-Ergodic Theorem (von Neumann, 1932)**
> Let $\mathcal{H}$ be a real or complex Hilbert space and let $T : \mathcal{H} \to \mathcal{H}$ be a bounded linear operator that is **power-bounded**, i.e., $\sup_{n \geq 0} \Vert T^{n} \Vert < \infty$. Define the **Cesàro mean** (time-average) of the orbit $\{T^{n}x\}_{n \geq 0}$ by
> $$\mu_{N}(x) \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} T^{n} x.$$
> Let $P$ denote the orthogonal projection of $\mathcal{H}$ onto the **fixed-point subspace** $\mathrm{Fix}(T) = \{x \in \mathcal{H} : Tx = x\} = \ker(I - T)$. Then for **every** $x \in \mathcal{H}$,
> $$\lim_{N \to \infty} \left\Vert \mu_{N}(x) - P x \right\Vert_{\mathcal{H}} \;=\; 0.$$

The operator $T$ itself is called a **mean-ergodic operator** whenever the conclusion of the theorem holds. Equivalently, $T$ is mean-ergodic if and only if the closed subspace of fixed points is precisely the limit of the Cesàro means in the norm topology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** A linear operator $U$ on a Hilbert space with $\Vert U \Vert \leq 1$ (in particular, any unitary or any contraction) automatically satisfies the hypotheses. The most common pedagogical case in KTU module–4 problems is the **unitary / contraction** case.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinning Arrow That Forgets Where It Started"

Imagine a clock hand spinning around at irrational angular speed. Every individual position $T^{n}x$ (i.e., the arrow at time $n$) visits a **different** spot on the clock face — there is *no convergence* of the orbit pointwise.

But if you instead **average** the position of the hand over the first $N$ time-steps and let $N \to \infty$, that running average **does** converge — and it converges to the **center of the clock face** (the projection of the initial point onto the space of "things that do not move"). The mean-ergodic theorem is the rigorous version of this fact, valid for *any* power-bounded linear operator on a Hilbert space.

| Informal Object | Mathematical Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Clock hand | Point $x \in \mathcal{H}$ |
| Spin by one tick | Unitary / contraction $T$ |
| Position at time $n$ | $T^{n} x$ |
| Running average of positions | Cesàro mean $\mu_{N}(x)$ |
| Center of the clock | Projection $P x$ onto $\mathrm{Fix}(T)$ |
| Limit of running averages | $\displaystyle\lim_{N \to \infty} \mu_{N}(x)$ |

## 1.3 Why It Matters in Electrical Science

The mean-ergodic theorem is a *cornerstone* of modern engineering mathematics because it underwrites the validity of **time-averaging = ensemble-averaging** in:

- **Statistical signal processing** — replacing long-run time averages with expectation operators.
- **Power-spectrum estimation** of stationary random processes.
- **Markov chain steady-state analysis** (transition operators are contractions on $\ell^{2}$).
- **Quantum information & measurement theory** — mean-ergodic channels.
- **Control theory** — stability of discrete-time dynamical systems under averaging.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook:** *Mean-ergodic* $\;\Longleftrightarrow\;$ **M**ean of orbits → **M**ean converges (in norm) to a fixed point. *Uniform-ergodic* → convergence of the *orbit itself* (much stronger, much rarer).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Decomposition of the Theorem

The proof and statement split cleanly into the following sub-pieces. Mastering each one is the difference between *memorising a theorem* and *understanding it*.

1. **The Cesàro mean operator**  
   $A_{N} := \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} T^{n}$ is a bounded linear operator with $\Vert A_{N} \Vert \leq 1$ whenever $\Vert T \Vert \leq 1$.

2. **The target projection**  
   $P$ is the orthogonal projection onto the closed subspace $\mathcal{M} = \mathrm{Fix}(T) = \ker(I - T)$.

3. **Decomposition principle**  
   $\mathcal{H}$ splits orthogonally as $\mathcal{H} = \mathcal{M} \oplus \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$ with $T \mathcal{M} = \mathcal{M}$ and $T \mathcal{M}^{\perp} \subseteq \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$ (this holds for *any* contraction, not just for isometries).

4. **The vanishing mean on the orthogonal complement**  
   For $x \in \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$ one proves $\Vert A_{N} x \Vert \to 0$ as $N \to \infty$. This is the analytical heart of the proof.

5. **Putting it together**  
   $A_{N} x = A_{N} P x + A_{N}(I - P) x = P x + A_{N} y$ for some $y \in \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$, and the second term vanishes.

## 2.2 Why and How — Pedagogical Layer

- **Why is the fixed-point subspace the natural target?** Because $A_{N} x$ is a *linear combination of points on the orbit*, and any fixed point of $T$ is automatically a fixed point of $A_{N}$, with $A_{N} x = x$. So the limit, if it exists, must lie in $\mathrm{Fix}(T)$.

- **Why Hilbert space?** The proof exploits the **orthogonal projection theorem** — the *existence* of a closest point in a closed convex set. In Banach space the analogous statement requires extra assumptions (e.g., uniformly convex spaces, Linden–Strauss theorem).

- **Why Cesàro, not plain limit?** A plain limit $\lim_{n \to \infty} T^{n} x$ generally does **not** exist; Cesàro averaging is the minimal smoothing that forces convergence in norm.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities below appear in the *mean-ergodic module* of GBMAT401 and are fair game in the ESE / internal assessments.

| Symbol / Statement | Formula / Definition | Standard Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| Cesàro mean | $\mu_{N}(x) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{N}\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} T^{n} x$ | $T$ bounded linear, $x \in \mathcal{H}$ |
| Cesàro operator | $A_{N} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{N}\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} T^{n}$ | $A_{N} \in \mathcal{L}(\mathcal{H})$ |
| Bound on $A_{N}$ | $\Vert A_{N} \Vert \;\leq\; \dfrac{1}{N}\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\Vert T^{n}\Vert \;\leq\; M$ | power-bounded by $M$ |
| Fixed-point subspace | $\mathrm{Fix}(T) \;=\; \ker(I - T) \;=\; \{x \in \mathcal{H} : Tx = x\}$ | always a closed subspace |
| Orthogonal projection onto $\mathrm{Fix}(T)$ | $P$ | $\mathcal{H} = \mathrm{Fix}(T) \oplus \mathrm{Fix}(T)^{\perp}$ |
| Main convergence | $\displaystyle\lim_{N \to \infty} \Vert A_{N} x - P x \Vert \;=\; 0$ | for every $x \in \mathcal{H}$ |
| Equivalent characterisation (mean-ergodic) | $T$ mean-ergodic $\iff$ $\overline{\mathrm{Ran}(I - T)} = \ker(I - T)$ | Yosida–Kakutani criterion |
| Contraction sufficient condition | $\Vert T \Vert \leq 1$ | $T$ is a contraction |
| Unitary sufficient condition | $T^{*}T = TT^{*} = I$ | $T$ unitary on $\mathcal{H}$ |
| Uniformly ergodic $\Rightarrow$ mean-ergodic | $\Vert T^{n} - P \Vert \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$ | much stronger hypothesis |
| Invariant subspace | $T \mathcal{M} \subseteq \mathcal{M}$, $\mathcal{M} = \mathrm{Fix}(T)$ | $\mathcal{M}$ is $T$-reducing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipes rule:** In the rendered table above all "norm bars" appear as the LaTeX macro `\Vert` rather than the literal character `\vert`, so the markdown pipe delimiter is preserved. KTU students *must* observe this when typesetting their own revision sheets.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

| Engineering Domain | How the Mean-Ergodic Theorem is Used |
|---|---|
| **Digital signal processing** | Ergodicity assumption for stationary processes — sample averages converge to ensemble means. |
| **Power spectral density estimation** | Welch / Bartlett methods rely on the Cesàro-type time average as a substitute for expectation. |
| **Markov chains / queuing theory** | The transition operator $P$ on $\ell^{2}$ is a contraction; the theorem guarantees convergence of state distributions to the stationary distribution. |
| **Control theory (discrete time)** | Stability of $x_{k+1} = T x_{k}$ is equivalent to the long-run behaviour of Cesàro means. |
| **Communications (channel capacity)** | Mean-ergodic theorems for non-stationary / non-ergodic sources, with rate-distortion consequences. |
| **Quantum measurement theory** | Mean-ergodic POVM measurements. |
| **Renewable energy & load forecasting** | Time-averaging of solar / wind data converges to the long-run expected power. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Yosida–Kakutani Proof in Full Detail

We present the standard von Neumann / Yosida–Kakutani argument. Take $\mathcal{H}$ a Hilbert space and $T \in \mathcal{L}(\mathcal{H})$ with $\Vert T \Vert \leq 1$ (contraction case). Let

$$A_{N} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} T^{n}, \qquad P \;=\; \text{orthogonal projection onto } \mathrm{Fix}(T).$$

### Step 1 — Reduce to the case $x \in \mathrm{Fix}(T)^{\perp}$.

Let $x \in \mathcal{H}$ be arbitrary. Because $T$ is a contraction we have $T \mathcal{M}^{\perp} \subseteq \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$ where $\mathcal{M} = \mathrm{Fix}(T)$. (Proof: if $y \perp \mathcal{M}$ and $z \in \mathcal{M}$, then $\langle Ty, z\rangle = \langle y, T^{*} z\rangle$. For contractions, $T^{*}$ is also a contraction, so $\Vert T^{*} z - z \Vert$ can be made small only when $T^{*} z \in \mathcal{M}$ — but in fact for a contraction $T$ we directly check $T \mathcal{M}^{\perp} \subseteq \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$ by testing against $z \in \mathcal{M}$:

$$\langle Ty, z\rangle \;=\; \langle y, T^{*} z\rangle \;=\; \langle y, z\rangle \quad (\text{since } Tz = z \Rightarrow T^{*}z \in \mathcal{M}\text{ in the isometric case}).$$

For the contraction case the invariance $T\mathcal{M}^{\perp} \subseteq \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$ is verified component-wise on a basis of $\mathcal{M}^{\perp}$, or by appealing to the standard fact that **any** contraction decomposes Hilbert space into a *maximal unitary* part and a *completely non-unitary* part. For simplicity — and because this is the **KTU-standard hypothesis** — we restrict to the **isometric** case $\Vert T \Vert = 1$ together with $T$ being an isometry, which is a clean model and gives the invariance directly.)

### Step 2 — Show that $A_{N} y \to 0$ for $y \in \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$.

Let $y \perp \mathcal{M}$. Consider the following telescoping identity, valid for *any* bounded operator $S$ and any $m \geq 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(I - S)\sum_{n=0}^{m-1} S^{n} \;=\; I - S^{m}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply this with $S = T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(I - T) \, A_{N} y \;=\; \frac{1}{N}(I - T)\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} T^{n} y \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\big(I - T^{N}\big)y.
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert A_{N} y \Vert^{2} \;=\; \langle A_{N} y,\, A_{N} y \rangle.
\end{aligned}
$$

We now exploit the **mean-ergodic lemma** (a sharper quantitative bound). Note that for any $x \in \mathcal{H}$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\Vert A_{N} x \right\Vert^{2}
&\;=\; \left\langle \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} T^{n} x,\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{m=0}^{N-1} T^{m} x \right\rangle \\
&\;=\; \frac{1}{N^{2}}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \langle T^{n} x, T^{m} x\rangle \\
&\;=\; \frac{1}{N^{2}}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \langle T^{\vert n - m \vert} x,\, x\rangle \quad (\text{since }T\text{ is an isometry}) \\
&\;=\; \frac{1}{N^{2}}\sum_{k=-(N-1)}^{N-1} (N - \vert k \vert)\,\langle T^{\vert k \vert} x, x\rangle.
\end{aligned}
$$

The leading diagonal $k = 0$ contributes

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{N^{2}} \cdot N \cdot \langle x, x\rangle \;=\; \frac{\Vert x \Vert^{2}}{N} \;\xrightarrow{N \to \infty}\; 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $\Vert A_{N} y \Vert^{2} \to 0$, i.e., $\Vert A_{N} y \Vert \to 0$ as $N \to \infty$ for every $y \in \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The reader should *memorise* the diagonal argument above — it is the single most common technical device examiners expect at KTU level.

### Step 3 — Decompose and conclude.

Let $x \in \mathcal{H}$ be arbitrary and write $x = P x + (I - P) x$ with $P x \in \mathcal{M}$ and $y := (I - P) x \in \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{N} x - P x \;=\; A_{N} P x + A_{N} y - P x \;=\; P x - P x + A_{N} y \;=\; A_{N} y.
\end{aligned}
$$

(Here we used that $A_{N}$ leaves $\mathcal{M}$ pointwise fixed: $A_{N} P x = P x$ because $T^{n} P x = P x$ for every $n \geq 0$.) Consequently,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\Vert A_{N} x - P x \right\Vert \;=\; \Vert A_{N} y \Vert \;\xrightarrow{N \to \infty}\; 0,
\end{aligned}
$$

which is precisely the conclusion of the mean-ergodic theorem. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Proof of the Invariance $T \mathcal{M}^{\perp} \subseteq \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$ (Isometric Case)

We must show that if $y \perp \mathcal{M}$ then $T y \perp \mathcal{M}$ as well. Take any $z \in \mathcal{M}$, i.e., $T z = z$. Since $T$ is an isometry, $T^{*}$ is also an isometry, and $\ker(I - T) = \ker(I - T^{*})$. So $z \in \ker(I - T^{*})$ and therefore

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle T y,\, z \rangle \;=\; \langle y,\, T^{*} z \rangle \;=\; \langle y,\, z \rangle \;=\; 0,
\end{aligned}
$$

because $y \perp \mathcal{M}$ and $T^{*}z \in \mathcal{M}$. Hence $Ty \in \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$, as claimed.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical / Structural Example

**Example (Schatten-style computation on $\mathbb{C}^{2}$).** Let $\mathcal{H} = \mathbb{C}^{2}$ and let

$$
\begin{aligned}
T \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \vert \lambda \vert < 1.
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a strict contraction. The fixed-point subspace is $\mathrm{Fix}(T) = \{(z,0) : z \in \mathbb{C}\} = \mathrm{span}\{e_{1}\}$, and the orthogonal projection is

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}.
\end{aligned}
$$

For an arbitrary $x = (a, b)^{T}$ we have $T^{n} x = (a,\, \lambda^{n} b)^{T}$, so

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{N} x \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} T^{n} x \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} a \\[2pt] \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\lambda^{n} b \end{pmatrix}
\;=\; \begin{pmatrix} a \\[2pt] \dfrac{1 - \lambda^{N}}{N(1 - \lambda)} \, b \end{pmatrix}.
\end{aligned}
$$

As $N \to \infty$, the geometric sum satisfies

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\vert \dfrac{1 - \lambda^{N}}{N(1 - \lambda)} \right\vert \;\leq\; \dfrac{2}{N \vert 1 - \lambda \vert} \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
\end{aligned}
$$

so

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{N} x \;\longrightarrow\; \begin{pmatrix} a \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \;=\; P x.
\end{aligned}
$$

This **explicitly realises** the mean-ergodic limit on a $2 \times 2$ toy. Notice that the individual orbit $T^{n} x$ does **not** converge when $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$ but $\lambda \neq 0$ (it goes to $(a,0)$ directly — actually it does converge in this case; the more dramatic case is $\vert \lambda \vert = 1$, $\lambda \neq 1$, where the orbit fails to converge but the Cesàro mean does).

> [!TIP]
> The KTU board **loves** the rotation case $T = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$ with $\theta/\pi \notin \mathbb{Q}$, because the orbit is dense on the unit circle, **no individual point converges**, yet $\Vert A_{N} x \Vert \to 0$ for all $x$ with zero mean (i.e., $x \in \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$). Keep this example in your back pocket for the 14-mark question.

## 3.4 Key Identity Box — The Engine of the Proof

The following telescoping identity, together with its cousin the Cesàro bound, is the algebraic heart of the entire theorem and the most-tested calculation in KTU module–4:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(I - T)\, A_{N} \;=\; \frac{1}{N}(I - T^{N}), \qquad\text{and}\qquad
A_{N}(I - T) \;=\; \frac{1}{N}(I - T^{N}).
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence on $\mathrm{Ran}(I - T)$ we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\Vert A_{N} y \right\Vert \;\leq\; \frac{1}{N}\big(\Vert y\Vert + \Vert T^{N} y\Vert\big) \;\leq\; \frac{2\Vert y\Vert}{N}.
\end{aligned}
$$

When $T$ is a contraction and $\overline{\mathrm{Ran}(I - T)} = \ker(I - T)$ (Yosida–Kakutani criterion), this bound is enough to show $A_{N} \to P$ in the **strong operator topology**.
