# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Mean-Ergodic Theorem (von Neumann). Clearly write down the hypothesis on the operator $T$ and the conclusion regarding the Cesàro means.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Let $\mathcal{H}$ be a Hilbert space and $T : \mathcal{H} \to \mathcal{H}$ a **power-bounded** linear operator, i.e., $\sup_{n \geq 0} \Vert T^{n} \Vert < \infty$. Define the Cesàro mean $A_{N} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} T^{n}$ and let $P$ be the orthogonal projection of $\mathcal{H}$ onto the fixed-point subspace $\ker(I - T)$. Then for every $x \in \mathcal{H}$,
> $$\lim_{N \to \infty} \big\Vert A_{N} x - P x \big\Vert = 0.$$
> Any such operator $T$ is called **mean-ergodic**.

*(1 mark for the operator hypothesis, 1 mark for the Cesàro mean definition, 1 mark for the conclusion.)*

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a mean-ergodic operator? How is mean-ergodicity related to uniform ergodicity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An operator $T$ on a Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$ is **mean-ergodic** if for every $x \in \mathcal{H}$ the Cesàro means $\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} T^{n} x$ converge **in norm** to some $P x$, where $P$ is the orthogonal projection onto $\ker(I - T)$. Uniform ergodicity is the strictly stronger condition that $\Vert T^{n} x - P x \Vert \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$ for every $x \in \mathcal{H}$. Every uniformly ergodic operator is mean-ergodic, but not conversely — e.g., an irrational rotation on $\mathbb{C}^{2}$ is mean-ergodic but not uniformly ergodic.

*(1 mark definition of mean-ergodic, 1 mark definition of uniform ergodicity, 1 mark for the implication and counter-example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE convention, every module question carries internal choice. Below we present **Question A** and **Question B**, each with two sub-parts of 7 marks. Either choice should be attemptable in ~25 minutes.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** [7 marks] State and prove the **Mean-Ergodic Theorem** for a contraction $T$ on a Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$.

**(b)** [7 marks] Let $T$ be the $2 \times 2$ rotation matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
T \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \phantom{-}\cos\theta \end{pmatrix}, \quad \theta \in (0, 2\pi),\; \frac{\theta}{\pi} \notin \mathbb{Q}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Find the fixed-point subspace $\mathrm{Fix}(T)$, the orthogonal projection $P$ onto it, and verify explicitly that for any $x = (a, b)^{T}$ the Cesàro means $A_{N} x$ converge to $P x$ in the Euclidean norm.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

*Statement (1 mark):* Already given in Question A.1 above; reproduce verbatim.

*Proof outline (6 marks):* We present the Yosida–Kakutani argument.

1. **Set up.** Let $T$ be a contraction on $\mathcal{H}$, so $\Vert T \Vert \leq 1$. Let $\mathcal{M} = \ker(I - T)$ and $P$ the orthogonal projection onto $\mathcal{M}$.

2. **Decomposition (2 marks).** For an isometry / unitary restriction the decomposition $\mathcal{H} = \mathcal{M} \oplus \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$ is $T$-reducing: $T \mathcal{M} \subseteq \mathcal{M}$ trivially (since $\mathcal{M}$ is the fixed set), and $T \mathcal{M}^{\perp} \subseteq \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$ by the inner-product test

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle T y, z\rangle \;=\; \langle y, T^{*} z\rangle \;=\; \langle y, z\rangle \;=\; 0 \quad (y \in \mathcal{M}^{\perp}, z \in \mathcal{M}).
\end{aligned}
$$

3. **Vanishing mean on the orthogonal complement (2 marks).** For $y \in \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$, expand

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert A_{N} y \Vert^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{N^{2}} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \langle T^{n} y, T^{m} y\rangle
\;=\; \frac{1}{N^{2}} \sum_{k=-(N-1)}^{N-1} (N - \vert k \vert) \langle T^{\vert k \vert} y, y\rangle.
\end{aligned}
$$

The $k = 0$ diagonal term equals $\Vert y\Vert^{2}/N \to 0$. The remaining double sum is bounded by

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{2}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N-1} \Vert y\Vert^{2} \;\leq\; 2 \Vert y\Vert^{2},
\end{aligned}
$$

but careful analysis (or a direct application of the **mean-ergodic lemma** below) shows the whole thing tends to $0$.

4. **Telescoping identity (1 mark).** $(I - T) A_{N} = (I - T^{N})/N$, so on $\mathrm{Ran}(I - T)$ we have $\Vert A_{N} y \Vert \leq 2 \Vert y \Vert / N \to 0$. Yosida–Kakutani says $\overline{\mathrm{Ran}(I - T)} = \ker(I - T) = \mathcal{M}$, so density of $\mathrm{Ran}(I - T)$ in $\mathcal{M}^{\perp}$ finishes the proof.

5. **Conclusion (1 mark).** $A_{N} x - P x = A_{N}(I - P) x \to 0$ for every $x$, since $(I - P) x \in \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

*Fixed-point subspace (2 marks):* Solve $T x = x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta - 1 & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta - 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}a\\b\end{pmatrix} \;=\; 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

The determinant of the coefficient matrix is

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\cos\theta - 1)^{2} + \sin^{2}\theta \;=\; 2 - 2\cos\theta \;=\; 4\sin^{2}(\theta/2).
\end{aligned}
$$

This vanishes only when $\theta = 0$ (mod $2\pi$). Since $\theta \neq 0$ in our problem, the determinant is **non-zero** and the only solution is $a = b = 0$. Hence

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Fix}(T) \;=\; \{(0,0)\}, \qquad P \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}.
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for fixed-point computation, 1 mark for $P$.)*

*Orbit and Cesàro mean (2 marks):* Powers of $T$ are powers of rotation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T^{n} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \cos n\theta & -\sin n\theta \\ \sin n\theta & \phantom{-}\cos n\theta \end{pmatrix}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{N} x \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} T^{n} x \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\big(a\cos n\theta - b\sin n\theta\big) \\[6pt] \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\big(a\sin n\theta + b\cos n\theta\big) \end{pmatrix}.
\end{aligned}
$$

*Convergence to zero (2 marks):* Use the standard trigonometric summation identities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\cos n\theta \;=\; \frac{\sin(N\theta/2)\cos((N-1)\theta/2)}{\sin(\theta/2)}, \qquad
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\sin n\theta \;=\; \frac{\sin(N\theta/2)\sin((N-1)\theta/2)}{\sin(\theta/2)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Both numerators are bounded by $1$ in absolute value, so each sum is bounded by $1/\vert \sin(\theta/2) \vert$. Hence

$$
\begin{aligned}
\big\Vert A_{N} x \big\Vert \;\leq\; \frac{C(\theta)}{N}\big(\vert a \vert + \vert b \vert\big) \;\longrightarrow\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

as $N \to \infty$, where $C(\theta) = 2 / \vert \sin(\theta/2) \vert$. Since $P x = (0,0)^{T}$, we conclude

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{N \to \infty} \big\Vert A_{N} x - P x \big\Vert \;=\; 0,
\end{aligned}
$$

confirming the mean-ergodic theorem in this example. Note that the individual orbit $T^{n} x$ does **not** converge (it is dense on the circle when $\theta/\pi \notin \mathbb{Q}$), so the Cesàro average is genuinely *stronger* than the pointwise limit. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** [7 marks] Define a **mean-ergodic operator** on a Hilbert space. Prove that if $T$ is **uniformly ergodic** (i.e., $\Vert T^{n} - P \Vert \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$ for some projection $P$), then $T$ is also mean-ergodic.

**(b)** [7 marks] Consider the operator

$$
\begin{aligned}
T \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \tfrac{1}{2} & 1 \\ 0 & \tfrac{1}{2} \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

on $\mathbb{C}^{2}$. Compute its fixed-point subspace, the corresponding projection, and verify the conclusion of the mean-ergodic theorem by an explicit calculation of the Cesàro means.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

*Definition (2 marks):* $T$ on a Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$ is **mean-ergodic** if for every $x \in \mathcal{H}$ the Cesàro means $\mu_{N}(x) = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} T^{n} x$ converge in norm to some limit. By von Neumann's theorem this limit must be $P x$, the orthogonal projection of $x$ onto $\ker(I - T)$.

*Proof (5 marks):* Suppose $T$ is uniformly ergodic, i.e., there is a bounded linear projection $P$ (with $P^{2} = P$) such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} \big\Vert T^{n} - P \big\Vert \;=\; 0. \tag{$\star$}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify $P$.** From $(\star)$ we have $T^{n} \to P$ in operator norm. Multiplying $(\star)$ on the left by $T$ and using $\Vert T T^{n} - T P\Vert = \Vert T^{n+1} - T P\Vert \to 0$, we obtain $T P = P$. Similarly, $P T = P$. Hence $P$ commutes with $T$ and $P$ is a projection, and $P x$ is fixed by $T$ for all $x$. Conversely, if $T y = y$ then $T^{n} y = y$ and the limit in $(\star)$ gives $P y = y$, so $\mathrm{Fix}(T) \subseteq \mathrm{Ran}(P)$. The reverse inclusion is immediate from $T P = P$, so $\mathrm{Ran}(P) = \mathrm{Fix}(T)$. In a Hilbert space, $P$ is the orthogonal projection onto $\mathrm{Fix}(T)$ because $P$ is a self-adjoint projection (we get $P = P^{*}$ from $P = \lim T^{n} T^{n*}$ when $T$ is a contraction, or by direct verification in the isometric case).

**Step 2 — Derive mean ergodicity.** Compute

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{N} x - P x \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\big(T^{n} x - P x\big) \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\big(T^{n} - P\big)x.
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore

$$
\begin{aligned}
\big\Vert A_{N} x - P x \big\Vert \;\leq\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \big\Vert T^{n} - P \big\Vert \cdot \Vert x \Vert.
\end{aligned}
$$

By $(\star)$ the sequence $\Vert T^{n} - P \Vert$ is bounded (say by $M$) and tends to $0$. Splitting the sum at some large $N_{0}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\Vert T^{n} - P \Vert
&\;\leq\; \frac{N_{0} M}{N} \;+\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=N_{0}}^{N-1}\Vert T^{n} - P\Vert.
\end{aligned}
$$

Given $\varepsilon > 0$, choose $N_{0}$ with $\Vert T^{n} - P\Vert < \varepsilon$ for $n \geq N_{0}$; then choose $N$ large enough that $N_{0} M / N < \varepsilon$. Hence the Cesàro mean of $\Vert T^{n} - P \Vert$ tends to $0$, and so does $\Vert A_{N} x - P x \Vert$. This is mean ergodicity.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

*Fixed-point subspace (2 marks):* Solve $T x = x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{pmatrix} -\tfrac{1}{2} & 1 \\ 0 & -\tfrac{1}{2} \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix}a\\b\end{pmatrix} \;=\; 0
\;\Longrightarrow\; -\tfrac{a}{2} + b = 0,\; -\tfrac{b}{2} = 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

The second equation forces $b = 0$, and the first then forces $a = 0$. Hence

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Fix}(T) \;=\; \{(0,0)\}, \qquad P \;=\; 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks for the eigenvalue computation, 1 mark for $P = 0$.)

*Powers of $T$ (1 mark):* Since $T = \tfrac{1}{2}I + N$ where $N = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ is nilpotent with $N^{2} = 0$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
T^{n} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2^{n}}\, I + n \cdot \tfrac{1}{2^{n-1}} N
\;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{1}{2^{n}} & \dfrac{n}{2^{n-1}} \\[6pt] 0 & \dfrac{1}{2^{n}} \end{pmatrix}.
\end{aligned}
$$

*Cesàro mean (2 marks):* For $x = (a, b)^{T}$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{N} x \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} T^{n} x
\;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{a}{2^{n}} + \dfrac{n b}{2^{n-1}} \\[6pt] \dfrac{b}{2^{n}} \end{pmatrix}
\;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{a}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\dfrac{1}{2^{n}} + \dfrac{2b}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\dfrac{n}{2^{n}} \\[6pt] \dfrac{b}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\dfrac{1}{2^{n}} \end{pmatrix}.
\end{aligned}
$$

*Convergence (2 marks):* The geometric sums are bounded:
$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} 2^{-n} \leq 2$ and $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} n 2^{-n} \leq 2$. Therefore

$$
\begin{aligned}
\big\Vert A_{N} x \big\Vert^{2}
\;\leq\; \bigg(\frac{2\vert a\vert}{N} + \frac{4 \vert b\vert}{N}\bigg)^{2} + \bigg(\frac{2\vert b\vert}{N}\bigg)^{2}
\;\leq\; \frac{C(a,b)}{N^{2}} \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $P = 0$, we have $\Vert A_{N} x - P x \Vert \to 0$, confirming the mean-ergodic theorem for this operator. (Note that $T$ is a *strict* contraction with $\Vert T \Vert = 1$ achieved on the vector $(0,1)^{T}$, but $\Vert T^{n}\Vert = 1/n \to 0$ on that direction, so $T$ is in fact **uniformly ergodic** to $0$.) $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks on the Mean-Ergodic Theorem (read carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Conflating *mean* and *uniform* ergodicity.** Writing "$T^{n} x \to P x$" instead of the Cesàro sum. The Cesàro mean is the **only** object guaranteed to converge. *(-2 to -3 marks if the statement is wrong on the ESE.)*
> 2. **Omitting the hypothesis $\sup_{n}\Vert T^{n}\Vert < \infty$.** Boundedness of $T$ alone is not enough for the proof. *(-1 mark.)*
> 3. **Saying "$\ker(I - T) = \mathrm{Ran}(I - T)$" without justification.** The correct fact is $\overline{\mathrm{Ran}(I - T)} = \ker(I - T)$ (closed-range / Yosida–Kakutani). Equality without closure is generally false. *(-2 marks.)*
> 4. **Forgetting that $P$ is the *orthogonal* projection.** In Banach space the closest-point projection need not exist; the theorem is *Hilbert-specific*. *(-1 mark.)*
> 5. **Mixing up the rotation example.** For the irrational rotation on $\mathbb{C}^{2}$ the fixed-point subspace is $\{0\}$, **not** the whole space. Several students incorrectly take $P = I$. *(-2 marks.)*
> 6. **Skipping the telescoping identity step.** The computation $(I - T) A_{N} = (I - T^{N})/N$ is the *engine* of the proof and the most common 3-mark step in the valuation key. Always write it out.
> 7. **No "for every $x \in \mathcal{H}$" wording.** Convergence is **pointwise in $x$** in the strong operator topology, *not* operator norm. The Cesàro operator $A_{N}$ need not converge in operator norm to $P$ (although it does in the uniformly ergodic case).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision checklist — print this and pin to your wall.**

- [x] The **Mean-Ergodic Theorem** says: for power-bounded $T$ on a Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$, the Cesàro means $A_{N} x$ converge in **norm** to $P x$, where $P$ is the orthogonal projection onto $\mathrm{Fix}(T) = \ker(I - T)$.
- [x] Power-bounded means $\sup_{n \geq 0} \Vert T^{n} \Vert < \infty$; contractions ($\Vert T \Vert \leq 1$) and unitaries satisfy this automatically.
- [x] The proof pivots on the **orthogonal decomposition** $\mathcal{H} = \mathrm{Fix}(T) \oplus \mathrm{Fix}(T)^{\perp}$ and the **vanishing-mean lemma** $\Vert A_{N} y\Vert \to 0$ for $y \in \mathrm{Fix}(T)^{\perp}$.
- [x] The **telescoping identity** $(I - T) A_{N} = (I - T^{N})/N$ is the algebraic engine.
- [x] The **Yosida–Kakutani criterion**: $T$ is mean-ergodic $\iff$ $\overline{\mathrm{Ran}(I - T)} = \ker(I - T)$.
- [x] **Mean ergodic ≠ uniformly ergodic**. Uniform ergodicity means $\Vert T^{n} - P \Vert \to 0$; mean ergodicity only requires the *averaged* orbit to converge.
- [x] **Canonical example**: irrational rotation on $\mathbb{C}^{2}$ — orbit is dense on the circle (no individual convergence) but Cesàro means tend to $0$.
- [x] **Canonical example**: $2 \times 2$ block-diagonal $T = \mathrm{diag}(1, \lambda)$ with $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$ — explicit geometric-sum computation shows $A_{N} x \to (a, 0)^{T}$.
- [x] **Applications**: ergodicity of stationary stochastic processes, Markov chain convergence to stationary distribution, time-averaging in signal processing, stability analysis in discrete-time control.
- [x] **Forgetting to write "for every $x \in \mathcal{H}$"** loses a mark. Always quantify.
- [x] **Never claim uniform convergence of the orbit** — it is the *Cesàro* sum that converges, not the orbit itself.
- [x] **Hilbert-space specific**: in general Banach space the theorem fails (counterexample: shift on $c_{0}$).
- [x] **Most-tested single equation**: $(I - T) A_{N} = (I - T^{N})/N$. Memorise it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Correlation Ergodic Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GBMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE – 4 > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Correlation Ergodic Process # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Autocorrelation Function — Formal Definition For a random process $\{X(t), t \in T\}$, the **autocorrelation function** is the joint second-order moment defined as the expected value of the product of the process evaluated at two different time instants: $$R_{XX}(t_1, t_2) = E[X(t_1) \cdot X(t_2)]$$ For a **Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS)** process, the statistical properties become time-invariant, and the autocorrelation depends only on the time difference $\tau = t_2 - t_1$: $$R_{XX}(\tau) = E[X(t) \cdot X(t + \tau)]$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The autocorrelation function is the single most important descriptor of a WSS process, because it captures both the **mean-square power** (at $\tau = 0$) and the **statistical dependence** between samples separated in time. ## 1.2 Cross-Correlation Function — Formal Definition For two jointly considered random processes $\{X(t)\}$ and $\{Y(t)\}$, the **cross-correlation function** is defined as: $$R_{XY}(t_1, t_2) = E[X(t_1) \cdot Y(t_2)]$$ Under joint wide-sense stationarity, this reduces to: $$R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(t) \cdot Y(t + \tau)]$$ ## 1.3 Ergodic Process — Formal Definition A stationary random process is called **ergodic** if **every ensemble average equals the corresponding time average** computed over a single sample function. For an ergodic process, the time average over the infinite past equals the statistical expectation: $$\langle X(t) \rangle = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} X(t) \, dt = E[X(t)] = \mu_X$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Concept Check:** Ergodicity is a *stronger* property than stationarity. A process can be WSS but not ergodic. Ergodicity allows engineers to **infer statistical properties from a single long observation** of one signal realization — this is fundamental in communication, signal processing, and noise analysis. ## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy **Analogy — The Classroom Average Problem:** Imagine 100 classrooms, each with 40 students taking the same exam. - The **ensemble average** would be: collect the average score from *each* of the 100 classrooms, then average these 100 averages. - The **time average** would be: pick *one* classroom, look at how its average evolves over the *entire academic year* (10 exams), and compute the running mean. > If after 10 exams the running mean of the single classroom **converges to the ensemble mean** of all 100 classrooms, then that classroom is "ergodic" — its history tells you everything about the whole population. In **electrical engineering**, this translates to: we record **one** long voltage waveform $x(t)$ from a noisy resistor, and if the process is ergodic, the DC offset (mean) and RMS power (variance) computed from that *single* trace are *exactly* the true statistical parameters. We never need to repeat the experiment 1000 times. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Metrics in Bold:** > - **Mean-square value:** $E[X^2(t)] = R_{XX}(0)$ (in $\text{V}^2$ or $\text{W}$ for unit resistance) > - **Average power:** $P_X = R_{XX}(0) - \mu_X^2 = \sigma_X^2$ (in Watts) > - **Variance of the time-average estimator** must tend to **0** for ergodicity. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Properties of the Autocorrelation Function $R_{XX}(\tau)$ For a WSS process, the autocorrelation function satisfies the following mathematically proven properties (these are **favorite 3-mark questions** in KTU exams): 1. **Even symmetry** about the origin: $$R_{XX}(\tau) = R_{XX}(-\tau)$$ 2. **Maximum at the origin** (Cauchy–Schwarz inequality consequence): $$R_{XX}(0) \geq \vert R_{XX}(\tau) \vert \quad \text{for all } \tau$$ 3. **Mean-square value** at zero lag: $$R_{XX}(0) = E[X^2(t)] = \text{mean-square value of the process}$$ 4. **Relation to mean and variance:** $$R_{XX}(\tau) = \sigma_X^2 + \mu_X^2 \quad \text{(only if } X(t) \text{ and } X(t+\tau) \text{ are uncorrelated)}$$ $$R_{XX}(0) = \sigma_X^2 + \mu_X^2$$ 5. **Limit at infinity (zero-mean process):** $$\lim_{\vert\tau\vert \to \infty} R_{XX}(\tau) = \mu_X^2$$ 6. **Non-negative definiteness:** For any set of instants $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n$ and any real coefficients $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_i \, a_j \, R_{XX}(t_i - t_j) \geq 0$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why does the maximum occur at $\tau = 0$?** > A random variable is *most correlated with itself* at the exact same instant. As the time gap grows, the correlation generally decays — this is the physical basis of all "memory loss" in stationary signals. ## 2.2 Properties of the Cross-Correlation Function $R_{XY}(\tau)$ For two *jointly* WSS processes $X(t)$ and $Y(t)$: 1. **Asymmetry relation:** $$R_{XY}(\tau) = R_{YX}(-\tau)$$ 2. **Magnitude bound** (Cauchy–Schwarz): $$\vert R_{XY}(\tau) \vert^2 \leq R_{XX}(0) \cdot R_{YY}(0)$$ 3. **Statistical independence** implies: $$R_{XY}(\tau) = \mu_X \cdot \mu_Y \quad \text{(constant, no } \tau \text{ dependence)}$$ ## 2.3 Ergodicity — Conditions and Theorems ### 2.3.1 Mean Ergodicity A WSS process is **mean-ergodic** if and only if: $$\lim_{T \to \infty} \text{Var}\left[ \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} X(t) \, dt \right] = 0$$ A sufficient condition for mean ergodicity (Papoulis theorem): $$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} \left(1 - \frac{\vert \tau \vert}{2T}\right) \left[R_{XX}(\tau) - \mu_X^2\right] d\tau = 0$$ In simpler form, **if the process is mean-ergodic, the autocorrelation function must satisfy:** $$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} C_{XX}(\tau) \, d\tau = 0$$ where $C_{XX}(\tau) = R_{XX}(\tau) - \mu_X^2$ is the **autocovariance** function. ### 2.3.2 Correlation Ergodicity A WSS process is **correlation-ergodic** if and only if: $$\lim_{T \to \infty} \text{Var}\left[ \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} X(t) X(t + \tau) \, dt \right] = 0$$ A sufficient condition involves the **fourth-order moment** of the process, often requiring the process to have a *continuous* autocorrelation function and zero mean-square autocorrelation at infinity. ## 2.4 Power Spectral Density (Bridge Concept) The **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem** links time-domain correlation to frequency-domain power: $$S_{XX}(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_{XX}(\tau) \, e^{-j\omega\tau} \, d\tau \quad \text{(Fourier Transform)}$$ $$R_{XX}(\tau) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_{XX}(\omega) \, e^{j\omega\tau} \, d\omega \quad \text{(Inverse Fourier Transform)}$$ ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Property | Expression | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Autocorrelation (WSS) | $R_{XX}(\tau) = E[X(t) X(t+\tau)]$ | Power, spectral analysis | | 2 | Cross-correlation (WSS) | $R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(t) Y(t+\tau)]$ | Signal alignment, system ID | | 3 | Even symmetry | $R_{XX}(\tau) = R_{XX}(-\tau)$ | Simplifies FT computation | | 4 | Maximum at origin | $R_{XX}(0) \geq \vert R_{XX}(\tau) \vert$ | DC power identification | | 5 | Mean-square value | $R_{XX}(0) = E[X^2(t)]$ | Power measurement | | 6 | Variance relation | $R_{XX}(0) - R_{XX}(\infty) = \sigma_X^2$ | Noise variance extraction | | 7 | Cross-correl. bound | $\vert R_{XY}(\tau) \vert \leq \sqrt{R_{XX}(0) R_{YY}(0)}$ | Coherence measurement | | 8 | Cross-symmetry | $R_{XY}(\tau) = R_{YX}(-\tau)$ | Two-sided analysis | | 9 | Mean ergodicity | $\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} C_{XX}(\tau) d\tau = 0$ | Single-trace statistics | | 10 | Wiener–Khinchin | $S_{XX}(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{XX}(\tau)\}$ | Spectral engineering | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > - **Communication systems:** Autocorrelation of PN sequences enables Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA). > - **Radar/Sonar:** Cross-correlation detects time-delay (range) of reflected signals. > - **Noise analysis:** Ergodicity justifies measuring thermal-noise power from a single resistor trace. > - **System identification:** Cross-correlation of input-output reveals impulse response (LTI systems). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Even Symmetry of Autocorrelation We start from the formal definition of $R_{XX}(\tau)$ for a WSS process: $$R_{XX}(\tau) = E[X(t) \cdot X(t + \tau)]$$ **Step 1:** Define a new variable $t' = t + \tau$, so $t = t' - \tau$. Then: $$R_{XX}(\tau) = E[X(t' - \tau) \cdot X(t')]$$ **Step 2:** Recognize that the *order of multiplication inside expectation* does not matter (scalar multiplication is commutative): $$R_{XX}(\tau) = E[X(t') \cdot X(t' - \tau)]$$ **Step 3:** The right-hand side is, by definition, the autocorrelation evaluated at lag $-\tau$: $$R_{XX}(\tau) = R_{XX}(-\tau) \qquad \blacksquare$$ This is the formal proof of even symmetry — one of the most-tested KTU derivations. ## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum of Autocorrelation at $\tau = 0$ **Step 1:** Consider any real constant $a$ and use the **non-negative expectation** of a squared random variable: $$E\left[ \left( X(t) + a \cdot X(t + \tau) \right)^2 \right] \geq 0$$ **Step 2:** Expand the squared expression inside the expectation: $$E\left[ X^2(t) + 2a \, X(t) X(t+\tau) + a^2 X^2(t+\tau) \right] \geq 0$$ **Step 3:** Apply linearity of expectation and the WSS property ($E[X^2(t)] = E[X^2(t+\tau)] = R_{XX}(0)$): $$R_{XX}(0) + 2a \, R_{XX}(\tau) + a^2 R_{XX}(0) \geq 0$$ **Step 4:** Collect terms as a quadratic in $a$: $$\left(1 + a^2\right) R_{XX}(0) + 2a \, R_{XX}(\tau) \geq 0$$ **Step 5:** A quadratic in $a$ that is $\geq 0$ for all $a$ must have a non-positive discriminant: $$\left[2 R_{XX}(\tau)\right]^2 - 4 \left(1 + a^2\right) R_{XX}^2(0) \cdot \text{(discriminant test on canonical form)}$$ A cleaner version: minimize the LHS over $a$ by setting $\frac{\partial}{\partial a}[\cdot] = 0$, giving $a = -R_{XX}(\tau)/R_{XX}(0)$. Substituting back: $$R_{XX}(0) - \frac{R_{XX}^2(\tau)}{R_{XX}(0)} \geq 0$$ **Step 6:** Multiply through by $R_{XX}(0) > 0$: $$R_{XX}^2(0) - R_{XX}^2(\tau) \geq 0 \implies R_{XX}^2(0) \geq R_{XX}^2(\tau)$$ Taking the (non-negative) square root: $$\vert R_{XX}(\tau) \vert \leq R_{XX}(0) \qquad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Power Spectral Density (Wiener–Khinchin) **Step 1:** Start with the autocorrelation of a WSS process: $$R_{XX}(\tau) = E[X(t) X(t + \tau)]$$ **Step 2:** Take the Fourier transform of both sides with respect to $\tau$: $$S_{XX}(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_{XX}(\tau) e^{-j\omega\tau} d\tau$$ **Step 3:** Substitute the definition of expectation: $$S_{XX}(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} E[X(t) X(t + \tau)] e^{-j\omega\tau} d\tau$$ **Step 4:** Swap the order of expectation and integration (Fubini's theorem applies since $X$ is finite-power): $$S_{XX}(\omega) = E\left[ X(t) \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(t + \tau) e^{-j\omega\tau} d\tau \right]$$ **Step 5:** Perform the change of variable $u = t + \tau$, so $du = d\tau$: $$S_{XX}(\omega) = E\left[ X(t) \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(u) e^{-j\omega(u - t)} du \right]$$ **Step 6:** Factor out the deterministic $e^{j\omega t}$: $$S_{XX}(\omega) = E\left[ X(t) e^{j\omega t} \right] \cdot E\left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(u) e^{-j\omega u} du \right]$$ Wait — this factorization is only valid if the process is statistically independent in a strong sense; the rigorous proof uses the limit definition with truncated signals. The final celebrated result is: $$\boxed{S_{XX}(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{XX}(\tau)\}} \qquad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.4 Derivation: Variance of the Time-Average Estimator (Mean Ergodicity Criterion) **Step 1:** Define the time-average estimator for a truncated observation of length $2T$: $$\hat{\mu}_X(T) = \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} X(t) \, dt$$ **Step 2:** Compute the expectation of this estimator: $$E[\hat{\mu}_X(T)] = \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} E[X(t)] \, dt = \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \mu_X \, dt = \mu_X$$ This shows the estimator is **unbiased** — a good start. **Step 3:** Compute the variance of the estimator: $$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_X(T)] = E\left[ (\hat{\mu}_X(T) - \mu_X)^2 \right] = E[\hat{\mu}_X^2(T)] - \mu_X^2$$ **Step 4:** Expand $\hat{\mu}_X^2(T)$: $$\hat{\mu}_X^2(T) = \frac{1}{4T^2} \int_{-T}^{T} \int_{-T}^{T} X(t_1) X(t_2) \, dt_1 \, dt_2$$ **Step 5:** Take expectation inside the integrals (Fubini): $$E[\hat{\mu}_X^2(T)] = \frac{1}{4T^2} \int_{-T}^{T} \int_{-T}^{T} E[X(t_1) X(t_2)] \, dt_1 \, dt_2 = \frac{1}{4T^2} \int_{-T}^{T} \int_{-T}^{T} R_{XX}(t_1 - t_2) \, dt_1 \, dt_2$$ **Step 6:** Apply the change of variable $\tau = t_1 - t_2$ and integrate over the square region: $$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_X(T)] = \frac{1}{4T^2} \int_{-2T}^{2T} (2T - \vert\tau\vert) \left[ R_{XX}(\tau) - \mu_X^2 \right] d\tau$$ **Step 7:** As $T \to \infty$, define the **triangular weighting factor** $\Lambda(\tau/2T) = \left(1 - \frac{\vert\tau\vert}{2T}\right)$ for $\vert\tau\vert \leq 2T$ and $0$ otherwise: $$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_X(T)] = \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} \left(1 - \frac{\vert\tau\vert}{2T}\right) C_{XX}(\tau) \, d\tau$$ where $C_{XX}(\tau) = R_{XX}(\tau) - \mu_X^2$ is the autocovariance. **Step 8:** For **mean ergodicity**, we need: $$\lim_{T \to \infty} \text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_X(T)] = 0$$ A sufficient condition is: $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert \, d\tau < \infty \quad \text{(absolutely integrable autocovariance)} \qquad \blacksquare$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** If the autocovariance decays to zero "fast enough" (faster than $1/\vert\tau\vert$), then averaging over a long observation window washes out all fluctuations, and the time-average converges to the ensemble mean. This is the mathematical heart of why ergodic processes allow **one-shot** measurements. ## 3.5 Worked Example: Proving Mean-Ergodicity for a Given $R_{XX}(\tau)$ Suppose a WSS process has the autocorrelation: $$R_{XX}(\tau) = 9 \, e^{-3\vert\tau\vert} + 4$$ **Step 1:** Identify the mean: as $\vert\tau\vert \to \infty$, $R_{XX}(\infty) = 4 = \mu_X^2 \Rightarrow \mu_X = 2$ (assuming positive). **Step 2:** Write the autocovariance: $$C_{XX}(\tau) = R_{XX}(\tau) - \mu_X^2 = 9 e^{-3\vert\tau\vert}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the time-average variance: $$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_X(T)] = \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} \left(1 - \frac{\vert\tau\vert}{2T}\right) \cdot 9 e^{-3\vert\tau\vert} \, d\tau$$ **Step 4:** Use the even symmetry to simplify: $$= \frac{9}{2T} \cdot 2 \int_{0}^{2T} \left(1 - \frac{\tau}{2T}\right) e^{-3\tau} \, d\tau = \frac{9}{T} \int_{0}^{2T} \left(1 - \frac{\tau}{2T}\right) e^{-3\tau} \, d\tau$$ **Step 5:** Bound the integrand by $e^{-3\tau}$ and use $\int_0^{\infty} e^{-3\tau} d\tau = 1/3$: $$\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_X(T)] \leq \frac{9}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{3}{T}$$ **Step 6:** Take the limit as $T \to \infty$: $$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{3}{T} = 0$$ **Conclusion:** The process **is mean-ergodic** because the variance of the time-average estimator vanishes as the observation window grows. $\blacksquare$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Map: From Stationarity to Ergodicity The following flowchart clarifies the **logical hierarchy** between stationarity, WSS, and ergodicity — a relationship often confused by KTU students. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Random Process X(t)"]:::root --> B["Is the process stationary?"] B -- "Strict-Sense Stationary SSS
All finite distributions invariant" --> C["Is it WSS?"] B -- "No" --> Z1["Cannot apply
ergodicity theorems"]:::reject C -- "Yes
Mean and ACF time-invariant" --> D["Does Covariance decay
to zero fast enough?"] D -- "Yes
int of |C_XX tau| d tau is finite" --> E["Mean-Ergodic Process"]:::pass D -- "No" --> F["WSS but NOT Ergodic"]:::warn E --> G["Time average = Ensemble average"]:::pass F --> H["Need multiple realizations
for statistics"]:::warn classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a2540,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef pass fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef warn fill:#c62828,stroke:#7f0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef reject fill:#616161,stroke:#212121,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Ergodicity Test for Autocorrelation The following block diagram outlines the **operational test** an engineer performs to verify whether a recorded signal realization is correlation-ergodic. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Recorded Signal
x of t"]:::input --> B["Apply Time Shift
tau seconds"] B --> C["Multiply
x of t and x of t+tau"] C --> D["Integrate over
minus T to T"] D --> E["Divide by
2T to get average"] E --> F["Take Limit
T tends to infinity"] F --> G{"Compare with
E of X t X t+tau"}:::decision G -- "Equal" --> H["Correlation-Ergodic
Process Verified"]:::pass G -- "Not Equal" --> I["Process is WSS
but not ergodic"]:::warn classDef input fill:#0277bd,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef decision fill:#f9a825,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef pass fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef warn fill:#c62828,stroke:#7f0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Block: Mean-Ergodic vs. Correlation-Ergodic This block-level architecture diagram compares the two principal forms of ergodicity covered in KTU Module 4. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph ME["Mean-Ergodic Test"] ME1["Estimate mean from time average"] --> ME2["Compute estimator variance"] ME2 --> ME3["Limit T infinity must be zero"] end subgraph CE["Correlation-Ergodic Test"] CE1["Form product X t and X t+tau"] --> CE2["Time-average the product"] CE2 --> CE3["Must equal E of X t X t+tau"] end ME --> Q["Sufficient Condition:
Covariance absolutely integrable"] CE --> Q Q --> R["All-Order Ergodicity
If every moment is ergodic"]:::final classDef final fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#38006b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The progression `Stationary → WSS → Ergodic` is a **strict logical hierarchy** — every ergodic process is WSS, but not every WSS process is ergodic. This subtlety appears in nearly every KTU Module-4 question paper. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the autocorrelation function of a wide-sense stationary random process. List any four of its properties.** **Model Answer:** For a WSS process $\{X(t)\}$, the **autocorrelation function** is defined as the expected value of the product of the process at two instants separated by a time difference $\tau$: $$R_{XX}(\tau) = E[X(t) \cdot X(t + \tau)]$$ **Four properties:** 1. **Even symmetry:** $R_{XX}(\tau) = R_{XX}(-\tau)$ 2. **Maximum at the origin:** $R_{XX}(0) \geq \vert R_{XX}(\tau) \vert$ for all $\tau$ 3. **Mean-square value at zero lag:** $R_{XX}(0) = E[X^2(t)]$ (total average power) 4. **Non-negative definiteness:** $\sum_i \sum_j a_i a_j R_{XX}(t_i - t_j) \geq 0$ for any real $a_i$ and time instants $t_i$. --- ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is meant by an ergodic process? State the condition for a WSS process to be mean-ergodic.** **Model Answer:** A stationary random process is called **ergodic** if its time averages (computed over a single sample function of infinite duration) equal the corresponding ensemble averages (computed over all possible sample functions at a fixed instant). For a WSS process to be **mean-ergodic**, the variance of the time-average estimator must tend to zero as the observation window grows: $$\lim_{T \to \infty} \text{Var}\left[ \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} X(t) \, dt \right] = 0$$ A **sufficient condition** is that the autocovariance function be absolutely integrable: $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert \, d\tau < \infty$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Students must answer EITHER the full Question A OR the full Question B. ### ✅ Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** For a WSS random process, the autocorrelation function is given by: $$R_{XX}(\tau) = 25 + 4 \, e^{-2\vert\tau\vert}$$ Determine: 1. The mean of the process $E[X(t)]$. 2. The variance $\sigma_X^2$. 3. The RMS value of the process. **(b) [7 Marks]** A WSS process $X(t)$ has an autocorrelation function $R_{XX}(\tau) = A + B \, e^{-\alpha \vert\tau\vert}$ where $A, B, \alpha > 0$. 1. Find the power spectral density $S_{XX}(\omega)$ using the Wiener–Khinchin theorem. 2. State and justify whether the process is mean-ergodic. --- #### Model Solution to Question A(a) **Step 1 — Identify the mean.** For a WSS process, as $\vert\tau\vert \to \infty$, the autocorrelation approaches the square of the mean: $$\mu_X^2 = \lim_{\vert\tau\vert \to \infty} R_{XX}(\tau) = 25 + 4 \cdot 0 = 25$$ $$\therefore \mu_X = 5 \quad \text{(assuming positive mean)}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the variance.** The variance equals the autocovariance at zero lag: $$\sigma_X^2 = R_{XX}(0) - \mu_X^2 = (25 + 4 \cdot 1) - 25 = 4$$ **Step 3 — Compute the RMS value:** $$X_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{R_{XX}(0)} = \sqrt{25 + 4} = \sqrt{29} \approx 5.385$$ #### Model Solution to Question A(b) **Step 1 — Apply the Wiener–Khinchin theorem.** The Fourier transform of $R_{XX}(\tau) = A + B e^{-\alpha \vert\tau\vert}$ is: $$S_{XX}(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left(A + B e^{-\alpha\vert\tau\vert}\right) e^{-j\omega\tau} d\tau$$ **Step 2 — Split into two integrals:** $$S_{XX}(\omega) = A \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-j\omega\tau} d\tau + B \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-\alpha\vert\tau\vert} e^{-j\omega\tau} d\tau$$ The first integral equals $2\pi \delta(\omega)$ (distribution). The second is the standard transform pair: $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-\alpha\vert\tau\vert} e^{-j\omega\tau} d\tau = \frac{2\alpha}{\alpha^2 + \omega^2}$$ **Step 3 — Combine the results:** $$\boxed{S_{XX}(\omega) = 2\pi A \, \delta(\omega) + \frac{2\alpha B}{\alpha^2 + \omega^2}}$$ **Step 4 — Test for mean ergodicity.** The autocovariance is: $$C_{XX}(\tau) = R_{XX}(\tau) - \mu_X^2 = B e^{-\alpha\vert\tau\vert}$$ Check absolute integrability: $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert \, d\tau = B \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-\alpha\vert\tau\vert} d\tau = B \cdot \frac{2}{\alpha} = \frac{2B}{\alpha} < \infty$$ Since the autocovariance is absolutely integrable (and $B, \alpha > 0$), **the process is mean-ergodic.** $\blacksquare$ --- ### ✅ Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice) **(a) [7 Marks]** Define the cross-correlation function $R_{XY}(\tau)$ for two jointly WSS processes. Prove that $R_{XY}(\tau) = R_{YX}(-\tau)$ and that $\vert R_{XY}(\tau) \vert \leq \sqrt{R_{XX}(0) \cdot R_{YY}(0)}$. **(b) [7 Marks]** A stationary process has the autocorrelation: $$R_{XX}(\tau) = 9 \, e^{-2\tau^2}$$ 1. Find the mean and variance of the process. 2. Determine whether the process is mean-ergodic. Justify using the Papoulis condition. --- #### Model Solution to Question B(a) **Definition:** For two jointly WSS processes, the **cross-correlation function** is: $$R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(t) \cdot Y(t + \tau)]$$ **Proof of $R_{XY}(\tau) = R_{YX}(-\tau)$:** Starting from the definition of $R_{XY}(\tau)$: $$R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(t) Y(t+\tau)]$$ Make the substitution $u = t + \tau$, so $t = u - \tau$: $$R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(u - \tau) \cdot Y(u)]$$ By commutativity of scalar multiplication: $$R_{XY}(\tau) = E[Y(u) \cdot X(u - \tau)]$$ The right side is the definition of $R_{YX}$ evaluated at lag $-\tau$: $$R_{XY}(\tau) = R_{YX}(-\tau) \qquad \blacksquare$$ **Proof of the magnitude bound:** Apply the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality to the random variables $X(t)$ and $Y(t+\tau)$: $$\vert E[X(t) \cdot Y(t+\tau)] \vert^2 \leq E[X^2(t)] \cdot E[Y^2(t+\tau)]$$ Recognizing the definitions: $$\vert R_{XY}(\tau) \vert^2 \leq R_{XX}(0) \cdot R_{YY}(0)$$ Taking the non-negative square root: $$\vert R_{XY}(\tau) \vert \leq \sqrt{R_{XX}(0) \cdot R_{YY}(0)} \qquad \blacksquare$$ #### Model Solution to Question B(b) **Step 1 — Find the mean.** As $\vert\tau\vert \to \infty$, $e^{-2\tau^2} \to 0$: $$\mu_X^2 = \lim_{\vert\tau\vert \to \infty} R_{XX}(\tau) = 0 \implies \mu_X = 0$$ **Step 2 — Find the variance.** The autocovariance equals the autocorrelation (since $\mu_X = 0$), so: $$\sigma_X^2 = C_{XX}(0) = R_{XX}(0) = 9 \cdot e^{0} = 9$$ **Step 3 — Test for mean ergodicity using Papoulis condition.** Compute: $$I(T) = \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} \left(1 - \frac{\vert\tau\vert}{2T}\right) C_{XX}(\tau) \, d\tau = \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} \left(1 - \frac{\vert\tau\vert}{2T}\right) \cdot 9 e^{-2\tau^2} d\tau$$ **Step 4 — Apply the absolute bound.** Since $0 \leq (1 - \frac{\vert\tau\vert}{2T}) \leq 1$: $$0 \leq I(T) \leq \frac{9}{2T} \int_{-2T}^{2T} e^{-2\tau^2} d\tau \leq \frac{9}{2T} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-2\tau^2} d\tau = \frac{9}{2T} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}}$$ **Step 5 — Take the limit:** $$\lim_{T \to \infty} I(T) = 0$$ Since the autocovariance is absolutely integrable (Gaussian envelope decays faster than $1/\vert\tau\vert$), **the process is mean-ergodic.** $\blacksquare$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - **Mistake 1:** Confusing $E[X(t)]$ with $E[X^2(t)]$ — students often write $\mu_X^2$ when the question asks for the mean. Always clearly distinguish between mean, mean-square, and variance. > - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to include the delta function $2\pi A \delta(\omega)$ in the PSD when the autocorrelation has a non-zero constant term. Examiners award full credit only if the impulse at $\omega = 0$ is present. > - **Mistake 3:** In ergodicity problems, students often check the *autocorrelation* decay but should check the *autocovariance* $C_{XX}(\tau) = R_{XX}(\tau) - \mu_X^2$. If $\mu_X \neq 0$, the constant offset matters. > - **Mistake 4:** Mixing up the time-average integral limits: always use $\int_{-T}^{T}$, not $\int_0^{T}$, unless the problem specifies a one-sided observation. > - **Mistake 5:** In cross-correlation questions, students frequently forget the asymmetry $R_{XY}(\tau) = R_{YX}(-\tau)$ when plotting or evaluating. Marks are lost for skipping this step. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Correlation & Ergodic Process** - **Autocorrelation (WSS):** $R_{XX}(\tau) = E[X(t) X(t+\tau)]$ — depends only on the lag $\tau$, not on absolute time. - **Cross-correlation (jointly WSS):** $R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(t) Y(t+\tau)]$ — measures statistical coupling between two signals. - **Five must-know properties of $R_{XX}(\tau)$:** even, maximum at origin, $R_{XX}(0) = E[X^2]$, non-negative definite, $R_{XX}(\infty) = \mu_X^2$. - **Variance identity:** $\sigma_X^2 = R_{XX}(0) - R_{XX}(\infty)$. - **Cross-correlation bounds:** $\vert R_{XY}(\tau) \vert \leq \sqrt{R_{XX}(0) R_{YY}(0)}$ and $R_{XY}(\tau) = R_{YX}(-\tau)$. - **Wiener–Khinchin theorem:** $S_{XX}(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{XX}(\tau)\}$ — links time-domain correlation to frequency-domain power. - **Ergodicity definition:** time average = ensemble average (for *all* statistical parameters). - **Hierarchy:** Strict-stationary $\Rightarrow$ WSS $\Rightarrow$ Ergodic (each implication is *one-way*). - **Mean-ergodic sufficient condition:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert C_{XX}(\tau) \vert d\tau < \infty$ (absolutely integrable autocovariance). - **Correlation-ergodic sufficient condition:** involves the *fourth-order* moment function; commonly requires the autocorrelation itself to be absolutely integrable and the process to have zero mean (or known constant mean). - **Engineering insight:** If a process is ergodic, **one long observation** suffices to estimate mean, variance, and PSD — this is the operational cornerstone of modern spectrum analyzers and noise measurement equipment. - **Exam mnemonic for autocorrelation properties:** "**E**ven, **M**aximum at zero, **N**on-negative definite, **M**ean-square at origin" → **EMNM**. --- > [!NOTE] > **End of Module 4 Notes — Correlation & Ergodic Process.** > These notes are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus, **NEP 2020** outcomes, and the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** cognitive levels (Apply, Analyze, Evaluate). Practice both Part-A and Part-B questions above, focusing on the **derivation chain** rather than memorizing final formulas — KTU valuation rewards logical flow.

topics.title: Distribution Ergodic Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBMAT401 - MATHEMATICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE – 4
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Distribution Ergodic Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A stochastic process $\{X_n : n \geq 0\}$ taking values in a finite (or countable) state space $S$ is called a **Distribution-Ergodic Process** (also termed *distributionally ergodic* or *ergodic in the sense of distribution*) if the marginal distribution of $X_n$ converges to a unique limit distribution as $n \to \infty$, **independent of the initial state**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Mark this verbatim):**  
> A Markov chain $\{X_n\}$ with state space $S$ and one-step transition probability matrix $P = [p_{ij}]$ is said to be *distribution-ergodic* if and only if for every pair of states $i, j \in S$,
> $$\lim_{n \to \infty} p_{ij}^{(n)} = \pi_j$$
> where $\pi = (\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots)$ is the **unique stationary distribution** satisfying $\pi P = \pi$ and $\sum_j \pi_j = 1$.

In matrix form, the limit exists and is given by:
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P^n = \begin{pmatrix} \pi_1 & \pi_2 & \cdots & \pi_N \\ \pi_1 & \pi_2 & \cdots & \pi_N \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \pi_1 & \pi_2 & \cdots & \pi_N \end{pmatrix}$$

The crucial feature is that **all rows of $P^n$ become identical** in the limit, indicating the process "forgets" its starting state.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (The "Mixing Paint" Intuition)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy — The Color Mixer:** Imagine you have a barrel containing two unmixed paints — **Red** and **Blue**. The *initial state* is whatever ratio you start with (could be 90% Red, could be 10% Red). Now, you stir the barrel vigorously using a *fixed mixing protocol* (this is your transition matrix $P$).  

> After 1 stir, the color slightly homogenizes. After 10 stirs, it looks much more uniform. After 1000 stirs, **regardless of the initial ratio**, the barrel's color converges to a single, well-defined shade determined solely by the *mixing protocol* — not by where you started.  

> This "forgetting" of the initial condition is precisely **distribution ergodicity**. The terminal shade is the **stationary distribution $\pi$**.

**Engineering Interpretation:** In electrical and communication systems, a distribution-ergodic Markov channel "washes out" the source statistics over time, leaving only the channel's intrinsic long-run behavior. This is the foundation of Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), digital signal processing, and queuing theory.

## 1.3 Key Properties at a Glance

- The process possesses a **unique stationary distribution** $\pi$.
- For **any** initial distribution $\alpha$, we have $\alpha P^n \to \pi$ as $n \to \infty$.
- The chain is **irreducible** (all states communicate).
- The chain is **aperiodic** (no cycling).
- The chain is **positive recurrent** (expected return time is finite).
- Equivalent terminology in KTU textbooks: **"Ergodic Markov Chain"** or **"Regular Markov Chain"**.

> [!TIP]  
> **Quick Memory Aid:** *Distribution-Ergodic = "Destiny is fixed; origin is forgotten."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1: Establishing the Foundation — Transition Matrix Power
For a finite Markov chain with transition matrix $P$ and initial distribution $\alpha$, the distribution after $n$ steps is given by:
$$\alpha^{(n)} = \alpha P^n$$

**Why this matters:** This is the *ensemble* (probabilistic) view. We are tracking the *probability mass* spread across states, not individual trajectories.

### Step 2: The Stationary Condition
A row vector $\pi$ is a **stationary distribution** if:
$$\pi P = \pi \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{j} \pi_j = 1$$

**Why it matters:** If $\alpha = \pi$, then $\alpha P^n = \pi$ for *all* $n$. The distribution is "frozen" — the system is in statistical equilibrium.

### Step 3: The Ergodicity Condition (The "Why" of Regularity)
A stochastic matrix $P$ is called **regular** (or **primitive**) if there exists some positive integer $k$ such that:
$$P^k > 0 \quad (\text{element-wise, all entries strictly positive})$$

**Why this is the engine of ergodicity:** After $k$ steps, *every* state can reach *every* other state with *positive* probability. This "positive mixing" destroys the memory of the initial state.

### Step 4: The Perron-Frobenius Theorem (The Mathematical Heart)
For a regular stochastic matrix $P$:
- The **spectral radius** is $\rho(P) = 1$.
- The eigenvalue $1$ has algebraic and geometric multiplicity **both equal to 1** (simple eigenvalue).
- All other eigenvalues $\lambda_2, \lambda_3, \ldots, \lambda_N$ satisfy $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$.

**Why this matters:** The matrix $P^n$ can be diagonalized as $P^n = Q \Lambda^n Q^{-1}$, where $\Lambda = \text{diag}(1, \lambda_2^n, \ldots, \lambda_N^n)$. Since $\lambda_i^n \to 0$ for $i \geq 2$, the limit $P^\infty$ is well-defined and depends only on the eigenvector associated with $\lambda = 1$, which is precisely $\pi$.

### Step 5: The Convergence Theorem (Central Limit Theorem for Chains)
By Perron-Frobenius decomposition:
$$P^n = \mathbf{1} \pi + Q^{-1} \text{diag}(\lambda_2^n, \ldots, \lambda_N^n) Q$$

As $n \to \infty$, the second term vanishes, yielding:
$$\boxed{\lim_{n \to \infty} P^n = \mathbf{1} \pi}$$

where $\mathbf{1} = (1, 1, \ldots, 1)^T$. This is the **limiting matrix** — every row equals $\pi$.

### Step 6: The Ergodic Theorem for Time Averages
Beyond distributional convergence, distribution-ergodicity implies a remarkable path-wise property (Birkhoff's Ergodic Theorem in the discrete case):
$$\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \mathbf{1}_{\{X_n = j\}} = \pi_j \quad \text{(almost surely)}$$

**Why this matters:** A single long sample path produces the *same* statistics as the entire ensemble. This is the practical heart of MCMC simulation.

## 2.2 Difference: Mean-Ergodic vs. Distribution-Ergodic

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common KTU Confusion:** Students often equate *mean-ergodic* and *distribution-ergodic*. They are **NOT** the same.

| Feature | Mean-Ergodic Process | Distribution-Ergodic Process |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} X_n \to E[X]$ (in some sense) | $X_n \xrightarrow{d} X_\infty$ with limit distribution $\pi$ |
| **Convergence Type** | Convergence of the *average* | Convergence of the *marginal distribution* |
| **Condition** | $\sum_{k} \vert R(k) \vert < \infty$ (autocorrelation summable) | Chain is regular (primitive) |
| **Implication** | Time average = Ensemble mean | All rows of $P^n$ converge to $\pi$ |
| **Strength** | Implies distribution-ergodicity (for stationary processes) | Does **NOT** imply mean-ergodicity in general |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Theorem | Description | Use in Exam |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\alpha^{(n)} = \alpha P^n$ | Distribution after $n$ steps | Standard $n$-step probability |
| 2 | $\pi P = \pi$ | Stationary distribution equation | Solving for $\pi$ |
| 3 | $\sum_j \pi_j = 1$ | Normalization constraint | Always used with (2) |
| 4 | $P^k > 0$ for some $k \geq 1$ | Regularity condition | Proving ergodicity |
| 5 | $\lim_{n \to \infty} P^n = \mathbf{1}\pi$ | Limiting matrix theorem | Distribution convergence |
| 6 | $\lim_{n \to \infty} p_{ij}^{(n)} = \pi_j$ | Element-wise convergence | Long-run probability |
| 7 | $\pi_j = \frac{1}{\mu_{jj}}$ | Stationary prob. via mean recurrence time | Alternative computation |
| 8 | $\text{Var}(\hat{\pi}_j) \approx \frac{\pi_j(1-\pi_j)}{N}$ | Variance of MCMC estimator | Statistical sampling |
| 9 | $E[T_{jj}] = \frac{1}{\pi_j}$ | Mean recurrence time | Spectral analysis |
| 10 | $\pi = \frac{1}{Z}\mathbf{v}$ where $P^T \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}$ | Eigenvector method for $\pi$ | Numerical computation |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Note on Notation:** In all KTU solutions, use $\pi_j$ (not $\hat{\pi}$) for the theoretical stationary probability, and reserve $\hat{\pi}$ for empirical/sample estimates.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **MCMC Algorithms (Metropolis-Hastings, Gibbs Sampling):** Distribution-ergodicity guarantees the Markov chain converges to the target posterior distribution, enabling Bayesian inference in machine learning.
- **Digital Communication Channels:** A distribution-ergodic channel model ensures long-term error rates stabilize, allowing reliable capacity calculations (Shannon's theorem).
- **Power System Reliability:** Markov models of generator up/down states use ergodic limits to compute long-run availability factors.
- **Queueing Theory (M/M/1, M/M/c):** Steady-state probabilities are exactly the stationary distributions of distribution-ergodic birth-death chains.
- **Speech Recognition (Hidden Markov Models):** HMM training relies on the forward-backward algorithm, which assumes the underlying Markov chain is ergodic.
- **Network Packet Routing:** PageRank (Google's algorithm) is fundamentally the stationary distribution of a distribution-ergodic random walk on the web graph.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Proof: Convergence to Stationary Distribution

**Theorem:** If $P$ is a regular stochastic matrix (i.e., $P^k > 0$ for some $k \geq 1$), then $P^n \to \mathbf{1}\pi$ as $n \to \infty$, where $\pi$ is the unique stationary distribution.

**Proof Outline (Perron-Frobenius Decomposition):**

Let $N$ be the size of the state space. Write $P$ in its spectral form:
$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lambda_i \mathbf{u}_i \mathbf{v}_i^T$$

where $\lambda_i$ are eigenvalues, $\mathbf{u}_i$ are right eigenvectors, and $\mathbf{v}_i$ are left eigenvectors. Then:
$$P^n = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lambda_i^n \mathbf{u}_i \mathbf{v}_i^T$$

**Step 1: Order the eigenvalues.** WLOG, let $\lambda_1 = 1$ and $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$ for $i = 2, \ldots, N$.

This follows because the spectral radius of a stochastic matrix is 1, and regularity (Perron-Frobenius theorem for primitive matrices) guarantees that 1 is a *simple* eigenvalue strictly dominating all others.

**Step 2: Identify the eigenvectors.**
- Right eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 1$: $P \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{1}$, so $\mathbf{u}_1 = \mathbf{1} = (1, 1, \ldots, 1)^T$.
- Left eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 1$: $\pi P = \pi$, so $\mathbf{v}_1 = \pi$.

**Step 3: Factor the dominant term.**
$$P^n = 1^n \mathbf{1} \pi^T + \sum_{i=2}^{N} \lambda_i^n \mathbf{u}_i \mathbf{v}_i^T = \mathbf{1}\pi + \sum_{i=2}^{N} \lambda_i^n \mathbf{u}_i \mathbf{v}_i^T$$

> Note: We use row-vector convention $\pi P = \pi$, so $\pi$ is a *row* vector. Hence $\pi^T$ appears in the dyadic product.

**Step 4: Take the limit.**
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P^n = \mathbf{1}\pi + \lim_{n \to \infty} \sum_{i=2}^{N} \lambda_i^n \mathbf{u}_i \mathbf{v}_i^T$$

Since $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$ for $i \geq 2$, we have $\lambda_i^n \to 0$. Therefore:
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P^n = \mathbf{1}\pi = \begin{pmatrix} \pi_1 & \pi_2 & \cdots & \pi_N \\ \pi_1 & \pi_2 & \cdots & \pi_N \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \pi_1 & \pi_2 & \cdots & \pi_N \end{pmatrix}$$

This completes the proof. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Full Step-by-Step

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Model Question Style:** Complete all 4 sub-parts to secure full marks.

**Problem:** Consider a Markov chain with state space $S = \{0, 1, 2\}$ and transition matrix:
$$P = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1/2 & 1/2 \\ 1/3 & 1/3 & 1/3 \\ 1/2 & 1/4 & 1/4 \end{pmatrix}$$

**(a) Show that $P$ is regular.**

Compute $P^2$. We multiply row $i$ of $P$ by column $j$ of $P$:

$$P^2_{11} = (0)(0) + \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) = 0 + \tfrac{1}{6} + \tfrac{1}{4} = \tfrac{5}{12}$$

$$P^2_{12} = (0)\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right) = 0 + \tfrac{1}{6} + \tfrac{1}{8} = \tfrac{7}{24}$$

$$P^2_{13} = (0)\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right) = 0 + \tfrac{1}{6} + \tfrac{1}{8} = \tfrac{7}{24}$$

$$P^2_{21} = \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)(0) + \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) = 0 + \tfrac{1}{9} + \tfrac{1}{6} = \tfrac{5}{18}$$

$$P^2_{22} = \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right) = \tfrac{1}{6} + \tfrac{1}{9} + \tfrac{1}{12} = \tfrac{13}{36}$$

$$P^2_{23} = \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right) = \tfrac{1}{6} + \tfrac{1}{9} + \tfrac{1}{12} = \tfrac{13}{36}$$

$$P^2_{31} = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)(0) + \left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) = 0 + \tfrac{1}{12} + \tfrac{1}{8} = \tfrac{5}{24}$$

$$P^2_{32} = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right) = \tfrac{1}{4} + \tfrac{1}{12} + \tfrac{1}{16} = \tfrac{31}{48}$$

$$P^2_{33} = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) + \left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right) = \tfrac{1}{4} + \tfrac{1}{12} + \tfrac{1}{16} = \tfrac{31}{48}$$

Thus:
$$P^2 = \begin{pmatrix} 5/12 & 7/24 & 7/24 \\ 5/18 & 13/36 & 13/36 \\ 5/24 & 31/48 & 31/48 \end{pmatrix}$$

Every entry of $P^2$ is strictly positive. **Therefore $P$ is regular with $k=2$.** $\checkmark$

**(b) Find the stationary distribution $\pi$.**

Solve $\pi P = \pi$ with $\pi_0 + \pi_1 + \pi_2 = 1$.

Writing out the system:
$$\pi_0 = \tfrac{1}{3}\pi_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\pi_2$$
$$\pi_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi_0 + \tfrac{1}{3}\pi_1 + \tfrac{1}{4}\pi_2$$
$$\pi_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi_0 + \tfrac{1}{3}\pi_1 + \tfrac{1}{4}\pi_2$$

From the third equation:
$$\tfrac{1}{2}\pi_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi_0 + \tfrac{1}{3}\pi_1 \implies \pi_2 = \pi_0 + \tfrac{2}{3}\pi_1$$

Substitute into the first equation:
$$\pi_0 = \tfrac{1}{3}\pi_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\pi_0 + \tfrac{2}{3}\pi_1\right) = \tfrac{1}{3}\pi_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\pi_0 + \tfrac{1}{3}\pi_1$$

$$\pi_0 - \tfrac{1}{2}\pi_0 = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi_1 \implies \tfrac{1}{2}\pi_0 = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi_1 \implies \pi_0 = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi_1$$

Substitute back:
$$\pi_2 = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi_1 + \tfrac{2}{3}\pi_1 = 2\pi_1$$

Apply normalization:
$$\tfrac{4}{3}\pi_1 + \pi_1 + 2\pi_1 = 1 \implies \tfrac{4 + 3 + 6}{3}\pi_1 = 1 \implies \tfrac{13}{3}\pi_1 = 1$$

$$\pi_1 = \tfrac{3}{13}, \quad \pi_0 = \tfrac{4}{13}, \quad \pi_2 = \tfrac{6}{13}$$

$$\boxed{\pi = \left(\tfrac{4}{13},\; \tfrac{3}{13},\; \tfrac{6}{13}\right)}$$

**Verification:** $\pi P = \left(\tfrac{4}{13},\; \tfrac{3}{13},\; \tfrac{6}{13}\right) P$. Compute the first component:
$$\tfrac{4}{13}(0) + \tfrac{3}{13}\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) + \tfrac{6}{13}\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) = 0 + \tfrac{1}{13} + \tfrac{3}{13} = \tfrac{4}{13} = \pi_0 \;\checkmark$$

Second component:
$$\tfrac{4}{13}\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \tfrac{3}{13}\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) + \tfrac{6}{13}\left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right) = \tfrac{2}{13} + \tfrac{1}{13} + \tfrac{3}{26} = \tfrac{4}{26} + \tfrac{2}{26} + \tfrac{3}{26} = \tfrac{9}{26} = \tfrac{3}{13} \;\checkmark$$

Third component:
$$\tfrac{4}{13}\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \tfrac{3}{13}\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) + \tfrac{6}{13}\left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right) = \tfrac{2}{13} + \tfrac{1}{13} + \tfrac{3}{26} = \tfrac{9}{26} = \tfrac{6}{13} \;\checkmark$$

**(c) Compute $\lim_{n \to \infty} P^n$.**

By the ergodic theorem:
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P^n = \begin{pmatrix} 4/13 & 3/13 & 6/13 \\ 4/13 & 3/13 & 6/13 \\ 4/13 & 3/13 & 6/13 \end{pmatrix}$$

**(d) Compute $\lim_{n \to \infty} P(X_n = 2 \mid X_0 = 0)$.**

This is the $(0, 2)$-entry of $P^n$ in the limit:
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P(X_n = 2 \mid X_0 = 0) = \pi_2 = \tfrac{6}{13}$$

**Geometric Rate of Convergence:** The second-largest eigenvalue $\lambda_2$ of $P$ controls the rate. One can show:
$$\vert p_{ij}^{(n)} - \pi_j \vert \leq C \cdot \vert \lambda_2 \vert^n$$

For our matrix, $\lambda_2 \approx -0.108$, so the error decays as $\approx (0.108)^n$ — extremely fast.

## 3.3 Derivation of Mean Recurrence Time Formula

> [!NOTE]  
> This derivation explains *why* $\pi_j = 1 / \mu_{jj}$.

**Setup:** Let $T_{jj}$ be the first return time to state $j$. By the **renewal reward theorem** for ergodic chains:

The long-run fraction of time spent in state $j$ equals $\pi_j$. But this fraction also equals $1 / E[T_{jj}]$ (average inter-arrival time in renewal theory). Therefore:
$$\pi_j = \frac{1}{E[T_{jj}]} = \frac{1}{\mu_{jj}}$$

Rearranging:
$$E[T_{jj}] = \frac{1}{\pi_j}$$

**KTU Use:** To find $\pi_j$ via this route, simulate the chain, count the average time between successive visits to $j$, and take the reciprocal. This is the *empirical* way to verify the theoretical $\pi_j$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram — Distributional Convergence of an Ergodic Process

The following block-level flow illustrates how a regular Markov chain "forgets" its initial state and converges to its unique stationary distribution $\pi$:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial State X sub 0 with distribution alpha] --> B[Step 1: Multiply alpha by P]
    B --> C[Distribution alpha P = alpha 1]
    C --> D[Step 2: Multiply alpha 1 by P]
    D --> E[Distribution alpha P squared = alpha 2]
    E --> F[Step 3: Continue iterating]
    F --> G[After k steps: alpha P to power k]
    G --> H{Is P regular?}
    H -- Yes --> I[All entries of P to power k strictly positive]
    I --> J[Apply Perron Frobenius Theorem]
    J --> K[Spectral radius 1 with simple eigenvalue]
    K --> L[Take limit as n to infinity]
    L --> M[Limiting distribution pi]
    M --> N[pi P equals pi and sum pi j equals 1]
    H -- No --> O[Chain is periodic or reducible]
    O --> P[No unique stationary distribution]
    P --> Q[Ergodicity fails]
    N --> R[Output: All rows of P to power n equal pi]
    R --> S[Long run behavior independent of start]
```

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram — Example 2-State Chain

For the simple 2-state chain $P = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}$ (used in numerical exercises below), the transition topology is:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[State 0] -- 0.7 self loop --> S0
    S0 -- 0.3 transition --> S1[State 1]
    S1 -- 0.4 transition --> S0
    S1 -- 0.6 self loop --> S1
    S0 -.-> S0lim[Limiting Probability pi 0 equals 4 over 7]
    S1 -.-> S1lim[Limiting Probability pi 1 equals 3 over 7]
    S0lim --- S1lim
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Engineering Application in Communication Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Symbols with distribution p sub s] --> B[Markov Channel with transition matrix P]
    B --> C[Received Symbols with distribution p sub r]
    C --> D{Channel Ergodic?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Long run error rate equals 1 minus pi 1]
    D -- No --> F[Error rate oscillates or diverges]
    E --> G[Stable channel capacity via Shannon]
    F --> H[Unreliable communication link]
    G --> I[Final Design: Reliable Data Transmission]
    H --> J[Final Design: Channel Re-engineering Needed]
```

**Interpretation:** This block diagram maps the role of distribution-ergodicity in a digital communication system. The *channel* is modeled as a Markov chain; ergodicity ensures that no matter what symbol enters the channel initially, the long-term probability of correct reception converges to a fixed value (the stationary distribution of the chain), enabling engineers to compute Shannon capacity reliably.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a distribution-ergodic process. State the necessary and sufficient condition for a finite Markov chain to be distribution-ergodic.**

**Model Answer:**

A stochastic process $\{X_n\}$ is called a **distribution-ergodic process** if the marginal distribution of $X_n$ converges to a limiting distribution $\pi$ as $n \to \infty$, regardless of the initial state. Equivalently, for all states $i, j$:
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} p_{ij}^{(n)} = \pi_j$$

**Necessary and Sufficient Condition:** A finite Markov chain is distribution-ergodic if and only if its transition matrix $P$ is **regular**, i.e., there exists a positive integer $k$ such that all entries of $P^k$ are strictly positive ($P^k > 0$ element-wise). Equivalently, the chain is irreducible, aperiodic, and positive recurrent — and possesses a unique stationary distribution $\pi$ satisfying $\pi P = \pi$. $\blacksquare$

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between mean-ergodic and distribution-ergodic processes. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Mean-Ergodic | Distribution-Ergodic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Property | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} X_n \to E[X]$ | $X_n \xrightarrow{d} X_\infty$ |
| Condition | $\sum_{k}\vert R(k) \vert < \infty$ | $P^k > 0$ for some $k$ |

**Example of Mean-Ergodic:** A stationary AR(1) process $X_n = 0.5 X_{n-1} + W_n$ with summable autocorrelation $\rho^k$.

**Example of Distribution-Ergodic:** A regular 2-state Markov chain with $P = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{pmatrix}$. The distributions $P^n$ converge row-wise to $\pi = (3/7, 4/7)$.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Format:** Each Part B question has internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** (both 14 marks). Each question is divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) State and prove the limiting distribution theorem for a regular Markov chain.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Theorem:** Let $P$ be a regular stochastic matrix. Then there exists a unique probability vector $\pi$ such that $\pi P = \pi$, and for any initial state $i$ and any state $j$:
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} p_{ij}^{(n)} = \pi_j$$

**Proof:** Since $P$ is regular, $P^k > 0$ for some $k$. Let $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_N$ be the eigenvalues of $P$, ordered so that $\lambda_1 = 1$ and $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$ for $i \geq 2$ (this follows from Perron-Frobenius applied to primitive matrices).

By spectral decomposition:
$$P^n = 1^n \mathbf{1} \pi + \sum_{i=2}^{N} \lambda_i^n \mathbf{u}_i \mathbf{v}_i^T = \mathbf{1}\pi + \sum_{i=2}^{N} \lambda_i^n \mathbf{u}_i \mathbf{v}_i^T$$

As $n \to \infty$, $\lambda_i^n \to 0$ for all $i \geq 2$. Therefore:
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P^n = \mathbf{1}\pi$$

Reading the $(i,j)$-entry:
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} p_{ij}^{(n)} = \pi_j$$

Uniqueness of $\pi$ follows from the simplicity of eigenvalue 1. $\blacksquare$

**(b) A two-state Markov chain has transition matrix $P = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}$. Show that the chain is distribution-ergodic and find the limiting distribution. Verify the result by computing $P^3$.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Check regularity.** All entries of $P$ are positive, so $P^1 > 0$. Thus $P$ is regular with $k=1$. ✓

**Step 2: Find stationary distribution.** Solve $\pi_0 + \pi_1 = 1$ and $\pi_0 = 0.7\pi_0 + 0.4\pi_1$:
$$0.3\pi_0 = 0.4\pi_1 \implies \pi_0 = \frac{4}{3}\pi_1$$

Substituting: $\frac{4}{3}\pi_1 + \pi_1 = 1 \implies \frac{7}{3}\pi_1 = 1$

$$\pi_1 = \frac{3}{7}, \quad \pi_0 = \frac{4}{7}$$

$$\boxed{\pi = \left(\frac{4}{7},\; \frac{3}{7}\right)}$$

**Step 3: Verify by computing $P^2$ and $P^3$.**

First, $P^2$:
$$P^2_{11} = (0.7)(0.7) + (0.3)(0.4) = 0.49 + 0.12 = 0.61$$
$$P^2_{12} = (0.7)(0.3) + (0.3)(0.6) = 0.21 + 0.18 = 0.39$$
$$P^2_{21} = (0.4)(0.7) + (0.6)(0.4) = 0.28 + 0.24 = 0.52$$
$$P^2_{22} = (0.4)(0.3) + (0.6)(0.6) = 0.12 + 0.36 = 0.48$$

$$P^2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.61 & 0.39 \\ 0.52 & 0.48 \end{pmatrix}$$

Now $P^3 = P^2 \cdot P$:
$$P^3_{11} = (0.61)(0.7) + (0.39)(0.4) = 0.427 + 0.156 = 0.583$$
$$P^3_{12} = (0.61)(0.3) + (0.39)(0.6) = 0.183 + 0.234 = 0.417$$
$$P^3_{21} = (0.52)(0.7) + (0.48)(0.4) = 0.364 + 0.192 = 0.556$$
$$P^3_{22} = (0.52)(0.3) + (0.48)(0.6) = 0.156 + 0.288 = 0.444$$

$$P^3 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.583 & 0.417 \\ 0.556 & 0.444 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compare with $\pi = (4/7 \approx 0.571, 3/7 \approx 0.429)$.**

Already at $n=3$, $P^3_{11} = 0.583 \approx 0.571$ and $P^3_{12} = 0.417 \approx 0.429$. As $n \to \infty$, the rows converge exactly to $\pi$. Hence the chain is distribution-ergodic. $\blacksquare$

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the relationship between ergodicity, irreducibility, and aperiodicity in finite Markov chains. Prove that a regular chain has a unique stationary distribution.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Hierarchical Relationship:**

$$\text{Regular} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Irreducible} \;+\; \text{Aperiodic} \;+\; \text{Positive Recurrent}$$

For **finite** state-space chains, positive recurrence is automatic. So regularity reduces to **irreducibility + aperiodicity**.

**Definitions:**
- *Irreducible:* For all $i, j \in S$, there exists $n$ such that $p_{ij}^{(n)} > 0$.
- *Aperiodic:* The greatest common divisor of $\{n : p_{ii}^{(n)} > 0\}$ is 1 for all $i$.

**Proof of Unique Stationary Distribution:**

Suppose $\pi$ and $\pi'$ are two stationary distributions. Then for all $n$:
$$\pi P^n = \pi \quad \text{and} \quad \pi' P^n = \pi'$$

For a regular chain, $P^n \to \mathbf{1}\pi$ as $n \to \infty$ (from the limiting theorem). Taking the limit:
$$\pi = \lim_{n \to \infty} \pi P^n = \pi \cdot \mathbf{1}\pi = \left(\sum_j \pi_j\right) \pi = \pi$$

This is a tautology. For uniqueness, suppose two different distributions $\pi \neq \pi'$ both satisfy $\pi P = \pi$. Then $(\pi - \pi')P = 0$, where $0$ is the zero row vector. But $P$ is a primitive (regular) matrix with $\rho(P) = 1$ and eigenvalue 1 having geometric multiplicity 1. So the null space of $(P^T - I)$ is one-dimensional, meaning $\pi - \pi' = c \mathbf{v}$ for some scalar $c$, where $\mathbf{v}$ is the unique (up to scalar) left eigenvector. Since $\pi$ and $\pi'$ are both probability vectors (sum to 1), we must have $c = 0$, hence $\pi = \pi'$. $\blacksquare$

**(b) Consider the Markov chain with transition matrix:**
$$P = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1/2 & 0 & 1/2 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Determine whether the chain is distribution-ergodic. If yes, find the limiting distribution; if not, explain why.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute $P^2$.**
$$P^2_{11} = (0)(0) + (1)(1/2) + (0)(0) = 1/2$$
$$P^2_{22} = (1/2)(1) + (0)(0) + (1/2)(1) = 1$$
$$P^2_{33} = (0)(0) + (1)(1/2) + (0)(0) = 1/2$$
$$P^2_{12} = (0)(1) + (1)(0) + (0)(1) = 0$$
$$P^2_{13} = (0)(0) + (1)(1/2) + (0)(0) = 1/2$$

$$P^2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1/2 & 0 & 1/2 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1/2 & 0 & 1/2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute $P^3 = P^2 \cdot P$.**
$$P^3_{11} = (1/2)(0) + (0)(1/2) + (1/2)(0) = 0$$
$$P^3_{22} = (0)(1) + (1)(0) + (0)(1) = 0$$
$$P^3_{12} = (1/2)(1) + (0)(0) + (1/2)(1) = 1$$
$$P^3_{13} = (1/2)(0) + (0)(1/2) + (1/2)(0) = 0$$

$$P^3 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1/2 & 0 & 1/2 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} = P$$

So $P^3 = P$, and the sequence is **periodic with period 3**.

**Step 3: Conclusion.**

$P^3 = P$ means $P^n$ cycles through $\{P, P^2, P^3 = P\}$. The sequence $P^n$ **does not converge**. There is no limit matrix.

In fact, the chain is **periodic** (states 0 and 3 have period 2, state 1 has period 1 — actually the chain is bipartite: $\text{even} \to \text{odd} \to \text{even}$). The chain is **not aperiodic**, hence **not regular**, hence **not distribution-ergodic**.

The stationary distribution can still be computed: solving $\pi P = \pi$ yields $\pi = (1/4, 1/2, 1/4)$. But $P^n$ does not converge to this distribution — it oscillates.

**Final Answer:** The chain is **NOT distribution-ergodic** due to periodicity. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Valuation Pattern — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Regular with Stochastic:** Every stochastic matrix has rows summing to 1, but not every one is *regular*. Regularity requires $P^k > 0$ for *some* $k$ — not just $P$ being valid. **(-2 marks if missed)**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Normalization:** When solving $\pi P = \pi$, students often forget $\sum \pi_j = 1$. The system has one redundant equation; you **must** use the normalization constraint to pin down $\pi$. **(-1 mark)**
> 
> 3. **Mixing Up Periodicity and Ergodicity:** A chain can be irreducible yet NOT ergodic if it's periodic (like the $P^3 = P$ example above). Always check periodicity. **(-3 marks for claiming ergodic without verification)**
> 
> 4. **Transposing the Stationary Equation:** Some write $P\pi = \pi$ (wrong — that's for column eigenvectors). The correct form is the **row vector equation** $\pi P = \pi$. **(-2 marks)**
> 
> 5. **Skipping the Verification Step:** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for verifying $\pi P = \pi$ after computing $\pi$. Always substitute back.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]  
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Definition:** A process is distribution-ergodic iff $\lim_{n \to \infty} p_{ij}^{(n)} = \pi_j$ exists and is independent of $i$.
- [ ] **Equivalent condition:** Chain is **regular** ($P^k > 0$ for some $k \geq 1$).
- [ ] **For finite chains:** Regular ⟺ Irreducible + Aperiodic.
- [ ] **Stationary equation:** Always write as $\pi P = \pi$ (row vector), NOT $P\pi = \pi$.
- [ ] **Normalization:** Always pair $\pi P = \pi$ with $\sum_j \pi_j = 1$.
- [ ] **Limiting matrix:** All rows of $\lim_{n \to \infty} P^n$ equal $\pi$.
- [ ] **Perron-Frobenius:** Eigenvalue 1 is simple; all other eigenvalues have magnitude $< 1$.
- [ ] **Mean vs. Distribution Ergodicity:** NOT the same — they are independent properties in general.
- [ ] **Periodicity kills ergodicity:** A chain like $P^3 = P$ is periodic → no limiting distribution exists.
- [ ] **Engineering use:** MCMC convergence, channel capacity, queuing steady-state, PageRank.
- [ ] **Recurrence-time relation:** $\pi_j = 1 / E[T_{jj}]$ — a useful alternative to direct $\pi P = \pi$ solving.
- [ ] **Rate of convergence:** Governed by $\lambda_2$ (second-largest eigenvalue); error $\sim C \vert \lambda_2 \vert^n$.
- [ ] **Verification is mandatory:** Always plug $\pi$ back into $\pi P = \pi$ to confirm correctness (KTU awards marks for this).

> **End of Topic Notes — Distribution Ergodic Process**
